| State of RSS in 2026 | Mon Jul 7, 9:00 | Blog + Newsletter | Maya |
| Accessible color tokens | Wed Jul 9, 9:00 | Blog | Priya |

Pre-flight for each card:

- [x] Final copy approved in [[z=2|Editing]]
- [x] Hero image and social card exported (1200x630)
- [x] Meta title and description under limits
- [x] Canonical URL and slug set
- [ ] Scheduled in the CMS (auto-publish armed)

Once the CMS confirms the post is live, archive the card in
[[z=4|Published]].

@plane z=4 label="Published" x=8 y=0
# Published (Live)

WIP limit: none. This is the archive and the scoreboard.

Live work, with a 14-day performance check booked for each piece. We review
what shipped to feed new ideas back into [[z=0|Backlog]].

Recently live:

- **Cold-brew at scale** - shipped Jun 30. 3,100 opens, 11.2% click rate on
  the newsletter. Strong; a "hot-brew comparison" follow-up went to the
  backlog.
- **Migrating off Webpack** - shipped Jun 25. 4,800 reads, ranking page 1 for
  "webpack to vite config". Spawned a backlog idea: a Rollup deep-dive.

Retro questions for each card:

1. Did it hit its audience and channel goal?
2. What was the biggest time sink, and which stage caused it?
3. Does it suggest a sequel worth adding to [[z=0|Backlog]]?

Cycle-time note: "Cold-brew" took 6 days backlog to live; "Webpack" took 12
because of a rewrite loop between editing and drafting. Tightening the
[[z=2|Editing]] checklist is the next process fix.

title: Holloway v. Meridian Freight - Case Timeline
src:
---
3md: 1.0
axis: time
title: Holloway v. Meridian Freight - Case Timeline
case_number: CV-2024-08817
court: Superior Court of California, County of Alameda
parties: Daniel Holloway (Plaintiff) v. Meridian Freight LLC (Defendant)
claim: Negligence; wrongful termination in violation of public policy
---
A chronological record of the matter **Holloway v. Meridian Freight LLC**, from
the underlying incident through trial. Each plane is a milestone on the time
axis. Read forward (increasing `z`) for the procedural history, or jump straight
to [[z=6|the trial]].

@plane z=0 label="Incident" x=0 y=0
# March 14, 2023 - The Incident

Daniel Holloway, a dock supervisor at Meridian Freight's Oakland terminal,
reported that a loading ramp's hydraulic brace had failed inspection twice in
February but was never repaired. On the morning of March 14 the brace gave way,
dropping a 1,900 lb pallet and crushing Holloway's left foot.

**Documented at the scene:**

- OSHA Form 301 injury report filed by the site safety officer at 09:42.
- Two coworker witness statements (R. Vega, T. Okafor) taken the same day.
- Photographs of the failed brace and the February maintenance log.

Holloway was placed on medical leave the following week. The repair invoices
that should have existed for the February inspections were never produced,
a gap that becomes central later in [[z=4|discovery]].

@plane z=1 label="Termination" x=2 y=0
# May 2, 2023 - Termination

Seven weeks after the injury, while still on approved medical leave, Holloway
received a termination letter citing "restructuring of dock operations."

Notable facts that frame the wrongful-termination claim:

1. No other dock supervisor was let go in the cited "restructuring."
2. The termination came eleven days after Holloway emailed HR asking why the
   February inspection failures had not been addressed.
3. His position was re-posted, under a new title, six weeks later.

These three facts form the causal chain the plaintiff will argue establishes
retaliation in violation of public policy.

@plane z=2 label="Complaint Filed" x=4 y=0
# September 18, 2023 - Complaint Filed

Plaintiff's counsel filed the original Complaint in Alameda Superior Court,
assigned case number **CV-2024-08817**. The pleading states two causes of
action:

- **First Cause:** Negligence (failure to maintain a safe workplace).
- **Second Cause:** Wrongful termination in violation of public policy
  (Labor Code section 1102.5 whistleblower retaliation).

Personal service on Meridian Freight's registered agent was completed on
September 22, starting the 30-day clock for a responsive pleading.

@plane z=3 label="Answer and Motion" x=6 y=0
# October 20, 2023 - Answer and Demurrer

Defendant timely filed an Answer with a general denial and fourteen affirmative
defenses, alongside a **demurrer** to the second cause of action arguing the
retaliation claim was not pled with sufficient particularity.

- **Nov 30, 2023 hearing:** The court **overruled** the demurrer, finding the
  termination timeline (see [[z=1|the termination plane]]) adequately pled
  causation. Defendant was given 20 days to answer the second cause, which it
  did on December 14.

With the pleadings settled, the case moved into [[z=4|discovery]].

@plane z=4 label="Discovery" x=8 y=0
# January - July 2024 - Discovery

The longest phase of the case. Key events:

| Date | Event |
| --- | --- |
| Jan 11 | Plaintiff served Form Interrogatories and Requests for Production |
| Feb 28 | Defendant's responses omitted the February 2023 maintenance records |
| Apr 03 | Plaintiff filed a Motion to Compel further production |
| Apr 25 | Court granted the motion; ordered records produced within 15 days |
| May 09 | Defendant produced records confirming **two failed inspections** |
| Jun 17 | Deposition of Meridian's maintenance lead, J. Castellano |
| Jul 30 | Discovery cutoff |

The compelled records corroborated the witness statements gathered back at
[[z=0|the incident]] and became the spine of the plaintiff's negligence case.

@plane z=5 label="Summary Judgment" x=10 y=0
# September 5, 2024 - Motion for Summary Judgment

Defendant moved for summary judgment, or in the alternative summary
adjudication, on both causes of action.

**Ruling (October 1, 2024):**

- **Negligence:** Motion **denied.** Triable issues of fact existed as to
  whether Meridian had notice of the brace defect, given the produced
  inspection records from [[z=4|discovery]].
- **Wrongful termination:** Motion **denied.** The eleven-day gap between
  Holloway's HR email and his termination raised a triable issue of pretext.

Both claims survived and were set for trial.

@plane z=6 label="Trial" x=12 y=0
# February 10-21, 2025 - Jury Trial

A nine-day jury trial before Judge Patricia Lindqvist.

**Plaintiff's case-in-chief:** Holloway, both eyewitnesses from
[[z=0|the incident]], the maintenance lead's [[z=4|deposition]] read into the
record, and a vocational economist on lost earnings.

**Verdict (February 21, 2025):** The jury found for the plaintiff on both
causes of action.

- Past and future medical: $312,000
- Lost earnings: $448,500
- Noneconomic damages: $400,000
- **Total award: $1,160,500**

Comparative fault was assigned at 0% to the plaintiff. Defendant filed a notice
of intent to move for a new trial; post-trial motions remain pending.

title: Four-Season Crop Rotation Plan
src:
---
3md: 1.0
axis: time
title: Four-Season Crop Rotation Plan
plot: North Field, Bed A (30 ft x 4 ft raised bed)
zone: USDA 6b
goal: build soil fertility while breaking pest and disease cycles
---
This plan rotates a single raised bed through four families over one year so that
no family returns to the same soil until the cycle closes. The Z axis is time:
each plane is one season. Heavy feeders follow soil builders, light feeders follow
heavy feeders, and a legume green manure resets nitrogen before the cycle repeats.

The `x` and `y` offsets sketch the bed footprint as seen from above, so a spatial
viewer can stack the seasons in place over the same ground.

@plane z=0 x=0 y=0 label="Spring: Legumes (soil builders)"
# Spring: Peas and Fava Beans

**Family:** Fabaceae (legumes)
**Role:** nitrogen fixation, soil structure

Legumes open the rotation because they partner with rhizobia bacteria to pull
nitrogen from the air and fix it into the soil, leaving the bed richer for the
hungry crop that follows.

## Planting

- Direct sow shelling peas as soon as the soil can be worked (late March here).
- Sow fava beans 1 inch deep, 4 inches apart, in a double row.
- Inoculate seed with garden pea/bean rhizobia for a stronger nitrogen boost.

## Bed map

| Row | Crop          | Spacing |
|-----|---------------|---------|
| 1-2 | Shelling peas | 2 in    |
| 3   | Fava beans    | 4 in    |

## Hand-off

Cut spent plants at the soil line and leave the roots in place: the nitrogen
nodules break down right where the next crop needs them. That nitrogen feeds the
[[z=1|summer heavy feeders]] directly.

@plane z=1 x=0 y=0 label="Summer: Heavy feeders"
# Summer: Tomatoes and Squash

**Family:** Solanaceae and Cucurbitaceae
**Role:** heavy feeders cashing in stored nitrogen

The bed is now nitrogen-rich from [[z=0|the spring legumes]], so it can carry the
hungriest crops of the year without a heavy compost dump.

## Planting

- Transplant tomato starts after the last frost (around May 15 in zone 6b).
- Cage or stake every tomato at planting time, before the roots fill in.
- Direct sow summer squash in two hills at the sunny end of the bed.

## Feeding and care

1. Side-dress tomatoes with compost when the first fruits set.
2. Water deeply twice a week, at the base, to keep blossom-end rot away.
3. Pinch tomato suckers weekly to keep airflow high and disease low.

> Note: do NOT follow this season with more Solanaceae. Tomatoes, peppers, and
> potatoes share soilborne diseases (early blight, verticillium), which is the
> whole reason we rotate families instead of repeating a favorite.

The next season pulls these tall plants out and switches to crops that live in
the soil rather than above it: see [[z=2|the fall root crops]].

@plane z=2 x=0 y=0 label="Fall: Root crops (light feeders)"
# Fall: Carrots and Beets

**Family:** Apiaceae and Amaranthaceae
**Role:** light feeders that loosen and mine the lower soil

After the [[z=1|summer heavy feeders]] are pulled, the bed has moderate fertility
left. Root crops thrive here: too much fresh nitrogen would give forked carrots
and leafy beets instead of clean roots.

## Planting

- Sow carrots and beets in mid-August for a fall harvest.
- Carrots need a fine, stone-free top 8 inches, so rake the bed smooth first.
- Thin carrots to 2 inches and beets to 3 inches once seedlings establish.

## Why roots come third

- They scavenge phosphorus and potassium from deeper soil layers.
- Their taproots break up compaction the shallow summer crops left behind.
- Light feeding means we skip extra amendments and let the bed coast down.

## Hand-off

Harvest through the first light frosts (frost sweetens carrots and beets). Then
prepare the bed for a cover crop that will rebuild what this year drew down: see
[[z=3|the winter green manure]].

@plane z=3 x=0 y=0 label="Winter: Green manure (reset)"
# Winter: Crimson Clover Cover Crop

**Family:** Fabaceae (legume cover crop)
**Role:** living mulch and nitrogen reset over winter

A bare bed in winter erodes, leaches nutrients, and grows weeds. A legume cover
crop fixes nitrogen again, protects the soil surface, and feeds soil microbes
through the cold months.

## Planting

- Broadcast crimson clover at 0.5 oz per 100 sq ft after the fall harvest.
- Rake in lightly and water; aim for 4 to 6 weeks of growth before hard frost.
- The clover overwinters as a low mat and surges again in early spring.

## Termination

1. About 3 weeks before spring planting, cut the clover at flowering.
2. Leave the cut tops as mulch, or turn them in shallowly as green manure.
3. Wait 2 to 3 weeks for the residue to begin breaking down.

## Closing the loop

The bed is now rebuilt and ready. Because winter ends on a legume, the cycle does
NOT simply repeat: next spring, shift the rotation forward so this bed starts with
heavy feeders while a neighboring bed takes the legumes. Over four years every bed
sees every family once. To restart the sequence, jump back to
[[z=0|the spring legumes]].

title: Salsa Basic Step (On1, Cross-Body Lead Foundation)
src:
---
3md: 1.0
axis: step
title: Salsa Basic Step (On1, Cross-Body Lead Foundation)
style: LA / cross-body
count: 8 beats over 6 weighted steps
timing: quick-quick-slow
tempo: 160-180 BPM
---
The salsa basic is danced over eight beats of music, but you only take six
weighted steps: beats 4 and 8 are held (the "slow"). This document walks the
Leader's footwork through one full basic, one step (one beat) per plane.

Each plane is one beat. The `x` offset tracks where your weight sits left to
right (negative is your left side, positive is your right); the `y` offset tracks
forward and back from your home position (positive is forward, negative is back).
Jump to [[z=1|beat 1]] to begin, or skip ahead to [[z=5|the back-break on 5]].

Counting reminder: say it out loud as "one-two-three, (hold), five-six-seven,
(hold)". The held beats are where most beginners rush, so the holds matter as
much as the steps.

@plane z=1 x=0 y=0.6 label="Beat 1 - Left foot forward (break)"
# Beat 1: Forward break, left foot

This is the "1" of "one-two-three". From a neutral standing start, feet under
your hips, you step your **left foot forward** and shift your weight onto it.
This is a break step: you commit the weight forward, then immediately reverse it.

- Weight: now on the LEFT foot, forward of home.
- Right foot: stays planted where it started, heel may lift slightly.
- Frame: keep your chest lifted and shoulders level. Do not lunge.
- Common fault: stepping too far forward. A salsa basic is small, roughly one
  shoe length, not a stride.

Your partner mirrors this by breaking **back** on their right foot. Continue to
[[z=2|beat 2]].

@plane z=2 x=0 y=0 label="Beat 2 - Replace onto right foot"
# Beat 2: Replace, right foot

This is the "2". You **replace** your weight back onto the right foot, which
never actually moved, it just receives the weight again. Think of beats 1 and 2
as a tiny rock: forward, then back.

- Weight: back on the RIGHT foot, at home position.
- Left foot: now light, ready to close.
- Feel: the hips settle to the right as the weight returns. Let the knee soften.

Nothing travels here. You are still standing over your home spot. The motion is
all in the weight transfer, not in covering ground. Next is [[z=3|beat 3]].

@plane z=3 x=-0.5 y=0 label="Beat 3 - Left foot closes (step in place)"
# Beat 3: Close, left foot

This is the "3", the end of the first "quick-quick-slow". You bring the **left
foot back beside the right** and step onto it in place. The weight finishes on
the left.

- Weight: on the LEFT foot, feet roughly together.
- This is the "slow" landing: step on 3, then **hold beat 4**.
- Hip action: a clear Cuban-motion hip settle to the left as you take the step.

Do not take a step on beat 4. Hold it. New dancers almost always sneak an extra
step here and lose the timing for the whole song. Halfway done: head to
[[z=5|beat 5]] for the back half.

@plane z=5 x=0 y=-0.6 label="Beat 5 - Right foot back (break)"
# Beat 5: Back break, right foot

We skip a plane number on purpose: there is no step on beat 4, it is the hold.
On the "5" you mirror beat 1 in reverse, stepping your **right foot back** and
breaking your weight onto it.

- Weight: now on the RIGHT foot, behind home.
- Left foot: stays planted at home, ready to receive weight back.
- Posture: resist leaning your upper body back. Keep the torso stacked over your
  hips even as the foot goes back.

Your partner breaks **forward** on their left foot here, mirroring you exactly.
This is the same rock motion as [[z=1|beat 1]], just pointed the other way.

@plane z=6 x=0 y=0 label="Beat 6 - Replace onto left foot"
# Beat 6: Replace, left foot

This is the "6". **Replace** your weight forward onto the left foot, which stayed
at home. The little back-and-forward rock of beats 5 and 6 mirrors the
forward-and-back of beats 1 and 2.

- Weight: back on the LEFT foot, at home position.
- Right foot: now light, ready to close.
- Keep it small. Like beat 2, nothing travels; the body stays over home.

One step left in the basic. Finish on [[z=7|beat 7]].

@plane z=7 x=0.5 y=0 label="Beat 7 - Right foot closes (step in place)"
# Beat 7: Close, right foot

This is the "7", the final weighted step of the basic. Bring the **right foot
back beside the left** and step onto it in place. The weight finishes on the
right.

- Weight: on the RIGHT foot, feet together.
- This is the second "slow": step on 7, then **hold beat 8**.
- On the hold, you are perfectly reset: weight right, ready to break left forward
  again.

That hold on beat 8 hands you straight back into [[z=1|beat 1]], and the basic
loops. Once this feels automatic, the same six-step skeleton underpins the
cross-body lead, the right turn, and almost every pattern that follows.

title: API Request Lifecycle: POST /v1/orders
src:
---
3md: 1.0
axis: step
title: API Request Lifecycle: POST /v1/orders
endpoint: POST /v1/orders
service: api-gateway
trace_id: 4f9a-2b71-c003
status: 201 Created
---
One request, traced end to end. Each plane is a stage the request passes through.
Scrub the Z axis to follow a single POST from the wire to the response.

@plane z=0 label="Ingress and TLS"
# 1. Ingress and TLS

The request arrives at the edge and is terminated.

```
POST /v1/orders HTTP/2
Host: api.corvid.dev
Authorization: Bearer eyJhbGciOi...
Content-Type: application/json
Content-Length: 142
```

| Field | Value |
| --- | --- |
| Client IP | 203.0.113.44 |
| TLS | 1.3, cert valid |
| Trace started | 4f9a-2b71-c003 |

@plane z=1 label="Auth and Rate Limit"
# 2. Auth and Rate Limit

Verify the bearer token and check the caller's quota.

```
[ ok ]  token verified  (sub=acct_8821, exp ok)
[ ok ]  scope check      orders:write present
[ ok ]  rate limit       17 / 1000 this minute
```

If the token had failed here, the request would short-circuit to a 401 and never
reach [[z=3|the handler]].

@plane z=2 label="Validation"
# 3. Validation

Parse and validate the JSON body against the schema.

```json
{
  "sku": "WIDGET-BLUE-M",
  "quantity": 2,
  "ship_to": "acct_8821_addr_1"
}
```

```
[ ok ]  sku exists
[ ok ]  quantity >= 1
[ ok ]  ship_to belongs to caller
```

@plane z=3 label="Business Handler"
# 4. Business Handler

Reserve inventory and create the order record.

```
[ ok ]  inventory.reserve(WIDGET-BLUE-M, 2)   -> reserved
[ ok ]  db INSERT orders                       -> id=ord_55012
[ ok ]  events.publish("order.created")        -> queued
```

| Field | Value |
| --- | --- |
| Order ID | ord_55012 |
| DB latency | 8 ms |
| Reserved | 2 units |

@plane z=4 label="Response and Logging"
# 5. Response and Logging

Serialize the result, emit metrics, and close the trace.

```
HTTP/2 201 Created
Location: /v1/orders/ord_55012
Content-Type: application/json

{ "id": "ord_55012", "status": "confirmed" }
```

| Field | Value |
| --- | --- |
| Status | 201 Created |
| Total latency | 31 ms |
| Trace closed | 4f9a-2b71-c003 |

Lifecycle complete. The full path from [[z=0|ingress]] to here took 31 ms.

title: Halcyon DC-2 - Rack Layout by Row
src:
---
3md: 1.0
axis: floor
title: Halcyon DC-2 - Rack Layout by Row
facility: Halcyon DC-2, Hall B
operator: Corvid Infrastructure
units: rack-U (1U = 44.45mm)
hot_aisle: A/B between rows, B/C between rows
power: 2N, A-feed and B-feed per rack
---
Cold-aisle/hot-aisle layout for Hall B, drawn one row at a time along the Z
(floor) axis. Walk the hall from the door inward: Row A (network and core),
Row B (compute), then Row C (storage and backup). Each plane is a single row
of 42U racks viewed from the front (the cold aisle). The x/y offsets place each
row on the raised floor grid in 600mm tiles, measured from the SE door corner.

Power is 2N throughout: every rack has an A-feed (Row PDU left) and a B-feed
(Row PDU right) on separate UPS strings. Follow the cross-plane links to trace
uplinks from compute up to the spine, and replication from storage back to
compute.

@plane z=0 x=0 y=0 label="Row A - Network & Core"
# Row A - Network Spine and Edge

The first row past the man-trap door. Carries all north-south and east-west
fabric for the hall, so it sits closest to the meet-me room cross-connects.

| Rack | Contents | Front-U used | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| A1 | Spine switch pair (32x400G) | 2U + 2U | MLAG peer-link across A1/A2 |
| A2 | Spine switch pair (32x400G) | 2U + 2U | Second spine, B-feed primary |
| A3 | Border/edge routers | 4U | Two upstreams, BGP to transit |
| A4 | Out-of-band + console servers | 6U | Separate mgmt VRF, 1G OOB |
| A5 | Fiber patch + MPO cassettes | 12U | Trunk break-out to Rows B and C |

- **Cold aisle** faces z-positive (toward Row B). Intakes pull from the 18C
  supply under the perforated tiles on this side.
- **Cabling**: overhead ladder rack at 2.6m carries the spine-to-leaf trunks
  down to the [[z=1|compute leaf switches in Row B]] and the
  [[z=2|storage leaf in Row C]].
- **Power draw**: ~6.2 kW per rack, well under the 8 kW/rack budget, because the
  switching gear is light compared to compute.

Console reachability for every device in the hall terminates in A4; if a leaf in
Row B goes dark, recover it from the [[z=0|OOB servers here]].

@plane z=1 x=0 y=4 label="Row B - Compute"
# Row B - Compute Fleet

The densest row. Twelve compute racks plus a leaf pair at each end of the row
for a leaf-spine fabric. Hot air exhausts into the B/C hot aisle and returns to
the CRAH units at the row ends.

```
  cold aisle (faces Row A)
  +----+----+----+----+----+----+
  | B0 | B1 | B2 | B3 | B4 | B5 |   B0 = leaf-1 (top), compute below
  +----+----+----+----+----+----+
  | B6 | B7 | B8 | B9 | B10| B11|   B11 = leaf-2 (top), compute below
  +----+----+----+----+----+----+
  hot aisle (faces Row C, contained)
```

| Item | Per rack | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| Compute nodes | 20x 1U dual-socket | 40 racks total of headroom in hall |
| Leaf switches | 2x 48x25G + 8x100G | One pair per 12-rack pod |
| In-rack PDU | 2x 32A 3-phase | A-feed left, B-feed right |
| Blanking panels | all empty U | Mandatory, prevents recirculation |

- **Uplinks**: each leaf pair takes 8x 100G north to the
  [[z=0|spine switches in Row A]] (4 to each spine, ECMP).
- **Storage path**: NVMe-over-TCP mounts come from the
  [[z=2|all-flash arrays in Row C]] across the leaf fabric, never through Row A.
- **Thermal**: hot-aisle containment with chimney returns. Target dT is 12C
  across the node; if intake at B exceeds 27C, the CRAH at the row end ramps
  before any throttling.

Power is the binding constraint here: at 7.4 kW/rack the pod draws ~89 kW, so
the 100 kW A-string and 100 kW B-string each carry the full row alone under 2N.

@plane z=2 x=0 y=8 label="Row C - Storage & Backup"
# Row C - Storage and Backup

The back row, against the hall's rear wall. All-flash primary arrays up front,
high-density spinning disk for backups and cold tiers behind the storage leaf.

| Rack | Contents | Front-U used | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| C1 | All-flash array head + 2 shelves | 8U | NVMe, serves Row B over TCP |
| C2 | All-flash expansion shelves | 24U | Same pool as C1 |
| C3 | Storage leaf pair (48x25G) | 2U + 2U | Uplinks to Row A spine |
| C4 | Backup target (dedup appliance) | 4U | Nightly snapshots land here |
| C5 | Cold archive JBODs (90-bay) | 36U | 4U head + JBOD chain |

- **Replication**: primary volumes in C1/C2 serve the
  [[z=1|compute nodes in Row B]] and snapshot every 15 minutes to the C4 dedup
  appliance; weekly fulls spool to the C5 archive.
- **Fabric**: the C3 leaf pair uplinks to the
  [[z=0|spine in Row A]] just like the compute leaves, keeping storage traffic
  one hop from any node.
- **Weight**: C5's loaded JBOD racks hit ~1,360 kg, so they sit over the
  reinforced floor tiles on the rear two grid columns (rated 1,500 kg/tile).

Power here is bimodal: the flash racks (C1-C3) run cool at ~4 kW, while the
archive racks (C4-C5) spin up to ~9 kW during backup windows. Backups are
scheduled 01:00-04:00 to stay off the compute power peak in
[[z=1|Row B]].

title: CI/CD Pipeline for api-gateway
src:
---
3md: 1.0
axis: stage
title: CI/CD Pipeline for api-gateway
trigger: push to main
commit: 7a6b5c4
runner: corvid-ci
status: passed
---
The build pipeline, stage by stage. Each plane is one stage; scrub the Z axis to
walk from checkout to deploy. A stage only runs if every earlier stage passed.

@plane z=0 label="Checkout and Setup"
# 1. Checkout and Setup

Clone the repo at the triggering commit and restore caches.

```
[ ok ]  git checkout 7a6b5c4
[ ok ]  restore cache: ~/.bun (hit)
[ ok ]  bun install --frozen-lockfile
```

Duration: 14s. Status: passed.

@plane z=1 label="Lint and Typecheck"
# 2. Lint and Typecheck

Static analysis. No network, fully cached.

```
[ ok ]  bun run lint        0 errors, 0 warnings
[ ok ]  bun run typecheck    no type errors
```

Duration: 22s. Status: passed.

@plane z=2 label="Unit Tests"
# 3. Unit Tests

Run the suite with coverage gating at 80%.

```
[ ok ]  bun test
        148 pass, 0 fail, 0 skip
        coverage: lines 87.3%  (gate 80%)
```

Duration: 41s. Status: passed.

@plane z=3 label="Build Image"
# 4. Build Image

Build and tag the container, push to the registry.

```
[ ok ]  docker build -t api-gateway:7a6b5c4 .
[ ok ]  docker push registry.corvid/api-gateway:7a6b5c4
        digest: sha256:b91f...e07a
```

Duration: 1m 38s. Status: passed.

@plane z=4 label="Deploy to Staging"
# 5. Deploy to Staging

Roll the new image onto staging and run smoke checks.

```
[ ok ]  kubectl set image deploy/api-gateway app=...:7a6b5c4
[ ok ]  rollout complete (3/3 pods ready)
[ ok ]  smoke: GET /healthz -> 200
```

Duration: 55s. Status: passed.

Promotion to production is gated on a manual approval. The artifact built in
[[z=3|the build image stage]] is the exact image that will be promoted.

title: Database Migration Sequence
src:
---
3md: 1.0
axis: version
title: Database Migration Sequence
database: app_production
engine: postgres 16
current_version: 0004
direction: forward
---
The schema migration chain. Each plane is one numbered migration; apply them in
Z order. Each plane carries the up SQL and the matching rollback.

@plane z=0 label="0001 initial schema"
# 0001  initial schema

```sql
CREATE TABLE users (
  id          BIGSERIAL PRIMARY KEY,
  email       TEXT NOT NULL UNIQUE,
  created_at  TIMESTAMPTZ NOT NULL DEFAULT now()
);
```

Rollback:

```sql
DROP TABLE users;
```

@plane z=1 label="0002 add password hash"
# 0002  add password hash

```sql
ALTER TABLE users ADD COLUMN password_hash TEXT;
UPDATE users SET password_hash = '' WHERE password_hash IS NULL;
ALTER TABLE users ALTER COLUMN password_hash SET NOT NULL;
```

Rollback:

```sql
ALTER TABLE users DROP COLUMN password_hash;
```

@plane z=2 label="0003 sessions table"
# 0003  sessions table

```sql
CREATE TABLE sessions (
  id          UUID PRIMARY KEY DEFAULT gen_random_uuid(),
  user_id     BIGINT NOT NULL REFERENCES users(id) ON DELETE CASCADE,
  expires_at  TIMESTAMPTZ NOT NULL,
  created_at  TIMESTAMPTZ NOT NULL DEFAULT now()
);
CREATE INDEX idx_sessions_user ON sessions(user_id);
```

Rollback:

```sql
DROP TABLE sessions;
```

This table is what the refresh-token rotation in [[z=3|migration 0004]] extends.

@plane z=3 label="0004 refresh rotation"
# 0004  refresh rotation

```sql
ALTER TABLE sessions
  ADD COLUMN refresh_token TEXT,
  ADD COLUMN rotated_from  UUID REFERENCES sessions(id);

CREATE UNIQUE INDEX idx_sessions_refresh
  ON sessions(refresh_token)
  WHERE refresh_token IS NOT NULL;
```

Rollback:

```sql
DROP INDEX idx_sessions_refresh;
ALTER TABLE sessions
  DROP COLUMN rotated_from,
  DROP COLUMN refresh_token;
```

Current head. The next planned migration (0005) will add a partial index for
expired-session cleanup.

title: Server Rack and Network Map: Rack B
src:
---
3md: 1.0
axis: space
title: Server Rack and Network Map: Rack B
site: PDX-DC1
rack: B
height_u: 8
note: z is rack depth tier; x and y place each node on the floor/rack plan
---
A spatial map of rack B. The Z axis is a logical depth tier (edge in front,
storage at back); `x` and `y` give each plane's position on the rack/floor plan
for a spatial viewer. The ASCII shows the front elevation of the rack.

Rack B front elevation:

```
+--------------------------------------+
| U8  [ switch-core-1   ] 48-port 10G  |
| U7  [ fw-edge-1       ] firewall     |
| U6  [ web-01          ] app node     |
| U5  [ web-02          ] app node     |
| U4  [ db-primary      ] postgres     |
| U3  [ db-replica      ] postgres     |
| U2  [ nas-archive     ] 96 TB        |
| U1  [ pdu / blank     ]              |
+--------------------------------------+
```

@plane z=0 x=0 y=0 label="Edge: core switch"
# Edge tier: switch-core-1 (U8)

The top-of-rack switch. Everything in the rack uplinks here.

| Field | Value |
| --- | --- |
| Mgmt IP | 10.0.0.2 |
| Uplink | 2x 40G to spine |
| Ports used | 18 / 48 |
| Position | U8, front |

@plane z=1 x=0 y=1 label="Edge: firewall"
# Edge tier: fw-edge-1 (U7)

North-south traffic policy enforcement.

| Field | Value |
| --- | --- |
| Mgmt IP | 10.0.0.3 |
| Throughput | 10 Gbps |
| Rules | 214 active |
| Position | U7, front |

@plane z=2 x=1 y=0 label="App: web-01 and web-02"
# App tier: web-01 / web-02 (U6, U5)

Two stateless app nodes behind the edge. Either can be drained for maintenance.

| Node | IP | Role | Position |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| web-01 | 10.0.1.11 | app | U6, front |
| web-02 | 10.0.1.12 | app | U5, front |

Both nodes talk to the database tier at [[z=3|the data tier]].

@plane z=3 x=1 y=1 label="Data: primary and replica"
# Data tier: db-primary / db-replica (U4, U3)

Streaming replication, primary plus one hot standby.

| Node | IP | Role | Position |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| db-primary | 10.0.2.21 | postgres primary | U4, front |
| db-replica | 10.0.2.22 | postgres replica | U3, front |

@plane z=4 x=2 y=1 label="Storage: archive NAS"

Bulk storage and backup target for the rest of the rack.

| Field | Value |
| --- | --- |
| Mgmt IP | 10.0.3.31 |

| Protocols | NFS, SMB |

title: Helios Analytics - Live Product Demo
src:
---
3md: 1.0
axis: slide
title: Helios Analytics - Live Product Demo
presenter: Dana Okafor
duration: 12 minutes
audience: prospect evaluation call
---
Speaker script for the Helios Analytics product walkthrough. Each plane is one
slide. Stage directions are in italics; the spoken line is the body text. Keep
the laptop on the staging workspace (data is pre-seeded) and share the browser
tab, not the whole screen.

@plane z=0 x=0 y=0 label="Open: the problem"
# Why teams lose a day every week to dashboards

*Stage: title slide up, camera on. Do not screen-share yet.*

"Before I show you anything, here is the number that matters. Teams we talk to
spend roughly one full working day every week stitching exports together in
spreadsheets just to answer a question a stakeholder asked that morning."

Talking points (say two, not all three):

- The data already exists; it is just trapped in five separate tools.
- By the time the report is ready, the question has moved on.
- Nobody trusts a number they cannot trace back to its source.

"For the next ten minutes I want to show you how Helios collapses that day into
about ninety seconds. Let's start where your morning starts."

Next: jump to [[z=1|the live workspace]].

@plane z=1 x=4 y=0 label="Workspace tour"
# The workspace, in thirty seconds

*Stage: share the browser tab now. Land on the "Revenue" workspace.*

"This is a workspace. Think of it as one question, fully answered, that stays
fresh on its own. On the left is the source list, in the middle is the canvas,
and on the right is the trace panel we will come back to."

Point to each region as you name it:

1. **Sources** - the three connectors already linked (Stripe, Postgres, Sheets).
2. **Canvas** - the charts the team actually looks at every morning.
3. **Trace** - collapsed for now; it explains where every number came from.

"Notice the timestamp at the top: synced four minutes ago. Nobody pressed a
button. That sync is the whole point, and it is what makes the next part work."

We pull on that thread in [[z=2|the live query]].

@plane z=2 x=8 y=0 label="Live query"
# Ask a question in plain English

*Stage: click the ask bar. Type the query live; do not paste.*

"Here is the moment that usually gets the 'wait, do that again' reaction. I'll
ask for something nobody pre-built."

Type exactly this into the ask bar:

```
revenue by plan tier, last 8 weeks, weekly
```

"Two seconds. No SQL, no waiting on the data team. And this is not a static
picture - it is a real query against the live warehouse, so it will be just as
current tomorrow morning."

If someone asks "can it handle our messy data?", say:

> "Good question, and the honest answer is that it depends on the join keys.
> Let me show you exactly how we make that auditable."

That is the perfect setup for [[z=3|the trace panel]].

@plane z=3 x=12 y=0 label="Trust and trace"
# Every number, traced to its source

*Stage: expand the trace panel on the right. Click the $48,200 cell.*

"This is the slide that wins the security and finance folks. Click any number
and Helios shows you the lineage: the exact rows, the transformation, and the
source connector, down to the query it ran."

What the panel reveals for the selected cell:

- Source: Stripe charges table, 312 rows.
- Transform: summed `amount`, grouped by `plan_tier`, filtered to paid status.
- Freshness: pulled in this morning's sync at 06:14.

"So when your CFO asks 'where did this come from', the answer is one click, not
a Slack thread. Nothing here is a black box."

Now the part that makes it stick: [[z=4|sharing and alerts]].

@plane z=4 x=16 y=0 label="Share and automate"
# Make it work while you sleep

*Stage: open the share dialog, then the alert composer.*

"The last piece is what turns a demo into a habit. Two clicks to share this
workspace read-only with your team, and viewers always see the live version,
never a stale screenshot."

Then set one alert in front of them:

- **Trigger:** weekly revenue drops more than 10 percent week over week.
- **Channel:** the team's #revenue Slack channel.
- **Payload:** the chart plus the traced breakdown from the last slide.

"Now the dashboard watches itself and pings you only when something actually
needs a human. That day a week you were spending? It just disappeared."

Recap if anyone joined late: revisit [[z=0|the original problem]].

@plane z=5 x=20 y=0 label="Close and next steps"
# What happens after this call

*Stage: stop screen-share, camera back on, slow down the pace.*

"That is the full loop: connect, ask, trust, automate. I want to be respectful
of your time, so here is what I would suggest as a next step."

Concrete asks (pick based on the room):

1. A two-week trial on one of your real workspaces (we connect Stripe together).
2. A thirty-minute session with whoever owns your data warehouse.
3. Pricing tailored to your seat count, sent by end of day.

"What resonated most, and what did I not show you that you were hoping to see?"

*Stage: stop talking here. Let them answer first.*

title: Checkout Service Deployment Pipeline
src:
---
3md: 1.0
axis: status
title: Checkout Service Deployment Pipeline
service: checkout-api
trigger: push to main
runner: github-actions
---
This 3md document walks a single commit (`a1b9f3c`) through the deployment
pipeline for the `checkout-api` service. Each plane is one status the build
moves through, from a freshly pushed commit to a fully promoted production
release (or a rollback). The `x`/`y` offsets place the stages left to right
with the failure branch dropped below the main row.

@plane z=0 x=0 y=0 label="Queued"
# Queued

Commit `a1b9f3c` landed on `main` and a pipeline run was enqueued.

- **Author:** dana@checkout.dev
- **Message:** Fix coupon stacking when cart has mixed tax rates
- **Files changed:** 4 (+118 / -37)
- **Concurrency group:** `deploy-checkout-api` (one run at a time)

The runner waits for a free `ubuntu-latest` slot, restores the cached
`~/.cache/pnpm` store keyed on `pnpm-lock.yaml`, and checks out the repo at
full depth so the changelog generator can see prior tags.

```yaml
on:
  push:
    branches: [main]
concurrency:
  group: deploy-checkout-api
  cancel-in-progress: false
```

Next stop: [[z=1|Building]].

@plane z=1 x=1 y=0 label="Building"
# Building

The image is compiled and tests run against the candidate artifact.

1. `pnpm install --frozen-lockfile`
2. `pnpm run build` (TypeScript to `dist/`, tree-shaken)
3. `pnpm run test:unit` and `pnpm run test:contract`
4. `docker buildx build --platform linux/amd64 -t checkout-api:a1b9f3c .`

Quality gates that must all pass before the artifact is published:

| Gate | Threshold | This run |
| --- | --- | --- |
| Unit coverage | >= 80% | 84.2% |
| Contract tests | 0 failures | 0 failures |
| Lint | 0 errors | 0 errors |
| Image size | <= 250 MB | 211 MB |

The image is pushed to the registry as
`ghcr.io/checkout/checkout-api:a1b9f3c` and also tagged `:build-1492`.

From here the pipeline forks: success flows to [[z=2|Staging]], any failed
gate jumps to [[z=-1|Failed]].

@plane z=2 x=2 y=0 label="Staging"
# Staging

The built image is rolled out to the `staging` namespace and smoke tested.

```bash
kubectl -n staging set image deploy/checkout-api \
  app=ghcr.io/checkout/checkout-api:a1b9f3c
kubectl -n staging rollout status deploy/checkout-api --timeout=120s
```

Post-deploy smoke checks (run by the `verify-staging` job):

- `GET /healthz` returns `200` within 2s
- `POST /v1/cart/price` with the coupon-stacking fixture returns the
  corrected total of `$42.10` (regression for this exact bug)
- p95 latency over a 60s synthetic load stays under 180ms

A human approval is required to leave staging. The pipeline pauses on a
GitHub Environment protection rule (`production` reviewers: 1 of the on-call
group). Approve to advance to [[z=3|Production]]; if smoke checks fail the
run lands in [[z=-1|Failed]].

@plane z=3 x=3 y=0 label="Production"
# Production

A canary rollout shifts live traffic onto `a1b9f3c` in stages.

| Step | Traffic on new version | Hold | Auto-abort if |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| 1 | 5% | 5 min | 5xx rate > 1% |
| 2 | 25% | 10 min | p95 > 250ms |
| 3 | 100% | - | - |

The progressive rollout is driven by Argo Rollouts; the analysis template
queries Prometheus for `http_requests_total` and `request_duration_seconds`.
If any metric breaches its guardrail, Argo automatically reverts to the
previous ReplicaSet and the run is marked [[z=-1|Failed]].

On full promotion the pipeline tags the commit `prod-2026.06.23` and posts a
release note to `#deploys`. Done. See [[z=0|Queued]] for where this began.

@plane z=-1 x=1.5 y=-1 label="Failed / Rollback"
# Failed / Rollback

Any gate, smoke check, or canary guardrail that trips routes here.

Automatic actions taken:

- Roll the active Deployment back to the last known-good revision:
  `kubectl -n <ns> rollout undo deploy/checkout-api`
- Mark the GitHub Deployment status `failure` so the commit shows a red check
- Open (or update) an incident in PagerDuty tagged `checkout-api/deploy`
- Drop a threaded message in `#deploys` with the failing job's log link

Triage checklist for the on-call engineer:

- [ ] Identify which stage failed: [[z=1|Building]], [[z=2|Staging]], or
      [[z=3|Production]] canary
- [ ] Confirm prior revision is serving traffic and healthy
- [ ] Decide: forward-fix on a new commit, or hold `main` for a revert
- [ ] Once a fix is pushed, a fresh run starts back at [[z=0|Queued]]

No artifact from a failed run is ever promoted; the `prod` tag stays on the
last successful commit.

title: Corvid Coffee Logo, v1 through v5
src:
---
3md: 1.0
axis: version
title: Corvid Coffee Logo, v1 through v5
client: Corvid Coffee Roasters
designer: studio raven
brief: A wordmark plus a raven glyph for a small-batch coffee roaster
---
This stack records how the Corvid Coffee mark evolved across five rounds,
from the first concept sketch to the locked production artwork. Each plane is
one delivered version. Read them in order, or jump straight to the final at
[[z=4|the locked mark]].

@plane z=0 label="v1 concept" x=0 y=0
# v1: First concept

The opening sketch. Goal was to find a silhouette, not to refine type.

- **Glyph:** a full raven, side profile, wings folded
- **Type:** Times New Roman placeholder, all caps
- **Color:** flat black on white only
- **Layout:** glyph stacked above the wordmark

## What worked
The bird reads instantly as a corvid even at thumbnail size.

## What did not
- The placeholder serif feels corporate and dated for a roaster
- The full-body bird carries too much detail to survive small sizes
- No accent color, so it dies next to colorful packaging

Next round tightens the bird and picks a real typeface. See [[z=1|v2]].

@plane z=1 label="v2 typeface" x=2 y=0
# v2: Real typeface, simpler bird

We dropped the placeholder serif and cropped the bird to a head-and-beak mark.

- **Glyph:** raven head in profile, sharp beak, one visible eye
- **Type:** Fraunces (a warm contemporary serif), small caps
- **Color:** ink black plus a single warm cream (#F2E8D5)
- **Layout:** glyph to the left of the wordmark, baseline aligned

## What worked
- Cropping to the head fixed the small-size problem from [[z=0|v1]]
- Fraunces gives the warmth the old serif lacked

## What did not
- The eye detail still clogs at favicon scale
- Cream-on-white has almost no contrast; the accent is invisible on light packaging

Round three commits to a bolder palette and flattens the eye. See [[z=2|v3]].

@plane z=2 label="v3 color" x=4 y=0
# v3: Palette and contrast

This round is about color confidence. The bird gets a knockout eye instead of a drawn one.

- **Glyph:** same head, eye now a negative-space notch (no thin line to clog)
- **Type:** Fraunces, small caps, tracking opened by 4 percent
- **Color:** espresso brown (#3B2417) plus burnt orange (#D8682B)
- **Layout:** unchanged from [[z=1|v2]], glyph left of wordmark

## What worked
- Burnt orange finally gives the mark a memorable accent on the bag
- The negative-space eye holds up at 16px

## What did not
- Brown plus orange tested as "autumn", not "coffee", in client review
- The wordmark and glyph optical sizes drifted apart after the tracking change

Next we fix the metrics and cool the palette toward roasted-bean tones. See [[z=3|v4]].

@plane z=3 label="v4 refinement" x=6 y=0
# v4: Metric and weight refinement

A precision pass. No big swings, just making every part agree with every other part.

- **Glyph:** beak angle steepened 6 degrees so it points into the wordmark
- **Type:** Fraunces optical size 72 for the wordmark to match the glyph weight
- **Color:** dark roast brown (#2A1A12) plus a muted terracotta (#C25B3A)
- **Layout:** glyph and cap-height of the wordmark now share one baseline grid

## What worked
- Terracotta reads as coffee, not pumpkin; the client review concern from [[z=2|v3]] is resolved
- Optical-size matching removed the weight mismatch

## What did not
- One tiny issue remains: the beak notch is a hair too deep for embroidery

Round five is the production lock with the embroidery fix. See [[z=4|the final lock]].

@plane z=4 label="v5 locked" x=8 y=0
# v5: Production lock

The approved, shipping artwork. This is the version in the brand kit.

- **Glyph:** beak notch widened 0.3mm for clean embroidery and foil stamping
- **Type:** Fraunces optical 72, locked tracking, hinted for screen
- **Color:** dark roast brown (#2A1A12), terracotta (#C25B3A), plus pure white for knockout use
- **Layout:** final lockup, plus a stacked variant for square avatars

## Delivered assets
- Horizontal lockup (SVG, PDF, PNG at 1x/2x/3x)
- Stacked lockup for social avatars
- Single-color brown and single-color white versions
- Favicon and 16px monochrome glyph

## Lineage
Every change traces back through the rounds: silhouette from [[z=0|v1]],
typeface from [[z=1|v2]], accent color from [[z=2|v3]], and metrics from
[[z=3|v4]]. This plane locks all four decisions together.

title: The Sunken Foundry - Difficulty Layers
src:
---
3md: 1.0
axis: layer
title: The Sunken Foundry - Difficulty Layers
level_id: foundry-03
checkpoint: forge-entrance
author: Level Design
---
The same level geometry across three difficulty layers. Each layer toggles a
different set of hazards, enemies, and pickups laid over the identical room. The
spatial offsets keep the three views side by side so designers can diff a single
encounter between modes at a glance.

@plane z=0 x=0 y=0 label="Easy"
# Easy: The Sunken Foundry

A guided first run. The conveyor belts move slowly and the molten channels are
capped, so a misstep costs health but rarely a life.

## Encounters
- 2 Rust Beetles patrol the entry catwalk (slow, telegraph 1.2s before lunging).
- 1 Furnace Drone hovers near the central pit, fires single bolts every 3s.
- No enemies past the second checkpoint.

## Hazards
- Conveyor speed: 1.5 m/s, reversible by the wall lever.
- Molten channel is shielded by a permanent grate. You can stand on it.

## Pickups
- 3 Health Cells along the main path (one before each jump).
- Forge Key sits in the open on the central platform.

## Designer notes
- Teaches the lever-then-jump rhythm with zero time pressure.
- Compare the same room with live lava in [[z=1|Normal]].

@plane z=1 x=12 y=0 label="Normal"
# Normal: The Sunken Foundry

The intended experience. The grate retracts on a timer, the conveyors run at
combat speed, and enemies coordinate to push you toward the pit.

## Encounters
- 4 Rust Beetles, two flanking from the upper catwalk (telegraph 0.7s).
- 2 Furnace Drones fire 3-bolt bursts and reposition between volleys.
- 1 Slag Brute guards the Forge Key, staggered only by the conveyor crush.

## Hazards
- Conveyor speed: 3 m/s. The wall lever now has a 5s cooldown.
- Molten channel grate retracts for 4s every 10s. Watch the amber warning light.

## Pickups
- 2 Health Cells, both off the main path (one behind the Slag Brute).
- Forge Key is carried by the Slag Brute and drops on defeat.

## Designer notes
- The grate timer forces a commit: cross during the closed window or wait.
- Drop down a notch for a calmer pass in [[z=0|Easy]], or raise the stakes in [[z=2|Hard]].

@plane z=2 x=24 y=0 label="Hard"
# Hard: The Sunken Foundry

For players who have the rhythm memorized. The grate is gone, the conveyors
reverse without warning, and the room reads your position to spawn pressure.

## Encounters
- 6 Rust Beetles in two waves, the second wave triggered by reaching the pit.
- 3 Furnace Drones with homing bolts and a shared shield that drops only when one is destroyed.
- 1 Slag Brute (enraged variant) plus a Cinder Wraith that revives a fallen Beetle every 15s.

## Hazards
- Conveyor speed: 4.5 m/s and direction flips on a hidden 7s cycle.
- No grate. The molten channel is open the entire fight. Falling is instant death.

## Pickups
- 1 Health Cell only, suspended over the lava (grapple to reach it).
- Forge Key drops from the Slag Brute but lands on the active conveyor.

## Designer notes
- Removing the grate turns the central pit into the real boss of the room.
- The Cinder Wraith punishes ignoring adds, so kill priority matters here.
- This is the same geometry as [[z=1|Normal]]; only the overlay changed.

title: The Sunken Vault
src:
---
3md: 1.0
axis: space
title: The Sunken Vault
genre: "choose-your-path"
start: "z=0"
---
A tiny choose-your-path dungeon. Each plane is a room; x/y give its position on
an imagined map so a spatial viewer can lay it out. Follow the exits, which are
cross-plane links to other rooms. Begin at [[z=0|the entrance]].

@plane z=0 label="entrance" x=0 y=0
# The Entrance
Water drips somewhere ahead. A cold stair descends north into the dark, and a
rusted door sits to the east.

- Take the stair down to [[z=1|the flooded hall]].
- Force the rusted door into [[z=2|the old armory]].

@plane z=1 label="flooded-hall" x=0 y=-1
# The Flooded Hall
Black water reaches your knees. Something pale drifts just below the surface.
A passage continues east; the stair behind you climbs back up.

- Wade east to [[z=3|the vault]].
- Retreat up the stair to [[z=0|the entrance]].

@plane z=2 label="armory" x=1 y=0
# The Old Armory
Racks of corroded weapons. One spear is still keen. You take it.
A low arch leads south, deeper in.

- Go through the arch to [[z=3|the vault]].
- Back through the door to [[z=0|the entrance]].

@plane z=3 label="vault" x=1 y=-1
# The Sunken Vault
The water pools around a stone chest ringed with old wards. A guardian of silt
and bone rises as you approach.

- If you carry the spear (came via the armory), you strike true and open the
  chest: [[z=4|you win]].
- Unarmed, the guardian drags you under: [[z=4|you fall]].

@plane z=4 label="ending" x=2 y=-1
# Ending
However it went, triumph with the relic in hand or a quiet drowning in the
dark, the Sunken Vault keeps its silence again. The End.

title: Major Events in the Evolution of Life
src:
---
3md: 1.0
axis: time
title: Major Events in the Evolution of Life
author: CorvidLabs Gallery
span: ~3.8 billion years
---
A walk along deep time. Each plane is one milestone on the Z axis, ordered by
how long ago it happened (z is millions of years before present, so larger z is
further back). Read from the deepest plane forward, or jump straight to
[[z=0.3|the rise of humans]].

@plane z=3800 x=0 y=0 label="First Life (~3.8 Ga)"
# First Life

The oldest chemical and fossil traces of life appear in rocks roughly 3.5 to 3.8
billion years old. The earliest cells were single, membrane-bound prokaryotes
with no nucleus, living in the oceans.

- **Form:** simple anaerobic cells (no oxygen required)
- **Energy:** chemosynthesis near hydrothermal vents and shallow seas
- **Legacy:** the universal genetic code, shared by every organism since

Everything that follows, from a redwood to a blue whale, descends from this
single-celled starting point. The next great leap was learning to eat sunlight:
see [[z=2400|the Great Oxidation]].

@plane z=2400 x=2 y=0 label="Great Oxidation (~2.4 Ga)"
# The Great Oxidation Event

Cyanobacteria evolved oxygen-producing photosynthesis and, over hundreds of
millions of years, flooded the atmosphere with free oxygen. For most existing
microbes this was a poison, triggering one of Earth's earliest mass extinctions.

| Before | After |
| --- | --- |
| Trace atmospheric oxygen | Oxygen-rich atmosphere |
| Anaerobic life dominant | Aerobic respiration possible |

Oxygen was the fuel that later powered large, active animals. The first complex
cells came next: continue to [[z=1800|the eukaryotic cell]].

@plane z=1800 x=4 y=0 label="Eukaryotes (~1.8 Ga)"
# The Eukaryotic Cell

A larger, more complex cell type arose, defined by a nucleus and internal
organelles. The leading explanation is **endosymbiosis**: one cell engulfed
another, and the captive became a permanent partner.

1. An ancestral host cell absorbed an oxygen-using bacterium.
2. The partner became the **mitochondrion**, the cell's power plant.
3. A separate event gave plant ancestors the **chloroplast**.

This partnership is why your cells still carry mitochondrial DNA distinct from
the DNA in your nucleus. With efficient energy in hand, life eventually went
multicellular and big: jump to [[z=540|the Cambrian Explosion]].

@plane z=540 x=6 y=0 label="Cambrian Explosion (~540 Ma)"
# The Cambrian Explosion

In a geologically brief window, most major animal body plans appeared in the
fossil record. Hard shells, jointed limbs, compound eyes, and active predators
all show up together, preserved beautifully in sites like the Burgess Shale.

- First arthropods, mollusks, and early chordates (our distant relatives)
- The arms race between predator and prey drove rapid innovation
- Vision and mineralized skeletons became widespread

Life had been microscopic for over three billion years; in the Cambrian it got
complicated fast. From these chordates came backbones, and eventually limbs for
land: move forward to [[z=375|life moves ashore]].

@plane z=375 x=8 y=0 label="Life on Land (~375 Ma)"
# Vertebrates Move Ashore

Lobe-finned fishes such as *Tiktaalik* show the transition from water to land:
sturdy, weight-bearing fins, a flexible neck, and lungs alongside gills. Their
descendants, the tetrapods, gave rise to amphibians, reptiles, birds, and
mammals.

> Tiktaalik is often called a "fishapod," part fish and part four-legged animal,
> a clear snapshot of an evolutionary transition in progress.

Plants and arthropods had greened the land first, building the food web these
pioneers walked into. The last stop on this timeline is the youngest branch:
arrive at [[z=0.3|the human lineage]].

@plane z=0.3 x=10 y=0 label="Humans (~300 Ka)"
# The Human Lineage

Anatomically modern *Homo sapiens* appears in Africa around 300,000 years ago,
the latest twig on an ancient tree. On the scale of this document, all of
recorded human history is a thin sliver at the very surface.

- **Brain and language:** complex cognition, symbolic thought, culture
- **Tools and fire:** technology that reshaped diet and environment
- **Migration:** out of Africa and across every continent

Stand here at the present and look back: every step, from [[z=3800|the first
cell]] through oxygen, complex cells, and limbs on land, was a prerequisite for
this plane existing at all.

title: Q2 FY2026 Business Review - Northwind Cloud
src:
---
3md: 1.0
axis: depth
title: Q2 FY2026 Business Review - Northwind Cloud
period: Q2 FY2026 (Apr 1 - Jun 30)
author: Office of the CFO
status: Board draft
---
Read this report at the depth you need. The surface plane (z=0) is the one-paragraph
executive summary. Each deeper plane peels back a layer: the KPI scorecard, the
segment-by-segment breakdown, and finally the line-level financial detail and
assumptions. Drill down by following the cross-plane links.

@plane z=0 label="Executive Summary"
# Executive Summary

Northwind Cloud closed Q2 FY2026 with **$48.2M in revenue**, up 19% year over year and
ahead of our $46.5M plan. Growth was led by the Enterprise segment and a strong rebound
in net revenue retention (now 114%). Operating margin improved 4 points to 12% as we held
headcount flat while revenue grew. The one risk worth the board's attention is rising cloud
infrastructure cost, which grew faster than revenue this quarter.

**Recommendation:** approve the FY2026 reforecast to $198M and fund the two hires in the
Reliability team needed to bend the infrastructure cost curve.

Go deeper: [[z=1|the KPI scorecard]], then [[z=2|segment performance]], then the
[[z=3|financial detail and assumptions]].

@plane z=1 x=0 y=0 label="KPI Scorecard"
# KPI Scorecard

Headline metrics versus plan and versus the prior-year quarter.

| Metric | Q2 FY2026 | Plan | YoY |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| Revenue | $48.2M | $46.5M | +19% |
| Net revenue retention | 114% | 110% | +6 pts |
| Gross margin | 71% | 72% | -1 pt |
| Operating margin | 12% | 9% | +4 pts |
| Cash from operations | $7.1M | $5.4M | +31% |
| Logo churn (annualized) | 5.8% | 7.0% | -1.5 pts |

**What moved:** revenue and retention both beat plan, driven by Enterprise expansions.
Gross margin slipped one point because infrastructure cost outpaced revenue (see the
[[z=3|cost detail]]). Operating margin still rose because we delivered the beat with flat
headcount.

For the story behind these numbers, open [[z=2|segment performance]].

@plane z=2 x=0 y=0 label="Segment Performance"
# Segment Performance

Revenue by segment, with the drivers behind each line.

### Enterprise - $26.4M (+28% YoY)

The growth engine this quarter. We signed three seven-figure renewals (Meridian Bank,
Cascade Health, Atlas Logistics), each expanding seat count by 30% or more on multi-year
terms. Net revenue retention in this segment hit 122%. Sales cycle shortened from 91 to
74 days as the new solutions-engineering pod removed late-stage technical blockers.

### Mid-Market - $15.1M (+11% YoY)

Steady. New-logo acquisition was on plan (47 new accounts), but expansion lagged because
the self-serve upgrade flow shipped late. The flow is now live and early data shows a
2.3x lift in in-product upgrades.

### SMB / Self-Serve - $6.7M (-4% YoY)

The soft spot. Logo churn here remains elevated at 11% annualized, concentrated in
accounts that never completed onboarding. The Customer Success team launched a guided
14-day activation program in May; cohorts since then show 38% better 90-day retention,
but the full revenue effect is still a quarter or two out.

Numbers tie back to the [[z=1|scorecard]]. For the full P&L and the assumptions behind
the reforecast, go to [[z=3|financial detail]].

@plane z=3 x=0 y=0 label="Financial Detail and Assumptions"
# Financial Detail and Assumptions

## Condensed P&L (Q2 FY2026, $000s)

| Line | Q2 FY2026 | Q2 FY2025 |
| --- | --- | --- |
| Revenue | 48,200 | 40,500 |
| Cost of revenue | 13,980 | 11,340 |
| **Gross profit** | **34,220** | **29,160** |
| Sales and marketing | 16,100 | 14,800 |
| Research and development | 9,200 | 8,600 |
| General and administrative | 3,140 | 3,010 |
| **Operating income** | **5,780** | **2,750** |

## The infrastructure cost watch item

Cost of revenue grew 23% YoY against 19% revenue growth. The gap is almost entirely
compute and egress in our two largest regions:

- Compute (managed Kubernetes): +31% YoY, driven by Enterprise workloads that run hotter
  than the SMB profile our capacity model assumed.
- Network egress: +44% YoY, concentrated in the new data-export feature shipped in April.

**Mitigation:** the two Reliability hires in the recommendation would own a right-sizing
and commitment-purchase program estimated to recover 1.5 to 2.0 gross-margin points by
Q4. This is the funding ask referenced in the [[z=0|executive summary]].

## FY2026 reforecast assumptions

1. Enterprise net revenue retention holds at 118% to 122% (Q2 actual: 122%).
2. SMB churn improves to 9% annualized as the activation program matures.
3. No price increases modeled; the reforecast is volume-driven.
4. Infrastructure mitigation lands by Q4, recovering ~1.5 margin points.

Under these assumptions the full-year lands at **$198M revenue** and **13% operating
margin**. Back up to the [[z=2|segment view]] or the [[z=1|scorecard]].

title: Measuring the Vitamin C Content of Orange Juice by Iodometric Titration
src:
---
3md: 1.0
axis: step
title: Measuring the Vitamin C Content of Orange Juice by Iodometric Titration
discipline: Analytical Chemistry
duration: 90 minutes
hazard-level: moderate
---
This procedure determines the ascorbic acid (vitamin C) concentration of a
juice sample by titrating it against a standardized iodine solution. Ascorbic
acid reduces iodine to iodide; once all the ascorbic acid is consumed, the next
drop of iodine reacts with a starch indicator to produce a blue-black color
that marks the endpoint.

Read every step before starting. Wear splash goggles and nitrile gloves for the
duration. The Z axis is the procedure step, in order.

@plane z=0 x=0 y=0 label="Safety and Materials"
# Step 0: Safety, Materials, and Reagents

## Hazards
- Iodine solution stains skin and clothing and is an irritant. Avoid contact.
- Glassware is fragile. Inspect the burette for chips before use.
- No open flames are required; this is a room-temperature procedure.

## Equipment
- 50 mL burette with stand and clamp
- 250 mL Erlenmeyer flask (x3, one per trial)
- 25 mL volumetric pipette and pipette filler
- 100 mL beaker, wash bottle of distilled water
- White tile or sheet of paper (to read the color change against)

## Reagents
| Reagent | Concentration | Volume needed |
|---------|---------------|---------------|
| Standardized iodine (I2) | 0.005 mol/L | ~120 mL |
| Starch indicator | 1% w/v | a few drops |
| Orange juice sample | as supplied | 75 mL total |

Once everything above is laid out and checked, continue to
[[z=1|preparing the burette]].

@plane z=1 x=2 y=0 label="Prepare the Burette"
# Step 1: Rinse and Fill the Burette

1. Rinse the clean burette with about 5 mL of the standardized iodine
   solution. Tilt and rotate so the liquid coats the inner wall, then drain
   through the stopcock to waste. This removes residual water that would dilute
   the titrant.
2. Close the stopcock. Using a funnel, fill the burette above the zero mark
   with iodine solution.
3. Open the stopcock briefly to fill the tip below the stopcock and expel any
   air bubble. An air bubble that leaves during titration adds false volume.
4. Drain to bring the meniscus to the 0.00 mL line. Read the bottom of the
   meniscus at eye level to avoid parallax error.

Record the exact starting reading; do not assume it is precisely zero. With the
burette ready, move on to [[z=2|preparing the sample]].

@plane z=2 x=4 y=0 label="Prepare the Sample"
# Step 2: Measure the Juice Sample

1. Swirl the juice container gently to suspend any pulp evenly. Do not shake
   hard, which introduces air bubbles and makes pipetting inaccurate.
2. Pipette exactly 25.00 mL of juice into a clean 250 mL Erlenmeyer flask using
   the volumetric pipette and filler. Never pipette by mouth.
3. Add roughly 50 mL of distilled water to the flask. This dilution does not
   change the amount of ascorbic acid present; it only gives a larger, lighter
   volume so the endpoint color is easier to see.
4. Add 5 to 8 drops of 1% starch indicator and swirl to mix.

The flask now contains a known volume of sample ready for titration. Proceed to
[[z=3|the titration]].

@plane z=3 x=6 y=0 label="Titrate to Endpoint"
# Step 3: Titrate Against Iodine

Place the flask on the white tile beneath the burette tip.

1. Add iodine solution from the burette in roughly 1 mL increments at first,
   swirling the flask continuously after each addition. The blue-black color
   that forms on contact should fade as it mixes.
2. As the color begins to fade more slowly, slow down to dropwise addition.
   This is the approach to the endpoint.
3. The endpoint is the first faint blue-black color that persists for at least
   30 seconds of swirling. Stop immediately and close the stopcock.
4. Read and record the final burette volume to the nearest 0.05 mL.

> Tip: if you overshoot (a strong, lasting blue), discard the trial and repeat.
> A perceptibly dark result is past the endpoint and will overstate the titre.

Repeat Steps 1 through 3 with fresh 25.00 mL juice aliquots until you have at
least three concordant trials (titres agreeing within 0.10 mL). Then continue to
[[z=4|the calculation]].

@plane z=4 x=8 y=0 label="Calculate and Report"
# Step 4: Calculate the Vitamin C Concentration

For each trial, the titre is the final reading minus the starting reading.
Average the concordant titres.

## Stoichiometry
Ascorbic acid reacts with iodine in a 1:1 mole ratio:

```
C6H8O6 + I2 -> C6H6O6 + 2 H+ + 2 I-
```

## Worked example
Suppose the mean titre is 18.40 mL of 0.005 mol/L iodine for a 25.00 mL sample.

1. Moles of I2 = 0.01840 L x 0.005 mol/L = 9.20 x 10^-5 mol
2. Moles of ascorbic acid = 9.20 x 10^-5 mol (1:1 ratio)
3. Mass = 9.20 x 10^-5 mol x 176.12 g/mol = 0.01620 g = 16.20 mg
4. Concentration = 16.20 mg / 25.00 mL = 0.648 mg/mL = 64.8 mg per 100 mL

## Reporting
- Quote the result as mg of vitamin C per 100 mL, with the mean and the range
  of the concordant titres.
- Compare against the label value, if printed, and comment on any difference
  (oxidation during storage typically lowers the measured value).

If your trials were not concordant, return to [[z=3|the titration]] and run
additional aliquots before averaging.

title: The Hollis Family Tree
src:
---
3md: 1.0
axis: depth
title: The Hollis Family Tree
surname: Hollis
maintained-by: Margaret Hollis-Reyes
last-updated: 2026-06-20
note: Depth counts generations back from the present. z=0 is the living children; deeper planes are older ancestors.
---
A working genealogy of the Hollis line, organized by generation. Each plane is one
generation. The deepest plane (z=3) holds the earliest known couple; the shallowest
(z=0) holds the youngest living descendants. Read top to bottom within a plane for
siblings; follow the cross-plane links to trace a line of descent.

@plane z=3 label="Great-grandparents (born 1890s-1900s)" x=0 y=0
# Generation IV - The Founders

The earliest couple we have records for. Both emigrated through Ellis Island and
settled in Lowell, Massachusetts.

## Samuel Hollis (1894-1971)
- Born in County Cork, Ireland; arrived 1911 aboard the SS Caledonia.
- Worked as a loom mechanic in the Lowell textile mills.
- Married Anna in 1919 at St. Patrick's Church.

## Anna Hollis, nee Kowalski (1898-1983)
- Born in Krakow; arrived 1913.
- Kept the family's first ledger, which is the source for most of these dates.

Together they had three children. The line we follow runs through their son
Thomas: see [[z=2|Generation III]].

@plane z=2 label="Grandparents (born 1920s)" x=2 y=0
# Generation III - The Builders

Samuel and Anna's children, born between the wars.

## Thomas Hollis (1921-1999)  *(our line)*
- Eldest son of [[z=3|Samuel and Anna]].
- Served in the Pacific, 1942-1945; returned to study civil engineering.
- Married Eleanor Vance in 1948.

## Eleanor Hollis, nee Vance (1926-2011)
- Schoolteacher in Concord for thirty-one years.
- Her diaries fill the gap in records for the 1950s.

## Siblings of Thomas (collateral lines, not expanded here)
- Mary Hollis (1923-2004), married into the Doyle family.
- Patrick Hollis (1925-1990), moved to Chicago; descendants out of contact.

Thomas and Eleanor raised two children, listed in [[z=1|Generation II]].

@plane z=1 label="Parents and their siblings (born 1950s)" x=4 y=0
# Generation II - The Parents

The children of [[z=2|Thomas and Eleanor]]. This is the generation most people
in the family still remember firsthand.

## Robert Hollis (b. 1952)  *(our line)*
- Architect, now retired, living in Portland, Maine.
- Married Diane Okafor in 1979.

## Diane Hollis, nee Okafor (b. 1955)
- Pediatric nurse; first non-European ancestor in the direct line.
- Brought the Okafor branch (Lagos, Nigeria) into the tree.

## Susan Hollis (b. 1956)
- Younger sister of Robert; never married, godmother to all of his children.
- Keeps the physical photo archive that these notes draw from.

Robert and Diane are the parents of the youngest generation in [[z=0|Generation I]].

@plane z=0 label="Living children (born 1980s-1990s)" x=6 y=0
# Generation I - The Present

The current youngest generation: children of [[z=1|Robert and Diane]].
This plane is updated as new births and marriages occur.

## Margaret Hollis-Reyes (b. 1983)
- Eldest. Maintainer of this family tree.
- Married Carlos Reyes in 2012; two children (Generation Zero, pending a new plane).

## James Hollis (b. 1986)
- Marine biologist in Monterey, California.
- Unmarried as of this revision.

## Aunt Susan's note
Per [[z=1|Susan Hollis]], a third sibling, Grace, was stillborn in 1989 and is
recorded here so she is not forgotten.

---

*To extend the tree forward, add a plane at z=-1 for the grandchildren.*

title: Self-Hosting Backups FAQ
src:
---
3md: 1.0
axis: depth
title: Self-Hosting Backups FAQ
subject: backup strategy for a home server
audience: people running a NAS or home server who want their data to survive a disk failure
convention: z=0 is the headline answer; higher z drills deeper into mechanics and edge cases
---
This FAQ is stacked by depth. Plane `z=0` is the one-line answer you can act on
today. Each deeper plane (z=1, z=2, z=3) peels back a layer: the rule of thumb,
then how it actually works, then the failure modes nobody warns you about.

Read straight through, or stop as soon as you have what you need.

@plane z=0 label="Quick answers" x=0 y=0
# Backups, in one breath

**Q: How many copies of my data do I need?**
Three. Keep the **3-2-1 rule**: 3 copies, on 2 different kinds of media, with 1 copy off-site.

**Q: Is RAID a backup?**
No. RAID protects against a *disk* dying, not against you deleting the wrong folder, ransomware, or the whole machine being stolen.

**Q: How often should backups run?**
Nightly for anything you would hate to re-create. Hourly snapshots for active projects.

**Q: How do I know a backup is good?**
Restore from it. A backup you have never restored is a hope, not a backup.

Want the reasoning? Drill into [[z=1|the rules of thumb]].

@plane z=1 label="Rules of thumb" x=2 y=0
# Why three copies, and what counts

The 3-2-1 rule maps to three independent ways your data dies:

- **3 copies** survive a single accident (one corrupt file is still good in two places).
- **2 media types** survive a media-class failure. An SSD and a spinning disk fail for different reasons; two SSDs from the same batch can fail the same week.
- **1 off-site copy** survives fire, flood, theft, and "the power supply took the whole rack with it."

**On RAID specifically:** RAID 1 mirrors and RAID 5/6 add parity, so a drive can fail and you stay online. That is *availability*, not *recoverability*. Every write, including a mistaken `rm -rf`, hits all members instantly. RAID buys uptime; backups buy a time machine.

**On frequency:** match the interval to how much work you can afford to lose (your RPO, recovery point objective). Lose at most an hour of edits? Snapshot hourly. Lose at most a day of family photos? Nightly is plenty.

Curious how the off-site and snapshot pieces actually work? See [[z=2|the mechanics]].

@plane z=2 label="How it works" x=4 y=0
# The mechanics underneath

**Snapshots vs copies.** A filesystem snapshot (ZFS, Btrfs, LVM) is not a second copy of your bytes. It is a frozen view: the filesystem keeps the old blocks alive instead of freeing them when data changes. That makes snapshots near-instant and cheap, but they live on the *same pool*. A snapshot rescues you from a bad edit; it does nothing if the pool itself dies. That is why a snapshot is step one, and `zfs send` (or `restic`/`borg`) to another machine is step two.

**Incremental backups.** Tools like restic and Borg deduplicate at the chunk level. The first run copies everything; later runs copy only changed chunks and record a new "snapshot" that points at the shared chunk store. A nightly job over 2 TB might move only a few hundred MB once the baseline exists.

**Off-site, in practice.** Three common shapes:

1. A second box at a relative's house, fed over a VPN (cheapest long-term).
2. An object-storage bucket (Backblaze B2, S3) with client-side encryption.
3. A rotated external drive you physically carry off-site weekly (low-tech, very robust).

**Verification.** Both Borg and restic have a `check` command that reads the repository and re-hashes chunks. Schedule it. Pair it with an occasional real restore of a random file (see [[z=1|the rules of thumb]] on "restore from it").

The traps live one plane deeper: [[z=3|the failure modes]].

@plane z=3 label="Failure modes" x=6 y=0
# What actually bites people

**1. Silent corruption (bit rot).** A flipped bit on disk can be backed up faithfully for months before you notice. Defense: a checksumming filesystem (ZFS, Btrfs) that scrubs on a schedule and reports errors, plus keeping enough snapshots to reach back *before* the corruption.

**2. Backing up the encryption, not the data.** If ransomware encrypts your files and your nightly job dutifully copies the encrypted versions, your backups are now useless too. Defense: keep a deep retention ladder (for example 7 daily, 4 weekly, 12 monthly) and make the backup target append-only or immutable so an attacker on the source host cannot rewrite history.

**3. Untested restores.** The classic. The backup ran green for two years; the day you need it, the archive is truncated, the passphrase is lost, or the tool version that wrote it no longer exists. Defense: a quarterly fire drill that restores to a scratch directory and diffs against the source.

**4. One credential, one blast radius.** If the source server holds the only key to the off-site bucket *and* has write access to delete old versions, a single compromise can wipe everything. Defense: application keys scoped to write-and-list only, with lifecycle/immutability rules enforced on the bucket side.

**5. The 3-2-1 that is secretly 1-1-1.** Two "copies" on the same pool, plus a sync job that propagates deletions instantly to the "off-site" copy, is one copy wearing a costume. Defense: confirm each copy is independent and that at least one is *not* a live mirror.

Back to the [[z=0|one-breath answers]] when you just need the checklist.

title: Fireworks Burst
src:
---
3md: 1.0
axis: frame
title: Fireworks Burst
fps: 24
loop: false
palette: gold-on-navy
---
A single aerial shell, drawn frame by frame. Read the planes in `z` order to
watch one chrysanthemum break: the shell climbs, the charge ignites, a sphere of
gold stars expands, gravity bends the trails into a willow, and the embers fade
to smoke. Each plane is one keyframe; the `x`/`y` offsets track the burst center
as it drifts on the wind.

@plane z=0 label="Launch" x=0 y=-40
# Frame 0 - Launch

The mortar fires. A single comet of packed black powder leaves the tube and
rises on a thin orange tail. Nothing has broken yet.

- **Altitude:** climbing, roughly 1/3 of apex
- **Visible:** one bright point, a faint smoke trail below it
- **Sound:** the dull *thump* of the lift charge
- **Color:** warm orange, almost white at the head

The fuse is timed to reach the burst charge near the top of the arc. Hold this
point of light steady; everything after this is the break.

Next: the shell coasts to its peak in [[z=1|the apex]].

@plane z=1 label="Apex" x=2 y=10
# Frame 1 - Apex

The comet slows at the top of its climb. For a fraction of a second it hangs,
nearly motionless, the tail thinning to almost nothing. Wind has nudged the
center about two units to the right.

This is the quiet beat before the burst. The timing fuse has just reached the
internal charge.

- **Velocity:** near zero (the still moment)
- **Visible:** a single dim ember, no trail
- **Tension:** maximum, the audience holds its breath

> A good shell breaks at apex, where it is slowest, so the sphere stays round
> instead of smearing into a teardrop.

Jump back to [[z=0|the launch]], or forward to [[z=2|ignition]].

@plane z=2 label="Ignition" x=2 y=12
# Frame 2 - Ignition

The burst charge detonates. The shell's casing splits and the packed stars are
thrown outward in every direction at once. This is the brightest single frame of
the whole sequence.

- **Flash:** a hard white core, blown-out for one frame
- **Stars:** dozens of points launching radially outward
- **Radius:** small but growing fast
- **Sound:** arrives late, the *crack* the crowd feels in the chest

Render this frame with a short, intense highlight. The next frame is all about
the symmetric spread.

On to [[z=3|the full bloom]].

@plane z=3 label="Bloom" x=3 y=12
# Frame 3 - Full Bloom

The chrysanthemum opens. Every star traces a straight radial line from the
center, forming a near-perfect sphere of gold. This is the iconic shape, the
frame you would put on a poster.

| Property   | Value                        |
|------------|------------------------------|
| Shape      | sphere (chrysanthemum break) |
| Star count | ~64 visible trails           |
| Color      | saturated gold, even falloff |
| Radius     | maximum extent               |

Each trail is the same length, which is what makes the burst read as round. The
core has already gone dark; only the expanding shell of stars remains.

From here gravity takes over. See [[z=4|the willow droop]].

@plane z=4 label="Willow" x=4 y=4
# Frame 4 - Willow Droop

Expansion stops. The stars have spent their outward energy, and now gravity
curves every trail downward. The crisp sphere softens into the drooping
"willow" form, golden strands raining toward the ground.

- **Motion:** outward speed gone, downward speed building
- **Shape:** sphere relaxing into a hanging dome
- **Color:** gold cooling toward amber at the trail tips
- **Trails:** longer now, with visible fade from head to tail

This is the lingering, graceful part of the effect, the part that earns the
*ooh* a beat after the *crack*.

Compare with [[z=3|the bloom]], or watch it die in [[z=5|the fade]].

@plane z=5 label="Fade" x=5 y=-6
# Frame 5 - Fade to Smoke

The stars burn out one by one. The bright amber tips wink off, leaving only
faint glowing embers and a drifting cloud of smoke lit from below by the next
shell on the ground.

- **Brightness:** near zero, a few stray sparks
- **Visible:** gray-blue smoke, a couple of dying embers
- **Color:** desaturated, mostly the navy of the night sky again
- **Sound:** silence, then the lift *thump* of the next shell

The frame returns almost to where it began: a dark sky with one small point of
light. If `loop` were true, this is where it would cut back to
[[z=0|the launch]] and start over.

*End of burst.*

title: Adult CPR First Aid
src:
---
3md: 1.0
axis: step
title: Adult CPR First Aid
audience: bystander responder, no equipment
standard: 2020 AHA / ERC guidelines (compression rate 100-120/min)
disclaimer: Reference only. Take a hands-on certified course.
---
Hands-only and standard CPR for an unresponsive adult who is not breathing
normally. Move through the planes in order. If you are alone with a phone,
put it on speaker so the dispatcher can coach you while your hands stay free.

@plane z=0 x=0 y=0 label="Check the scene and the person"
# Step 0: Make sure it is safe, then check responsiveness

Before you touch anyone, look for danger to yourself: traffic, fire, live
wires, water, fumes. You cannot help if you become the second casualty.

Once the scene is safe:

1. Tap the person firmly on both shoulders.
2. Shout, "Are you okay? Can you hear me?"
3. Look at the chest for 5 to 10 seconds. Are they breathing normally?

A single gasp every few seconds is **agonal breathing**. It is not normal
breathing, and it means you should treat the person as not breathing.

- Responsive and breathing: do not start CPR. Keep them comfortable and
  monitor.
- No response and no normal breathing: continue to [[z=1|call for help]].

@plane z=1 x=2 y=0 label="Call 911 and get an AED"
# Step 1: Call emergency services and send for an AED

Time is the enemy. Brain cells start dying within minutes once blood flow
stops, so the call and the AED request happen right now, not later.

- **If others are present:** point at one specific person and say, "You, in
  the red jacket, call 911. You, find an AED and bring it here." Naming
  people prevents the bystander freeze where everyone assumes someone else
  will act.
- **If you are alone:** call 911 yourself, put the phone on speaker, and
  start compressions while you talk. Do not leave an adult to search for an
  AED unless the dispatcher tells you one is very close.

Tell the dispatcher: the exact location, that the person is unresponsive and
not breathing, and that you are starting CPR. Then go straight to
[[z=2|chest compressions]].

@plane z=2 x=4 y=0 label="Chest compressions"
# Step 2: Push hard and fast in the center of the chest

This is the single most important step. Good compressions keep oxygenated
blood moving to the brain and heart.

**Hand placement**

1. Kneel beside the person on a firm, flat surface.
2. Put the heel of one hand on the center of the chest, on the lower half of
   the breastbone (between the nipples).
3. Place your other hand on top and interlace your fingers. Keep fingers off
   the ribs.

**Technique**

- **Depth:** at least 2 inches (5 cm), no more than 2.4 inches (6 cm).
- **Rate:** 100 to 120 compressions per minute. The beat of "Stayin' Alive"
  or "Baby Shark" lands in this range.
