title: Building for the Edge - Conference Keynote Outline
src:
---
3md: 1.0
axis: slide
title: Building for the Edge - Conference Keynote Outline
event: DevSummit 2026
speaker: Mara Voss, VP Platform Engineering
duration: 40 minutes plus 10 for Q and A
---
Speaker outline for the closing keynote. Each plane is one slide. The `z` value
is the slide number, and the small `x`/`y` offsets group the three-slide demo arc
(slides 3, 4, 5) slightly to the right so a spatial viewer can see them as a set.
Read top to bottom, or jump straight to [[z=5|the live demo]].

@plane z=1 x=0 y=0 label="Title and Hook"
# Building for the Edge

**Why your next bottleneck is 3,000 miles away**

Mara Voss, VP Platform Engineering

Opening hook (90 seconds, no slides advance):

> "Last month a single config change cut our checkout latency by 280ms. We did
> not buy faster servers. We moved the work closer to the people doing it."

- Land the promise: by the end, you will know when edge compute pays off and when
  it quietly burns money.
- Set the frame: three numbers - latency, cost, and operational blast radius.
- Promise a live demo at the end (see [[z=5|the demo]]); ask folks to hold
  questions until then.

@plane z=2 x=0 y=0 label="The Problem"
# The distance tax

Every request to a central region pays for the speed of light. You cannot
optimize your way out of physics.

- A round trip from Sydney to us-east-1 is ~200ms before your code runs a line.
- Users feel anything above 100ms; above 300ms they start abandoning carts.
- We measured it: 41% of our p99 latency was pure network transit, not compute.

Three forces pushing work outward:

1. **Regulation** - data residency rules now cover 70+ jurisdictions.
2. **Cost** - egress bandwidth is the line item nobody forecasts correctly.
3. **Experience** - real-time features (typing indicators, live cursors) die at
   distance.

This is the gap the rest of the talk closes. Next we look at [[z=3|the shift]]
that makes edge practical today.

@plane z=3 x=2 y=0 label="The Shift / Demo Setup"
# What changed: the platform caught up

Edge used to mean a CDN that could only cache static files. That is no longer
true.

- Compute runtimes now boot in under 5ms at hundreds of locations.
- State is the hard part, and it finally has answers: regional read replicas,
  CRDT-backed stores, and durable per-object coordinators.
- The mental model shifts from "one big region" to "many small ones that agree
  eventually, and sometimes immediately."

**Demo setup (what you are about to see):**

- A toy order service deployed two ways - one central, one edge-first.
- Same code, two configs.
- We will watch p50 and p99 side by side from three continents.

Continue to [[z=4|the architecture]] before we run it.

@plane z=4 x=2 y=0 label="Reference Architecture"
# A pattern that actually ships

Not "rewrite everything." A seam you can adopt incrementally.

```
client -> edge function (auth, read, render)
            |
            +-- regional cache (hot reads)
            |
            +-- central core (writes, billing, audit)
```

Rules of thumb that keep teams out of trouble:

- **Reads at the edge, writes to the core.** Most traffic is reads; route them
  close.
- **Keep the source of truth singular.** Eventual consistency is a feature, not
  an accident, but money and audit logs stay central.
- **Make failure boring.** If the edge cannot reach the core, degrade to
  read-only, do not error.

Now the part you came for: [[z=5|the live demo]].

@plane z=5 x=2 y=0 label="Live Demo"
# Live demo: same code, two worlds

Switching to the terminal. If the wifi cooperates, this is the whole talk in 4
minutes.

- **Run 1 (central):** order placed from a Singapore client.
  - p50: 312ms, p99: 740ms. Audible "oof" expected from the room.
- **Run 2 (edge-first):** identical request, edge read path enabled.
  - p50: 38ms, p99: 96ms.
- **The kill switch:** I pull the core connection mid-demo. Reads keep serving
  from the regional cache; writes queue and drain when it returns.

Fallback if the demo fails: I have a recording, and I will own the irony of a
latency talk being blocked by conference wifi.

This sets up the takeaways on [[z=6|the closing slide]].

@plane z=6 x=0 y=0 label="Takeaways and Close"
# Three things to do Monday

Bring it home (2 minutes, slow down here):

1. **Measure your distance tax.** Pull p99 by region this week. You cannot fix
   what you have not split out.
2. **Find one read-heavy path.** Move just that to the edge. One seam, not a
   rewrite. Revisit [[z=4|the architecture]] for the seam.
3. **Plan the failure mode first.** Decide what "degraded" means before you ship,
   not during the incident.

Closing line:

> "The fastest request is the one that never had to cross an ocean. Go find yours."

- Thank the team and the conference organizers.
- Slides, demo source, and benchmarks: github.com/maravoss/edge-keynote.
- Open the floor for Q and A (10 minutes).

title: Simple Ribbed Beanie (Knit Flat, Seamed)
src:
---
3md: 1.0
axis: step
title: Simple Ribbed Beanie (Knit Flat, Seamed)
designer: Corvid Knitworks
yarn: Worsted weight, approx 110 yards
needles: US 8 (5.0 mm) straight
gauge: 18 sts and 24 rows to 4 inches in stockinette
size: Adult medium, 20 inch finished circumference
---
A beginner-friendly beanie worked flat in rows, then seamed up the back. Read
each step in order: every plane below is one block of rows. Abbreviations used
throughout: `k` = knit, `p` = purl, `k2tog` = knit two together, `st(s)` =
stitch(es), `RS` = right side, `WS` = wrong side.

Cast on 90 stitches loosely. Jump to [[z=1|the ribbed brim]] when ready.

@plane z=0 label="Cast On" x=0 y=0
# Step 0: Cast On

Using the long-tail cast-on, cast 90 stitches onto a single US 8 needle. Leave a
tail of about 18 inches; you will use it later to [[z=5|sew the back seam]].

- Total stitches: 90
- Tip: cast on over two needles held together if your cast-on tends to be tight.
  A loose edge keeps the brim stretchy.

This is row 0. Every odd row below is a wrong-side (WS) row and every even row is
a right-side (RS) row.

@plane z=1 label="Ribbed Brim" x=0 y=1
# Step 1: 2x2 Ribbed Brim (Rows 1 to 16)

The brim is a stretchy 2x2 rib worked over all 90 stitches for 16 rows (about 2.5
inches).

- **RS rows (1, 3, 5 ... 15):** `*k2, p2*` repeat from `*` to last 2 sts, k2.
- **WS rows (2, 4, 6 ... 16):** `*p2, k2*` repeat from `*` to last 2 sts, p2.

Knitting the knits and purling the purls as they face you keeps the columns
aligned. After row 16 the brim will fold nothing; it sits flat. Continue to
[[z=2|the stockinette body]].

@plane z=2 label="Stockinette Body" x=0 y=2
# Step 2: Stockinette Body (Rows 17 to 44)

Switch to stockinette and work straight until the piece measures about 7 inches
from the cast-on edge.

- **RS rows (odd):** knit all 90 sts.
- **WS rows (even):** purl all 90 sts.

That is 28 rows of stockinette. The fabric will want to curl at the edges; this
is normal and disappears once seamed. When the piece reaches 7 inches, stop on a
completed WS row and move to [[z=3|the crown decreases]].

@plane z=3 label="Crown Decreases" x=0 y=3
# Step 3: Crown Shaping (Rows 45 to 58)

Decrease evenly to draw the top of the hat closed. Work these on RS rows only;
purl all WS rows in between.

| Decrease row | Instruction | Sts remaining |
|---|---|---|
| Row 45 | `*k8, k2tog*` to end | 81 |
| Row 47 | `*k7, k2tog*` to end | 72 |
| Row 49 | `*k6, k2tog*` to end | 63 |
| Row 51 | `*k5, k2tog*` to end | 54 |
| Row 53 | `*k4, k2tog*` to end | 45 |
| Row 55 | `*k3, k2tog*` to end | 36 |
| Row 57 | `*k2, k2tog*` to end | 27 |

Each listed row removes 9 stitches. Purl rows 46, 48, 50, 52, 54, and 56 plain.
After row 57 you have 27 sts on the needle. Proceed to [[z=4|closing the crown]].

@plane z=4 label="Close The Crown" x=0 y=4
# Step 4: Final Decrease and Gather (Rows 58 to 59)

- **Row 58 (WS):** purl all 27 sts.
- **Row 59 (RS):** `*k1, k2tog*` to end, leaving 18 sts.

Cut the working yarn, leaving a 10 inch tail. Thread the tail through a tapestry
needle, then pass it through all 18 live stitches and slip them off the knitting
needle. Pull the tail snug to gather the crown into a tight circle, and secure it
with a small stitch on the inside. Finish at [[z=5|the seam]].

@plane z=5 label="Seam and Finish" x=0 y=5
# Step 5: Seam and Finish

You now have a flat trapezoid gathered at the top. Fold it right sides together so
the two side edges meet.

1. Using the long cast-on tail from [[z=0|the cast on]] and a tapestry needle,
   sew the back seam with mattress stitch from the brim up to the crown.
2. Weave in all remaining ends on the wrong side, working each end through at
   least 2 inches of fabric so it does not pop out.
3. Turn the hat right side out. Block lightly by misting with water and laying
   flat to dry.

Optional: fold the [[z=1|ribbed brim]] up for a double-thick cuff. Your beanie is
finished.

title: Annotated contract
src:
---
3md: 1.0
axis: layer
title: Annotated contract
---
The base layer is the source text. Higher layers are overlays a reader can
toggle independently: plain English, then legal review notes.

@plane z=0 label="source"
Section 4. The Provider shall deliver the Work Product no later than thirty (30)
days following the Effective Date.

@plane z=1 label="plain-english" x=0 y=0
You have 30 days from signing to deliver.

@plane z=2 label="review" x=0 y=0 author="counsel"
Define "deliver": on acceptance, or on submission? Flag before signing.

title: Brief in Opposition to Summary Judgment
src:
---
3md: 1.0
axis: depth
title: Brief in Opposition to Summary Judgment
court: United States District Court, Northern District of California
case: Vela v. Meridian Logistics, Inc., No. 3:24-cv-01187-WHA
party: Plaintiff Daniela Vela
counsel: Okafor & Reyes LLP
---
Read top down: the foreground plane (z=0) is the bare holding a busy judge
needs first. Each plane behind it adds depth, ending at the full doctrinal
reasoning at z=4. Drill from any layer using the cross-plane links.

@plane z=0 label="Holding" x=0 y=0
# Conclusion

Summary judgment must be DENIED. A reasonable jury could find that Meridian
classified Ms. Vela as an independent contractor while exercising the control
of an employer, so the central factual question (control over the manner and
means of her work) is genuinely disputed and cannot be resolved on the papers.

Drill down for the supporting structure: [[z=1|standard of review]],
[[z=2|the disputed facts]], [[z=3|the controlling test]], and the
[[z=4|full doctrinal analysis]].

@plane z=1 label="Standard" x=0 y=-1
# Standard of Review

Summary judgment is proper only where there is no genuine dispute as to any
material fact. Fed. R. Civ. P. 56(a). The court views the evidence in the light
most favorable to the non-moving party and draws all reasonable inferences in
her favor. Tolan v. Cotton, 572 U.S. 650, 657 (2014).

The moving party bears the initial burden. Credibility determinations and the
weighing of evidence are jury functions, not those of a judge on a Rule 56
motion. Anderson v. Liberty Lobby, Inc., 477 U.S. 242, 255 (1986).

Because the [[z=2|record facts]] are disputed on the question that
[[z=3|the governing test]] makes dispositive, the [[z=0|denial]] follows.

@plane z=2 label="Disputed Facts" x=1 y=-2
# The Material Facts Are Disputed

Drawing all inferences for Ms. Vela, the record shows:

- **Scheduling.** Meridian assigned her routes through its dispatch app and
  penalized drivers who declined more than two assignments per week (Vela Decl.
  paragraph 7; Dispatch Policy 4.2).
- **Supervision.** A route manager tracked her in real time and sent corrective
  messages about speed and rest stops (Ex. 12, message logs).
- **Equipment.** She was required to lease a Meridian-branded van and run
  company software; she could not substitute her own vehicle (Lease Agr. 1-3).
- **Integration.** Last-mile delivery is Meridian's core business, not a
  peripheral service.

Meridian's contrary evidence (a signed contractor agreement and her separate
LLC) goes to weight, not to the absence of a dispute. That conflict is exactly
what [[z=1|Rule 56]] reserves for trial, and it is the conflict the
[[z=3|Borello factors]] turn on.

@plane z=3 label="Governing Test" x=1 y=-3
# The Controlling Legal Test

California applies the Borello multi-factor test to common-law employment
status, with the right to control the manner and means of work as the
principal factor. S. G. Borello & Sons, Inc. v. Dept. of Industrial Relations,
48 Cal. 3d 341, 350 (1989).

Secondary factors include:

1. Whether the worker is engaged in a distinct occupation or business.
2. Whether the work is part of the principal's regular business.
3. Who supplies the instrumentalities and place of work.
4. The method of payment (by time versus by the job).
5. Whether the parties believe they are creating an employment relationship.

No single secondary factor is decisive; they are weighed together against the
control inquiry. Applying them to the [[z=2|disputed record]] is a jury task.
The detailed weighing appears in the [[z=4|full analysis]].

@plane z=4 label="Full Analysis" x=2 y=-4
# Full Doctrinal Analysis

**Control is the heart of the dispute.** The Borello court framed control over
the manner and means of work as the most significant consideration. 48 Cal. 3d
at 350. Meridian's real-time tracking, route assignment, and corrective
messaging (see [[z=2|the supervision facts]]) are classic indicia of an
employer's control. A jury could reasonably find Meridian directed not just the
result but the method.

**The secondary factors do not rescue the motion.** Three of the five point
toward employment on this record:

- *Regular business.* Delivery is Meridian's regular business, weighing toward
  employee status (factor 2).
- *Instrumentalities.* Meridian supplied the branded van and software (factor
  3), again favoring employment.
- *Method of payment.* Per-route pay is consistent with either status and is
  therefore neutral (factor 4).

The contractor agreement and the LLC (factor 5, the parties' belief) cut the
other way, but Borello is explicit that a label the parties adopt does not
control where the economic reality differs. Where the factors point in
different directions, the weighing belongs to the jury.

**Meridian's authorities are distinguishable.** Each granted summary judgment
on an *undisputed* record where the hiring party exercised no day-to-day
control. Here the [[z=1|Rule 56 standard]] forbids resolving the live control
dispute on the papers.

**Disposition.** For these reasons the motion should be denied. This is the
reasoning that supports the [[z=0|holding]] stated at the foreground.

title: Why Does the Moon Change Shape?
src:
---
3md: 1.0
axis: slide
title: Why Does the Moon Change Shape?
subject: Grade 5 Earth Science
duration: 50 minutes
standard: NGSS 5-ESS1-2
teacher: Ms. Okafor
---
Lesson plan for a single 50-minute class period. Each plane is one slide in the deck,
projected in order. The Hook ([[z=0|slide 1]]) sets up a question the Wrap-Up
([[z=5|slide 6]]) answers, and the model on [[z=2]] is the anchor for the whole lesson.

@plane z=0 x=0 y=0 label="Hook"
# Why Does the Moon Change Shape?

**Bell-ringer (5 min).** Slide shows four photos of the Moon taken on different nights:
a thin crescent, a half Moon, a gibbous Moon, and a full Moon.

Prompt on the board:
> "These are all the same Moon, photographed in one month. What is going on? Write one
> guess in your notebook before we talk."

Teacher moves: stay quiet for the first 60 seconds, then take three hands. Do not
correct wrong guesses yet, just record them on chart paper. Common ones to expect:
- "Clouds cover part of it."
- "The Earth's shadow eats it."
- "It actually shrinks and grows."

We will come back to these exact guesses in the [[z=5|Wrap-Up]].

@plane z=1 x=1 y=0 label="Prior Knowledge"
# What We Already Know

**Quick review (5 min).** Anchor today's lesson on what students locked in last week.

Cold-call review questions:
1. Where does the Moon's light come from? (The Sun. The Moon does not glow on its own.)
2. How long does the Moon take to orbit the Earth once? (About one month.)
3. Does the Moon spin? (Yes, once per orbit, so we always see the same face.)

Write the key sentence on the board and have the class read it aloud:

> "The Moon does not make light. It reflects sunlight, and we only see the lit part
> that faces us."

That single idea is the lever for the [[z=2|model demo]]. Half the Moon is always lit;
the question is how much of the lit half points toward Earth.

@plane z=2 x=2 y=0 label="Model Demo"
# Modeling the Phases

**Hands-on demonstration (15 min).** This is the heart of the lesson.

Materials per group of four:
- 1 polystyrene ball on a pencil (the Moon)
- 1 desk lamp with the shade removed (the Sun)
- The student's own head (the Earth)

Procedure:
1. One student holds the ball at arm's length and slowly turns in a full circle while
   the lamp stays fixed.
2. The rest of the group watches how much of the lit side of the ball they can see as
   it travels around.
3. Each student records the shape they see at four stations: lamp behind them, ball to
   the left, lamp behind the ball, ball to the right.

Safety note: lamps get hot, no touching the bulb, cords taped to the floor.

What they should notice: the lit half never changes, but our *view* of it does. When
the ball is between the student and the lamp, we see the dark side (new Moon). Connect
this straight back to the [[z=1|key sentence]] from review.

@plane z=3 x=3 y=0 label="Guided Practice"
# Naming the Phases

**Guided practice (10 min).** Now attach vocabulary to what they just observed.

Project a phase diagram and fill it in together. Students label a printed handout as
the class goes:

| Position of Moon | What we see | Name |
| --- | --- | --- |
| Between Sun and Earth | Dark face | New Moon |
| Quarter way around | Right half lit | First Quarter |
| Opposite the Sun | Full face lit | Full Moon |
| Three-quarters around | Left half lit | Third Quarter |

Teach "waxing" (getting bigger, right side lit) versus "waning" (getting smaller,
left side lit) with a hand gesture: right hand cups a growing Moon, left hand cups a
shrinking one.

Check for understanding: hold up the foam ball at a new position and ask the class to
name the phase with a thumbs signal before anyone speaks.

@plane z=4 x=4 y=0 label="Independent Work"
# Your Turn: Moon Journal

**Independent practice (8 min in class, then homework).**

In class: each student sketches the four main phases in order and writes one sentence
explaining why the new Moon looks dark, using the words "lit" and "facing."

Homework (the Moon Journal):
- [ ] Look for the Moon at the same time each night for the next week.
- [ ] Draw what you see, even if it is hidden by clouds (write "cloudy").
- [ ] Note whether it is waxing or waning.

Differentiation:
- **Support:** sentence starter provided ("The new Moon looks dark because...").
- **Stretch:** predict the phase exactly seven nights from tonight and explain.

Rubric lives on the back of the handout: 2 points for ordered phases, 2 for the
explanation, 1 for correct waxing/waning labels.

@plane z=5 x=5 y=0 label="Wrap-Up"
# So, Why Does the Moon Change Shape?

**Closure (2 min).** Return to the chart paper from the [[z=0|Hook]].

Read each original guess aloud and have the class vote thumbs-up or thumbs-down now
that they have the model:
- "Clouds cover part of it." (No, the shape is sharp and curved, not patchy.)
- "The Earth's shadow eats it." (No, that is a lunar eclipse, which is rare.)
- "It shrinks and grows." (No, only our view of the lit half changes.)

Exit ticket (one index card):
> "In one sentence, tell me why the Moon looks different shapes during the month."

Collect cards at the door. Tomorrow's lesson on eclipses builds directly on the model
from [[z=2|the demo]], so flag any exit tickets that still mention Earth's shadow.

title: Maplewood Central Library - Floor Guide
src:
---
3md: 1.0
axis: floor
title: Maplewood Central Library - Floor Guide
building: Maplewood Central Library
address: 412 Birchwood Avenue
hours: Mon-Thu 9am-9pm, Fri-Sat 9am-6pm, Sun 12pm-5pm
elevators: 2 (north lobby, south stair core)
---
Welcome to Maplewood Central Library. This guide stacks the building floor by
floor, lowest to highest. Each floor lists its collections, service desks, and
quiet ratings. Use the elevators in the north lobby or take the south stair
core. Restrooms are on every floor near the elevators.

@plane z=-1 x=0 y=0 label="Lower Level - Archives and Parking"
# Lower Level (B1)

The lower level holds the climate-controlled archives and the staff workroom.
Patron access is by appointment at the [[z=0|Welcome Desk]] on the ground floor.

## Collections
- Local history archives (1851 to present)
- Microfilm: regional newspapers, census records
- Map and atlas vault (oversized, flat storage)

## Services
- Archivist office, room B1-04 (request items 24 hours ahead)
- Two microfilm reader stations
- Underground parking entrance (40 spaces, accessible bays nearest the elevator)

## Quiet rating
Silent. No phone calls. Gloves provided for handling original materials.

@plane z=0 x=0 y=0 label="Ground Floor - Welcome and Popular Reads"
# Ground Floor

The front entrance, the busiest service point, and the popular collections. Start
here, then ride up to [[z=1|Adult Nonfiction]] or down to [[z=-1|the Archives]].

## Service desks
- Welcome Desk (left of the main doors): library cards, directions, lost and found
- Holds pickup shelves (self-service, alphabetized by last name)
- Self-checkout kiosks (four units, card or app)

## Collections
- New and popular fiction
- Large print and audiobooks
- DVDs, board games, and the Library of Things (tools, kits, ukuleles)
- Daily newspapers and current magazines

## Amenities
- Cafe cart (coffee, tea, pastries) open until 4pm
- Friends of the Library used-book nook

## Quiet rating
Lively. Conversation welcome. Phone calls fine near the entrance.

@plane z=1 x=0 y=0 label="Second Floor - Adult Nonfiction and Reference"
# Second Floor

Nonfiction, reference, and the reading room. Quieter than [[z=0|the ground floor]];
for group work head up to [[z=2|the Learning Commons]].

## Collections
- Nonfiction (Dewey 000 to 999), shelved north to south
- Reference (in-library use only)
- Genealogy and government documents

## Services
- Reference Desk (center of the floor): research help, interlibrary loan
- Two scan-to-email stations and a black-and-white printer
- The Reading Room: 30 individual carrels with task lighting

## Quiet rating
Quiet study. Headphones required for audio. Calls only in the stair landing.

@plane z=2 x=0 y=0 label="Third Floor - Youth and Learning Commons"
# Third Floor

The children's and teen wings, plus collaborative spaces. The noisiest upper
floor by design. For silence, return to [[z=1|the Reading Room]].

## Children's wing (west)
- Picture books and early readers
- Story-time alcove (sessions Tue and Thu, 10:30am)
- Play-and-learn area with low shelving and bean bags

## Teen wing (east)
- Young adult fiction and graphic novels
- Gaming consoles (booking required at the desk)
- Homework help table (volunteer tutors, weekday afternoons)

## Learning Commons (center)
- Six bookable group study rooms (seats 4 to 8, reserve online)
- Maker bench: 3D printer, vinyl cutter, sewing machine
- Open laptop bar with 20 charging seats

## Quiet rating
Active. Talking and collaboration encouraged. Maker tools run during open hours.

@plane z=3 x=0 y=0 label="Fourth Floor - Rooftop Reading Terrace"
# Fourth Floor

The top floor opens onto the rooftop terrace, the calmest spot for reading with
a view. Bring a book up from any floor; food from the [[z=0|ground-floor cafe]] is
welcome on the terrace.

## Spaces
- Glass-walled quiet lounge with armchairs
- Covered outdoor terrace (open Apr through Oct, weather permitting)
- Community meeting room (seats 60, booked through the Welcome Desk)

## Amenities
- Water bottle filling station
- Accessible terrace ramp and shaded seating

## Quiet rating
Restful. Low conversation indoors, quiet enjoyment on the terrace.

title: Intro to Film Photography
src:
---
3md: 1.0
axis: time
title: Intro to Film Photography
course_code: PHOT 101
meeting: Tuesdays 18:00 to 20:30
instructor: J. Okafor
weeks: "6"
---
A six-week course syllabus. The axis is time: each plane is one week of class,
scrub the Z axis to move through the term. Every plane lists the topic, the
in-class work, the assignment due, and what to bring. Camera required by week 1; a
35mm SLR loaner is available on request.

@plane z=0 label="Week 1 - The Camera"
# Week 1: The Camera and the Frame
Topic: how a film SLR works. Aperture, shutter, ISO, and the exposure triangle.

- In class: load a roll, dry-fire metering drills, composition walk outside
- Assignment due: none
- Bring: your camera, one roll of ISO 400 black and white film
- Read: handout on the exposure triangle

The shooting habits you build now pay off when we develop in [[z=2|week 3]].

@plane z=1 label="Week 2 - Light and Exposure"
# Week 2: Reading Light
Topic: metering modes, the zone system basics, exposure compensation.

- In class: bracket the same scene across five stops, predict the results
- Assignment due: shoot a full roll exploring hard versus soft light
- Bring: your exposed roll from this week, light meter app on your phone
- Read: chapter 2, "Previsualization"

@plane z=2 label="Week 3 - Developing"
# Week 3: Developing Film
Topic: the darkroom chemistry workflow, developer, stop, fix, wash.

- In class: develop the roll you shot in [[z=1|week 2]], step by step
- Assignment due: bring two exposed rolls ready to develop
- Bring: notebook, an old shirt, your exposed rolls
- Safety: closed-toe shoes required in the darkroom

@plane z=3 label="Week 4 - Printing"
# Week 4: The Enlarger
Topic: contact sheets, test strips, dodging and burning.

- In class: make a contact sheet, then one fine 8x10 print
- Assignment due: a contact sheet from your best roll so far
- Bring: your developed negatives in sleeves
- Read: chapter 4, "From Negative to Print"

@plane z=4 label="Week 5 - The Project"
# Week 5: Studio and Critique Prep
Topic: building a cohesive series, sequencing, and self-editing.

- In class: edit your negatives down to a six-image series, peer feedback
- Assignment due: a working edit of 12 candidate prints
- Bring: all prints made so far, pushpins
- Note: final series due next week

@plane z=5 label="Week 6 - Final Critique"
# Week 6: Final Critique
Topic: present and defend your finished series.

- In class: gallery walk and group critique of every student's final six prints
- Assignment due: final mounted series of six prints plus a one-paragraph statement
- Bring: your matted prints ready to hang
- Looking back: the strongest work usually traces to habits set in [[z=0|week 1]].

title: Backyard Vegetable Garden Plan
src:
---
3md: 1.0
axis: time
title: Backyard Vegetable Garden Plan
zone: USDA 7b
plot: 4 raised beds, 4ft by 8ft each
sun: full sun, 8 hours
---
A season-by-season plan for a four-bed raised garden. The axis is time: each plane
is a season, scrub the Z axis to walk through the gardening year. Bed numbers (B1
to B4) are consistent across seasons so you can practice crop rotation. The layout
below shows the beds.

```
   [ B1 ]   [ B2 ]
   [ B3 ]   [ B4 ]
   (path runs north-south down the middle)
```

@plane z=0 label="Early Spring"
# Early Spring (March to April)
Cool weather. Last frost expected mid-April; protect tender starts.

- B1: peas (sugar snap), spinach, radishes
- B2: lettuce mix, arugula, scallions
- B3: prep and amend with compost, plant garlic if not already in
- B4: cover crop (crimson clover) turned under late April

Tasks: build pea trellis, start tomato and pepper seeds indoors. The starts you
sow now go into the ground in [[z=1|summer]].

@plane z=1 label="Summer"
# Summer (May to August)
Peak growth. Water deeply in the early morning.

- B1: tomatoes (Sungold, San Marzano) on stakes
- B2: peppers and basil, cucumbers on the trellis from spring
- B3: bush beans, zucchini, marigolds for pest control
- B4: sweet corn block, with squash at the base

Tasks: mulch heavily, side-dress tomatoes with compost mid-June, harvest beans
every other day. Plan the fall sowings on [[z=2|autumn]] before the corn finishes.

@plane z=2 label="Autumn"
# Autumn (September to October)
Cooling off. Second cool-season crop and cleanup.

- B1: clear tomatoes, sow garlic for next year
- B2: fall lettuce, kale, Swiss chard
- B3: turnips, beets, more radishes
- B4: broccoli and cauliflower transplants

Tasks: pull spent summer plants, top up compost, sow cover crop where beds are
empty.

@plane z=3 label="Winter"
# Winter (November to February)
Rest and plan. Most beds dormant.

- B1: garlic overwintering under straw mulch
- B2: kale and chard under a low tunnel, harvest as needed
- B3: bare, cover-cropped, resting the soil
- B4: bare, sheet-mulched with leaves

Tasks: order seeds, sharpen and oil tools, sketch next year's rotation so heavy
feeders move off [[z=1|the summer tomato bed]]. The cycle restarts at
[[z=0|early spring]].

title: Maple Street House Tour
src:
---
3md: 1.0
axis: floor
title: Maple Street House Tour
listing: "412 Maple Street"
type: 3-bed Craftsman
square_feet: "2150"
year_built: "1924"
---
A floor-by-floor walkthrough of a house for sale. The axis is floor: each plane is
a level of the building, from the basement up to the attic. Scrub the Z axis to
climb the house. Negative z is below grade. Use this as an open-house handout or a
listing supplement.

@plane z=-1 label="Basement"
# Basement (below grade)
Partially finished, dry, good ceiling height.

- Laundry room with washer and dryer included
- Utility area: gas furnace (2019), 50-gallon water heater (2021)
- Finished rec room with carpet, 280 sq ft
- Storage room with built-in shelving
- One small egress window

Mechanicals are all here. The living spaces start one flight up on [[z=0|the main floor]].

@plane z=0 label="Main Floor"
# Main Floor
The heart of the house. Original oak floors throughout.

- Front entry with coat closet and the original leaded-glass door
- Living room with a working wood-burning fireplace
- Formal dining room with built-in china cabinet
- Kitchen: quartz counters, gas range, eat-in nook
- Half bath off the hallway
- Back mudroom to the yard

Stairs at the front hall lead up to [[z=1|the bedrooms]].

@plane z=1 label="Second Floor"
# Second Floor
All three bedrooms and the full baths.

- Primary bedroom, 16 by 14, with two closets
- Primary ensuite: walk-in shower, double vanity
- Bedroom 2, 12 by 11, currently an office
- Bedroom 3, 11 by 10, with the original built-in window seat
- Hall bath with a clawfoot tub
- Linen closet

@plane z=2 label="Attic"
# Attic (top level)
Finished bonus space under the gable.

- Open flex room, roughly 350 usable sq ft with sloped ceilings
- Currently a playroom and reading loft
- Two dormer windows, lots of light
- Insulated and on its own mini-split for heating and cooling
- Plenty of eave storage behind knee-wall doors

Could be a fourth bedroom or studio. Note the dry, conditioned storage that
complements [[z=-1|the basement storage]] below.

title: Five Days in Kyoto
src:
---
3md: 1.0
axis: time
title: Five Days in Kyoto
travelers: "2"
season: autumn
budget_per_day: "12000 yen"
---
A day-by-day trip plan for Kyoto in mid-November. Each plane is one day; scrub the
Z axis to move forward and backward through the trip. The arrival day is z=0 and
the departure day is z=4. Hotel is the Hotel Kanra near Kyoto Station for all
nights, so every day starts and ends there.

@plane z=0 label="Day 1 - Arrival and Higashiyama"
# Day 1: Arrival and Higashiyama
Land at Kansai (KIX), take the Haruka express to Kyoto Station (75 min).

- 14:00 Check in at Hotel Kanra, drop bags
- 15:30 Walk Kiyomizu-dera temple before it closes (last entry 17:30)
- 17:00 Stroll the slopes of Sannenzaka and Ninenzaka
- 19:00 Dinner: ramen at Honke Daiichiasahi near the station

Light first day to shake off the flight. See [[z=4|the departure plan]] now so
you book the airport train in advance.

@plane z=1 label="Day 2 - Arashiyama"
# Day 2: Arashiyama
Take the JR Sagano line from Kyoto Station to Saga-Arashiyama (15 min).

- 08:30 Bamboo Grove early, before the crowds
- 09:30 Okochi Sanso garden and matcha
- 11:00 Tenryu-ji temple and its pond garden
- 12:30 Lunch: yudofu (hot tofu) at Shoraian by the river
- 14:00 Iwatayama Monkey Park hike
- 16:00 Cross the Togetsukyo bridge, coffee at Arabica Kyoto

@plane z=2 label="Day 3 - Fushimi and Gion"
# Day 3: Fushimi Inari and Gion
Big walking day. Comfortable shoes.

- 07:30 Fushimi Inari shrine, hike the full torii gate trail to the summit
- 11:00 Return, lunch: kitsune udon near the shrine entrance
- 13:30 Nishiki Market for snacks and knives
- 16:00 Rest at the hotel
- 18:00 Gion district at dusk, watch for geiko along Hanamikoji
- 19:30 Dinner: kaiseki at Gion Karyo (reserve ahead)

@plane z=3 label="Day 4 - Temples and tea"
# Day 4: Golden Pavilion and tea
Bus day. Buy an all-day bus pass at the station kiosk.

- 09:00 Kinkaku-ji, the Golden Pavilion
- 10:30 Ryoan-ji rock garden
- 12:00 Lunch near Ryoan-ji
- 14:00 Ginkaku-ji, the Silver Pavilion
- 15:00 Walk the Philosopher's Path
- 17:00 Tea ceremony at Camellia in Higashiyama

@plane z=4 label="Day 5 - Departure"
# Day 5: Departure
Checkout is 11:00. Allow 90 minutes to the airport.

- 08:30 Last breakfast at the hotel cafe
- 09:30 Souvenir run at Kyoto Station's Isetan
- 10:45 Checkout, store bags if needed
- 12:00 Haruka express back to KIX
- 15:00 Flight home

If you skipped anything, the easy wins were on [[z=0]] in Higashiyama.

title: Household Budget Q1
src:
---
3md: 1.0

currency: USD
household: "2 adults"

A monthly household budget for the first quarter. Each plane is one month; scrub
the Z axis to compare months. Income is steady at 6,200 take-home; the goal is to
keep total spending under 4,960 so 20 percent goes to savings. Each plane closes
with a quick verdict.

@plane z=0 label="January"
# January
Income: 6,200

| Category        | Budget | Actual |
| --------------- | ------ | ------ |
| Rent            | 1,950  | 1,950  |
| Groceries       | 650    | 712    |
| Utilities       | 280    | 305    |
| Transport       | 220    | 198    |
| Dining out      | 300    | 384    |
| Subscriptions   | 95     | 95     |
| Misc            | 200    | 240    |
| Savings         | 1,240  | 1,016  |

Verdict: holiday hangover. Dining out and groceries ran hot, savings missed by
224. Tighten dining in [[z=1|February]].

@plane z=1 label="February"
# February
Income: 6,200

| Category        | Budget | Actual |
| --------------- | ------ | ------ |
| Rent            | 1,950  | 1,950  |
| Groceries       | 650    | 631    |
| Utilities       | 280    | 290    |
| Transport       | 220    | 205    |
| Dining out      | 300    | 268    |
| Subscriptions   | 95     | 80     |
| Misc            | 200    | 150    |
| Savings         | 1,240  | 1,426  |

Verdict: back on track. Cancelled one streaming service and cooked at home. Beat
the savings target by 186. Carry the momentum into [[z=2|March]].

@plane z=2 label="March"
# March
Income: 6,200

| Category        | Budget | Actual |
| --------------- | ------ | ------ |
| Rent            | 1,950  | 1,950  |
| Groceries       | 650    | 668    |
| Utilities       | 280    | 240    |
| Transport       | 220    | 230    |
| Dining out      | 300    | 295    |
| Subscriptions   | 80     | 80     |
| Misc            | 200    | 310    |
| Savings         | 1,240  | 1,227  |

Verdict: nearly on target. A car repair pushed Misc up, but lower heating helped.
Savings within 13 of goal.

@plane z=3 label="Q1 Summary"
# Q1 Summary
Three-month roll-up.

| Metric                | Amount  |
| --------------------- | ------- |
| Total income          | 18,600  |
| Total spending        | 14,931  |
| Total saved           | 3,669   |
| Savings rate          | 19.7%   |

Just shy of the 20 percent goal, dragged down by [[z=0|January]]. Action for next
quarter: cap dining out at 280 and build a 200/month repair buffer so a single
[[z=2|car repair]] does not blow the Misc line.

title: The Lighthouse Letter
src:
---
3md: 1.0
axis: scene
title: The Lighthouse Letter
genre: literary short fiction
pov: third person, close
word_count: "approx 900"
---
A short story told in scenes. The axis is scene: each plane is one scene of the
narrative, and the story is meant to be read straight through from z=0. Cross-plane
links mark the callbacks and foreshadowing, so a reader can jump between the seed
of an image and its payoff. The non-linear links are intentional craft, not
navigation chrome.

@plane z=0 label="The Arrival"
# Scene 1: The Arrival
Mara reached the island on the last ferry of October, the only passenger. The
lighthouse stood white against a bruised sky, and the keeper's cottage crouched
beside it like an afterthought. Her aunt had left her the place in a will written
in a hand Mara did not recognize.

On the kitchen table sat a single envelope, unopened, addressed to no one. She did
not touch it. Not yet. She would learn what it held in [[z=3|the reading]].

@plane z=1 label="The First Night"
# Scene 2: The First Night
The lamp upstairs still turned. Someone had kept it running. Mara climbed the
spiral stairs and found the lens immaculate, the brass polished, a logbook open to
yesterday's date though the cottage had supposedly stood empty for a year.

The last entry read only: "She is coming. Keep the light." Mara closed the book
and told herself it meant nothing.

@plane z=2 label="The Visitor"
# Scene 3: The Visitor
By morning a man waited at the door, oilskin dripping, a fisherman from the
mainland named Ezra. He had rowed across to warn her. "Your aunt walked into the
water," he said, "the night the letter came back unopened." He looked past her to
the table. "Is that it? The one she could never send?"

Mara realized he meant the envelope she had seen on [[z=0|the night she arrived]].

@plane z=3 label="The Reading"
# Scene 4: The Reading
Alone again, Mara opened it. The letter was her aunt's confession: forty years
ago she had let a ship founder on the rocks rather than relight a lamp she had let
go dark out of grief. The envelope had been addressed, stamped, and never sent,
returned to her own hands because she could not name a soul to forgive her.

The ink of the final line matched the logbook upstairs from [[z=1|the first night]]:
"Keep the light."

@plane z=4 label="The Light"
# Scene 5: The Light
That evening Mara climbed the stairs and did not let the lamp falter. The beam
swept the black water, steady, mile after mile. Ezra's boat was a small bright
splinter heading safely home.

She understood now what [[z=2|the fisherman]] had carried across the strait, and
why her aunt had stayed. Some debts you do not pay off. You keep them turning,
night after night, so no one else goes down in the dark.

title: Wildflower Road - Song Chart
src:
---
3md: 1.0
axis: section
title: Wildflower Road - Song Chart
key: G major
tempo: "92 bpm"
time_signature: "4/4"
capo: "2"
---
A full song chart laid out by section. Each plane is one section of the
arrangement; scrub the Z axis to walk through the form from intro to outro. Chords
are given relative to the capo (capo 2, so shapes in G sound in A). Strum pattern
unless noted: D DU UDU.

@plane z=0 label="Intro"
# Intro (4 bars)
Fingerpicked, no vocals. Sets the mood.

```
| G   | Cadd9 | G   | D/F# |
```

Let the last D/F# ring, then the band comes in on [[z=1|the first verse]].

@plane z=1 label="Verse 1"
# Verse 1 (8 bars)
Full strum enters here.

```
| G        | Cadd9    | G        | D        |
| Em       | C        | G    D   | G        |
```

Lyric: "Down the wildflower road where the gravel turns to gold..."

@plane z=2 label="Pre-Chorus"
# Pre-Chorus (4 bars)
Build. Hold back the strum, palm-muted eighths.

```
| Em       | C        | G        | D        |
```

Crescendo into the downbeat of [[z=3|the chorus]].

@plane z=3 label="Chorus"
# Chorus (8 bars)
Open up, full voicings, harmonies on the title line.

```
| C        | G        | D        | Em       |
| C        | G        | D        | G        |
```

Lyric hook: "And I'll carry you home, carry you home."

@plane z=4 label="Verse 2"
# Verse 2 (8 bars)
Same chords as [[z=1|Verse 1]]. Add a tambourine on beats 2 and 4.

```
| G        | Cadd9    | G        | D        |
| Em       | C        | G    D   | G        |
```

@plane z=5 label="Bridge"
# Bridge (6 bars)
New harmonic color. Drop to a single guitar and voice for the first two bars.

```
| Am       | Em       | C        | D        |
| Am       | D        |
```

Big ritardando on the last D, then crash back into the final chorus.

@plane z=6 label="Final Chorus"
# Final Chorus (8 bars)
Repeat [[z=3|the chorus]] twice, second time up a step in energy. Everyone sings.

@plane z=7 label="Outro"
# Outro (4 bars)
Wind down to the intro figure, fingerpicked again.

```
| G   | Cadd9 | G   | G |
```

Final chord: let G ring and fade.

title: Spanish Kitchen Verbs Deck
src:
---
3md: 1.0
axis: layer
title: Spanish Kitchen Verbs Deck
level: A2
deck_size: "6 cards"
direction: en to es
---
A flashcard deck for kitchen and cooking verbs. The axis is layer: each plane is a
layer of the same study card, not a separate card. A study tool reveals layers in
order so you can self-test. Layer z=0 is the prompt (English), z=1 is the target
word, z=2 is pronunciation, z=3 is the meaning and an example, z=4 is conjugation
help, and z=5 is a mnemonic. Cards are separated by horizontal rules within each
layer so all six cards stay aligned across layers.

@plane z=0 label="Prompt (English)"
# Layer 0: Prompt
Cover everything below and recall the Spanish.

1. to chop
---
2. to boil
---
3. to fry
---
4. to bake
---
5. to stir
---
6. to taste

@plane z=1 label="Target word (Spanish)"
# Layer 1: Target word

1. picar
---
2. hervir
---
3. freir
---
4. hornear
---
5. remover
---
6. probar

Stuck on the sound of any of these? Reveal [[z=2|pronunciation]].

@plane z=2 label="Pronunciation"
# Layer 2: Pronunciation (approximate)

1. pee-KAR
---
2. air-VEER
---
3. fray-EER
---
4. or-neh-AR
---
5. reh-mo-VAIR
---
6. pro-BAR

@plane z=3 label="Meaning and example"
# Layer 3: Meaning and example

1. picar: to chop or mince. "Voy a picar la cebolla." (I am going to chop the onion.)
---
2. hervir: to boil. "El agua ya hierve." (The water is already boiling.)
---
3. freir: to fry. "Frio los huevos en aceite." (I fry the eggs in oil.)
---
4. hornear: to bake. "Horneo el pan 40 minutos." (I bake the bread 40 minutes.)
---
5. remover: to stir. "Remueve la salsa sin parar." (Stir the sauce without stopping.)
---
6. probar: to taste or try. "Prueba la sopa, por favor." (Taste the soup, please.)

@plane z=4 label="Conjugation help"
# Layer 4: Conjugation (present, yo form)

1. picar -> yo pico (regular -ar)
---
2. hervir -> yo hiervo (e to ie stem change)
---
3. freir -> yo frio (irregular, accent on the i)
---
4. hornear -> yo horneo (regular -ar)
---
5. remover -> yo remuevo (o to ue stem change)
---
6. probar -> yo pruebo (o to ue stem change)

@plane z=5 label="Mnemonic"
# Layer 5: Mnemonic

1. picar sounds like "pick at" the vegetables.
---
2. hervir: "her veer" toward the boiling pot.
---
3. freir: "fry" hides right inside fre-IR.
---
4. hornear: a "horn" oven, a horno, where you bake.
---
5. remover: you "re-move" the spoon around and around.
---
6. probar: to "probe" the soup is to taste it.

If you got the word but missed the conjugation, drill [[z=4|the yo forms]] again.

title: Downtown Transit Map
src:
---
3md: 1.0
axis: space
title: Downtown Transit Map
city: Portland
line: MAX Blue Line
note: x and y are abstract grid coordinates, not real distances
---
A schematic transit map. Each plane is a stop placed by x/y on a shared grid, so a
spatial viewer can lay them out as a route diagram. The axis is space: z just
orders the stops west to east along the line. Use the overview grid below to see
the whole route, then scrub through individual stops for details.

```
   y
 5 |        Library(2)
 4 |  Civic(0)
 3 |          Pioneer(3)----Mall(4)
 2 |   Goose(1)
 1 |                              Convention(5)
   +-------------------------------------------- x
     1    2    3    4    5    6    7    8
```

@plane z=0 x=2 y=4 label="Civic Center West"
# Civic Center West
Western terminus of the downtown segment.

- Transfers: Streetcar A Loop
- Nearby: Providence Park stadium, food carts on SW 10th
- Accessible: yes, level boarding
- Bike share dock: 12 slots

Next stop east is [[z=1|Goose Hollow]].

@plane z=1 x=3 y=2 label="Goose Hollow"
# Goose Hollow
Quiet residential stop at the base of the hill.

- Transfers: Bus 15
- Nearby: Lincoln High School, Tanner Springs Park
- Accessible: yes
- Park and ride: no

@plane z=2 x=4 y=5 label="Central Library"
# Central Library
Busy midday stop.

- Transfers: Bus 20, Bus 12
- Nearby: Multnomah County Central Library, Director Park
- Accessible: yes, elevator to platform
- Ticket machine: 2 on platform

@plane z=3 x=5 y=3 label="Pioneer Square"
# Pioneer Square
The hub. Highest ridership on the line.

- Transfers: MAX Red, Green, and Yellow lines
- Nearby: Pioneer Courthouse Square, shopping core
- Accessible: yes
- Staffed info booth: weekdays 8 to 18

This is the interchange. Eastbound continues to [[z=4|Mall stop]]; for the western
end go back to [[z=0|Civic Center]].

@plane z=4 x=7 y=3 label="Mall and 5th"
# Mall and 5th
Transit mall stop with frequent buses.

- Transfers: Bus 1, Bus 9, Bus 17
- Nearby: Pioneer Place mall, Apple Store
- Accessible: yes
- Real-time arrivals sign: yes

@plane z=5 x=8 y=1 label="Convention Center"
# Convention Center
Eastern terminus of the downtown segment, across the river.

- Transfers: Bus 6
- Nearby: Oregon Convention Center, Rose Quarter
- Accessible: yes
- Park and ride: 400 spaces

title: Ship It Before You Read It: Trust Your Test Suite
src:
---
3md: 1.0
axis: slide
title: "Ship It Before You Read It: Trust Your Test Suite"
event: "DevConf Lightning Talks"
duration: "5 minutes"
speaker: "Robin Vasquez"
---
A five-slide, five-minute lightning talk. Budget roughly one minute per slide.
Read the room, not the notes. If you only have time for one slide, make it
[[z=3|the demo]].

@plane z=0 x=0 y=0 label="Title"
# Ship It Before You Read It

### Trust Your Test Suite

Robin Vasquez, Platform Team

A 5-minute story about how we went from "merge on a Friday and pray" to
"deploy 40 times a day and forget we did."

> Timer starts now. Five slides, one minute each. No live coding, I promise.

Next up: [[z=1|why we were scared to ship]].

@plane z=1 x=2 y=0 label="The Problem"
# The 4 PM Friday Problem

Every deploy used to mean a human reading the diff line by line, then crossing
fingers. The numbers told the story:

- **22 minutes** average code review, most of it re-reading the same files
- **1 in 6** deploys rolled back within the hour
- **0** people willing to ship after lunch on a Friday

The bottleneck was not the code. It was our **lack of trust** in the code.

Reading harder did not help. We needed to read less and verify more. See
[[z=2|the one change we made]].

@plane z=2 x=4 y=0 label="The Idea"
# One Rule: The Suite Is the Reviewer

We made a single bet: if a change passes the suite, it is safe to ship. No
human gate. That forced three habits:

1. **Every bug fix ships with the test that would have caught it.** No test,
   no merge.
2. **Tests run in under 90 seconds** or they get split. Slow suites get
   skipped, and a skipped suite is a lie.
3. **Flaky tests are deleted, not muted.** A test you cannot trust is worse
   than no test.

The pitch to the team: stop reading every line, start trusting the line that
already failed once. Now watch it work in [[z=3|the demo]].

@plane z=3 x=6 y=0 label="The Demo"
# 40 Seconds, Start to Production

Here is the actual pipeline from a real Tuesday:

```
$ git commit -m "Fix: refund rounds half-cent down"
$ git push
  -> 312 tests, 38s, green
  -> deploy to prod: 9s
  -> live
```

The refund bug had bitten us twice before. This time the **failing test came
first**, the fix made it pass, and nobody read the diff. It shipped in under a
minute and stayed shipped.

That is the whole trick. The takeaways are on [[z=4|the last slide]].

@plane z=4 x=8 y=0 label="Takeaways"
# Three Things to Steal

1. **Write the failing test before the fix.** It proves the bug and guards the
   future in one move.
2. **Keep the suite fast and honest.** Under 90 seconds, zero flakes, or people
   stop believing it.
3. **Let green mean go.** Trust is a feature you build, then ship on.

We went from 6 deploys a week to 40 a day, and Friday afternoons are boring
again. Boring is the goal.

Thanks. Find the runbook at example.com/ship-it, and jump back to
[[z=0|the start]] if you want the one-line version.

title: ASCII Loading Spinner
src:
---
3md: 1.0
axis: frame
title: ASCII Loading Spinner
fps: 10
loop: true
glyphs: "| / - \\"
artist: CorvidLabs
---
A four-frame braille-free spinner built from the classic terminal glyph set
`| / - \`. Each plane is one cel. Play them in order (0, 1, 2, 3) and loop back
to [[z=0|frame 0]] to keep the bar spinning. Render in place by emitting a
carriage return (`\r`) before each frame so every cel overwrites the last on the
same line, which is what turns four static characters into one rotating spoke.

At 10 fps the spoke makes a full half-turn every 200 ms and reads as smooth
motion. Drop to 4 fps for a deliberate, chunky "still working" feel; push to 20
fps and it blurs into a near-solid wheel.

@plane z=0 x=0 y=0 label="Vertical"
# Frame 0 - Vertical (|)

The spoke stands straight up, the rest pose every loop returns to. Print a
prompt prefix, then this glyph, then hold for one frame interval.

```
Loading |
```

- Glyph: `|` (pipe, U+007C)
- Angle: 90 degrees, pointing straight up and down
- Role: the anchor frame; comparison frames are read against this one

Rotate one eighth of a turn clockwise to reach [[z=1|frame 1, the forward
slash]].

@plane z=1 x=2 y=0 label="Forward slash"
# Frame 1 - Forward slash (/)

The spoke tips clockwise off vertical. To the eye the top has swung to the right
and the bottom to the left, the first hint of rotation after the rest pose.

```
Loading /
```

- Glyph: `/` (solidus, U+002F)
- Angle: 45 degrees
- Role: the "it moved" frame; without it frame 0 to frame 2 looks like a jump

Keep turning clockwise to lay the spoke flat at [[z=2|frame 2, the dash]].

@plane z=2 x=4 y=0 label="Horizontal"
# Frame 2 - Horizontal (-)

The spoke lies flat, a quarter turn from the start. This is the visual opposite
of [[z=0|frame 0]], and alternating only these two already reads as a crude
blink-style spinner if you ever need a two-frame fallback.

```
Loading -
```

- Glyph: `-` (hyphen-minus, U+002D)
- Angle: 0 degrees, pointing left and right
- Role: the halfway mark of the loop

Continue clockwise into the final lean at [[z=3|frame 3, the back slash]].

@plane z=3 x=6 y=0 label="Back slash"
# Frame 3 - Back slash (\)

The spoke leans the other way, top swung left and bottom swung right. One more
eighth-turn and it would stand vertical again, which is exactly why the loop
closes cleanly here.

```
Loading \
```

- Glyph: `\` (reverse solidus, U+005C)
- Angle: 135 degrees
- Role: the closing frame; it sets up the seamless wrap

The spoke completes its turn and the loop restarts at [[z=0|frame 0, Vertical]].
Emit a final `\r` and overwrite the whole line with `Done` when the work
finishes so no half-drawn spoke is left on screen.

title: Riverbend Galleria - Store Directory
src:
---
3md: 1.0
axis: floor
title: Riverbend Galleria - Store Directory
mall: Riverbend Galleria
address: 1200 Confluence Parkway
hours: Mon-Sat 10am-9pm, Sun 11am-6pm
levels: 4
---
Welcome to the Riverbend Galleria directory. Use the floor axis to move
between levels, from the parking concourse at the bottom to the rooftop
dining terrace on top. Each level lists stores with their suite numbers and
the nearest entrance. Elevators and escalators run through the central
atrium on every floor.

Quick jumps: [[z=0|Parking Concourse]], [[z=1|Street Level]],
[[z=2|Fashion Level]], [[z=3|Rooftop Dining]].

@plane z=0 x=0 y=0 label="P - Parking Concourse"
# Parking Concourse (Level P)

Covered parking with direct elevator access to the atrium. Pay stations
accept tap-to-pay and validate the first two hours free with any same-day
receipt.

| Zone | Spaces | Nearest entrance |
|------|--------|------------------|
| Blue (A1-A6) | 240 | North atrium elevators |
| Green (B1-B4) | 180 | South escalator bank |
| Accessible | 32 | Adjacent to both elevator cores |
| EV charging | 12 (Level 2 + 2 fast) | Blue zone, bays A3-A4 |

## Services on this level
- Guest services kiosk and lost-and-found, just inside the North entrance
- Stroller and wheelchair loan, free with a refundable deposit
- Bike racks and a repair stand outside the South doors

Take the central elevator up one floor to reach [[z=1|Street Level]] for
guest services, the food hall, and the transit lobby.

@plane z=1 x=0 y=0 label="1 - Street Level"
# Street Level (Level 1)

The main entrance level, anchored by the grocery hall and the transit lobby.
This is where most visitors arrive on foot or by bus.

## Anchor and grocery
- **Confluence Market** (Suite 110) - full grocery, deli, and pharmacy
- **Riverbend Pharmacy** (Suite 112) - inside the market, north counter

## Food hall (east wing)
| Suite | Vendor | Cuisine |
|-------|--------|---------|
| 120 | Ember & Oak | wood-fired pizza |
| 122 | Pho Confluence | Vietnamese noodles |
| 124 | The Green Bowl | salads and grain bowls |
| 126 | Sweetwater Creamery | gelato and shakes |

## Services and transit
- Bus and light-rail lobby at the west doors, sheltered waiting area
- ATM cluster and currency exchange near the central atrium
- Free Wi-Fi: network "Galleria-Guest", no password required

Head down to [[z=0|Parking Concourse]] for your car, or ride the escalator
up to [[z=2|Fashion Level]] for apparel and electronics.

@plane z=2 x=0 y=0 label="2 - Fashion Level"
# Fashion Level (Level 2)

Apparel, footwear, beauty, and electronics ring the atrium balcony. The
north wing holds the department-store anchor; the south wing is electronics
and home.

## North wing - apparel and beauty
- **Larkspur & Co.** (Suite 210) - department store anchor, two floors
- **Northwind Outfitters** (Suite 214) - outdoor and activewear
- **Lumen Beauty** (Suite 218) - cosmetics and fragrance
- **Tidewater Shoes** (Suite 222) - footwear for the whole family

## South wing - electronics and home
- **Circuit Confluence** (Suite 240) - phones, laptops, repairs
- **Hearth & Loom** (Suite 244) - kitchen, bedding, and decor
- **Pageturner Books** (Suite 248) - books, stationery, and a small cafe

## Good to know
- Personal styling by appointment at Larkspur, third-floor balcony desk
- Phone and tablet screen repair at Circuit Confluence, same-day on most models
- Family restroom and nursing room near the central atrium

When you are ready to eat, ride the glass elevator one more level to the
[[z=3|Rooftop Dining]] terrace, or return to [[z=1|Street Level]] for the
grocery hall.

@plane z=3 x=0 y=0 label="3 - Rooftop Dining"
# Rooftop Dining Terrace (Level 3)

An open-air terrace with full-service restaurants, a covered bar, and seasonal
events. The terrace wraps the atrium skylight and looks out over the river.

## Restaurants
| Suite | Restaurant | Notes |
|-------|------------|-------|
| 310 | The Confluence Grill | steaks and seafood, reservations advised |
| 314 | Saffron Terrace | Indian, large vegetarian menu |
| 318 | La Marea | coastal Spanish, tapas and paella |
| 322 | Cloudline Bar | covered cocktail bar, opens at 4pm |

## On the terrace
- Outdoor seating with heaters, open year-round under the awnings
- Live music on Friday and Saturday evenings, 7pm to 9pm
- Seasonal market stalls in summer, near the river-view railing

## Getting here
- Express glass elevator from [[z=1|Street Level]], stops only at the terrace
- Escalator from [[z=2|Fashion Level]], north wing
- Last seating is 30 minutes before mall close; the bar stays open later

Heading home? Take the express elevator straight down to the
[[z=0|Parking Concourse]].

title: Downtown Mobility Map
src:
---
3md: 1.0
axis: layer
title: Downtown Mobility Map
city: Riverbend
projection: Web Mercator
center: 41.8781,-87.6298
zoom: "14"
units: metric
---
A single downtown viewport rendered as a stack of toggleable overlays. The base
street grid sits at the bottom; transit, bike, and live traffic stack on top of
it. Each overlay aligns to the same bounding box, so a point at one (x, y) offset
refers to the same corner across every plane.

Start at the [[z=0|base map]], then turn layers on from there.

@plane z=0 x=0 y=0 label="Base map"
# Base Map (Street Grid)

The foundation layer. Streets, blocks, water, and parks. Everything above is
registered to this grid.

## Reference corners

| Marker | Cross streets        | Notes                          |
|--------|----------------------|--------------------------------|
| A      | Main St & 1st Ave    | City Hall, fountain plaza      |
| B      | Harbor Blvd & 9th    | Ferry terminal, taxi rank      |
| C      | Elm St & Park Way    | Riverbend Park north entrance  |

- **Scale:** 1 grid square = 100 m
- **One-way streets:** drawn with a single chevron at each intersection
- **Water:** the Riverbend channel runs diagonally across the southwest quadrant

Turn on overlays to read movement across this grid: [[z=1|transit]],
[[z=2|bike network]], and [[z=3|live traffic]].

@plane z=1 x=0 y=0 label="Transit"
# Transit Overlay

Rail and high-frequency bus lines drawn over the [[z=0|street grid]]. Stops snap
to the nearest corner.

## Lines in view

- **Blue Line (rail):** runs north-south through Main St, stops at City Hall (A)
  and the ferry terminal (B). Every 6 minutes at peak.
- **Route 22 (bus):** east-west along Park Way, connects Riverbend Park (C) to
  the Blue Line transfer at Main St. Every 10 minutes.
- **Harbor Shuttle:** loops Harbor Blvd to the ferry, timed to boat departures.

## Reading the overlay

1. Solid thick line = rail, right of way is grade separated.
2. Dashed line = bus, shares lanes with [[z=3|traffic]].
3. Filled circle = accessible stop with elevator or ramp.

> Transfer tip: the City Hall (A) stop links rail, the Route 22 bus, and the bike
> dock shown on the [[z=2|bike network]].

@plane z=2 x=0 y=20 label="Bike network"
# Bike Network Overlay

Protected lanes, painted lanes, and dock-based bikeshare, registered to the same
grid as the [[z=0|base map]].

## Lane types

- **Protected (green):** Park Way, full length. Physical curb separation.
- **Painted (yellow):** 1st Ave and 9th St. Lane marking only, shared with cars.
- **Shared street (dotted):** Elm St, posted 20 km/h, no dedicated lane.

## Bikeshare docks

| Dock   | Location                | Bikes (typical) |
|--------|-------------------------|-----------------|
| D-01   | City Hall plaza (A)     | 12 of 20        |
| D-02   | Ferry terminal (B)      | 4 of 16         |
| D-03   | Park north entrance (C) | 18 of 24        |

Dock D-02 runs low most mornings because of ferry commuters; rebalancing vans
restock it around 09:30. Riders heading north can connect to the Route 22 bus on
the [[z=1|transit overlay]] at the Park Way corner.

@plane z=3 x=0 y=0 label="Live traffic"
# Live Traffic Overlay

Real-time roadway speeds colored over the [[z=0|street grid]]. Snapshot taken at
17:42, weekday evening peak.

## Current conditions

- **Harbor Blvd (B):** red, stop and go. Ferry offload plus a stalled vehicle
  near 9th. Average 8 km/h.
- **Main St (A to B):** amber, heavy but moving. 18 km/h, signal-limited.
- **Park Way:** green, free flowing. 34 km/h. The protected bike lane on the
  [[z=2|bike network]] keeps turning conflicts low here.

## Color key

| Color | Meaning              | Speed vs free flow |
|-------|----------------------|--------------------|
| Green | Free flowing         | over 80 percent    |
| Amber | Slow                 | 40 to 80 percent   |
| Red   | Congested            | under 40 percent   |

> Routing note: avoid Harbor Blvd southbound until the stall clears. The Blue
> Line on the [[z=1|transit overlay]] bypasses this corridor entirely.

title: ACL Reconstruction - Recovery Timeline
src:
---
3md: 1.0
axis: time
title: ACL Reconstruction - Recovery Timeline
procedure: Left ACL reconstruction, hamstring autograft
surgery_date: 2026-04-02
surgeon: Dr. P. Nakamura
pt_clinic: Riverside Sports Rehab
---
Post-operative rehabilitation plotted by phase, from surgery to return to sport.
Each plane is a recovery phase; scrub the Z axis to move forward in healing time.
Pain and swelling are tracked alongside functional milestones.

@plane z=0 label="Phase 0 - Week 0 to 2"
# Phase 0: Acute (Weeks 0-2)

**Goals:** Protect the graft, control swelling, restore full extension.

## Status
- Pain: 6/10 at rest, managed with scheduled acetaminophen + short opioid taper.
- Swelling: marked, knee circumference +4 cm vs right.
- ROM: 0-90 degrees target by end of week 2.

## Program
- [x] Heel slides, quad sets, straight-leg raises
- [x] Ice + elevation 20 min, 4x/day
- [x] Brace locked in extension for ambulation
- [x] Weight-bearing as tolerated with crutches

@plane z=1 label="Phase 1 - Week 2 to 6"
# Phase 1: Early strengthening (Weeks 2-6)

**Goals:** Full ROM, normalize gait, wean off crutches.

## Status
- Pain: 2/10, only with end-range flexion.
- Swelling: mild, +1 cm.
- ROM: 0-130 degrees achieved.

## Program
- [x] Stationary bike (low resistance)
- [x] Mini-squats 0-60 degrees, calf raises
- [x] Discontinue crutches once gait is non-antalgic
- [ ] Begin closed-chain leg press, light

@plane z=2 label="Phase 2 - Month 2 to 4"
# Phase 2: Strength and proprioception (Months 2-4)

**Goals:** Restore quad strength symmetry, single-leg balance.

## Status
- Pain: 0/10 with daily activity.
- Quad strength: ~70% of contralateral on isokinetic test.

## Program
- Progressive leg press, hamstring curls, step-ups
- Single-leg balance on foam, perturbation training
- Pool jogging, elliptical
- **No cutting, pivoting, or running yet.**

@plane z=3 label="Phase 3 - Month 4 to 6"
# Phase 3: Return to running (Months 4-6)

**Goals:** Graduated running program, plyometric introduction.

## Status
- Quad strength: 85% symmetry by month 5.
- Hop test (single-leg): 88% limb symmetry index.

## Program
- Linear running progression (walk/run intervals to continuous)
- Double-leg then single-leg jumps, box drops
- Agility ladder, light deceleration drills

@plane z=4 label="Phase 4 - Month 6 to 9"
# Phase 4: Return to sport (Months 6-9)

**Goals:** Pass return-to-sport battery, full clearance.

## Return-to-sport criteria
- [ ] Limb symmetry index > 90% on hop battery
- [ ] Quad strength > 90% symmetry
- [ ] No effusion after high-load sessions
- [ ] Completed sport-specific cutting/pivoting progression
- [ ] Psychological readiness (ACL-RSI) score acceptable

## Notes
Earliest typical clearance is 9 months to reduce re-rupture risk; do not rush
back to pivoting sport before criteria are met. Reflect on the swelling baseline
from [[z=0|the acute phase]] when judging how far the knee has come.

title: Head CT - Axial Slices (Superior to Inferior)
src:
---
3md: 1.0
axis: depth
title: Head CT - Axial Slices (Superior to Inferior)
modality: CT, non-contrast
study: Routine head, 5 mm axial reconstruction
indication: Headache, rule out acute hemorrhage
orientation: radiological (patient left = image right)
---
A non-contrast head CT read slice by slice from the vertex down to the skull base.
Each plane is one axial level; scrub the Z axis to descend through the head.
Grids are schematic cross-sections - any non-space, non-dot character is tissue.

@plane z=0 label="Vertex - high convexity"
# Slice 1: High convexity (z = vertex)

Superior frontal and parietal lobes. Interhemispheric fissure midline.

```
.......#######.......
....###############..
..####OOOOOOOOO####...
.####OOO     OOO####..
.####OO   |   OO####..
.####OOO  |  OOO####..
..####OOOOOOOOO####...
....###############..
.......#######.......
```

- `#` = skull / scalp
- `O` = cortex and white matter
- `|` = interhemispheric fissure (midline)

**Read:** No extra-axial collection. Sulci symmetric. Midline preserved.

@plane z=1 label="Lateral ventricles - bodies"
# Slice 2: Bodies of the lateral ventricles

```
......###########......
...###OOOOOOOOOOO###...
..##OOOOOO|OOOOOO##....
.##OOOO ~~~|~~~ OOO##..
.##OOO ~LV~|~LV~ OO##..
.##OOOO ~~~|~~~ OOO##..
..##OOOOOO|OOOOOO##....
...###OOOOOOOOOOO###...
......###########......
```

- `~` = CSF in lateral ventricles (LV)
- `O` = brain parenchyma

**Read:** Lateral ventricles symmetric, normal size. No mass effect.

@plane z=2 label="Basal ganglia / third ventricle"
# Slice 3: Basal ganglia and third ventricle

```
.......#########.......
....##OOOOOOOOOOO##....
..##OOO[CN][CN]OOO##...
.##OO[P]  ~|~  [P]OO##.
.##OO[GP]~3V~[GP]OO##..
.##OO[P]  ~|~  [P]OO##.
..##OOO[CN][CN]OOO##...
....##OOOOOOOOOOO##....
.......#########.......
```

- `[CN]` = caudate nucleus head
- `[P]` = putamen, `[GP]` = globus pallidus
- `~3V~` = third ventricle

**Read:** Gray-white differentiation preserved. No hyperdensity to suggest acute blood. No hypodensity to suggest established infarct. Compare with [[z=3|the suprasellar level]] for the circle of Willis.

@plane z=3 label="Suprasellar cistern"
# Slice 4: Suprasellar cistern and midbrain

```
.......#########.......
....##OOOOOOOOOOO##....
..##OOOO  ***  OOOO##..
.##OOO *star-cistern* O##
.##OOOO [MB-pons] OOO##.
..##OOOOOOOOOOOOOOO##..
....##OOO     OOO##....
......##temporal##.....
.......#########.......
```

- `***` / `star-cistern` = suprasellar (star-shaped) cistern - patent
- `[MB-pons]` = midbrain / upper pons

**Read:** Basal cisterns patent. No effacement to suggest herniation or diffuse edema.

@plane z=4 label="Skull base / posterior fossa"
# Slice 5: Posterior fossa and skull base

```
.......#########.......
....###OOOOOOOOO###....
..###OO[cerebellum]OO###
.###OO    ~4V~    OO###.
.###OO [L-hemi][R-hemi]##
..###OOOOOOOOOOOOO###..
....####[petrous]####..
.......#########.......
```

- `~4V~` = fourth ventricle (midline, CSF)
- `[petrous]` = petrous temporal bone

**Read:** Cerebellar hemispheres symmetric, fourth ventricle midline and normal. No posterior fossa hemorrhage.

## Impression
No acute intracranial hemorrhage, mass effect, or large territorial infarct on this non-contrast study.

title: Clinical Trial Roadmap - CRV-208 (novel GLP-1/GIP agonist)
src:
---
3md: 1.0
axis: stage
title: Clinical Trial Roadmap - CRV-208 (novel GLP-1/GIP agonist)
sponsor: Corvid Therapeutics
indication: Type 2 diabetes with obesity
ind_number: 162447
status: Phase 2 active
---
The full development pathway for an investigational drug, from lab to label.
Each plane is a regulatory stage; scrub the Z axis to advance through the program.
Sample sizes and endpoints are representative of a typical metabolic program.

@plane z=0 label="Preclinical"
# Stage 0: Preclinical

**Objective:** Establish safety and biological rationale before first human dose.

- In vitro receptor binding and selectivity (GLP-1 vs GIP).
- Rodent and non-rodent toxicology (28-day and 13-week GLP studies).
- Pharmacokinetics, metabolism, carcinogenicity signals (thyroid C-cell focus).
- [x] IND-enabling package assembled
- [x] FDA IND cleared, 30-day safety hold passed

@plane z=1 label="Phase 1 - Safety"
# Stage 1: Phase 1

**Objective:** Safety, tolerability, dose-ranging in humans.

| Parameter        | Value                                   |
|------------------|-----------------------------------------|
| Population       | Healthy adult volunteers                |
| N                | 64                                      |
| Design           | SAD then MAD, placebo-controlled        |
| Primary endpoint | Adverse events, max tolerated dose      |
| Duration         | ~4 months                               |

- Dose-limiting toxicity: nausea at the highest single ascending dose.
- PK supports once-weekly subcutaneous dosing.

@plane z=2 label="Phase 2 - Efficacy signal"
# Stage 2: Phase 2 (ACTIVE)

**Objective:** Proof of concept, dose selection, preliminary efficacy.

| Parameter        | Value                                       |
|------------------|---------------------------------------------|
| Population       | T2DM, BMI 30-45, HbA1c 7.0-10.0%            |
| N                | 320, four arms                              |
| Design           | Randomized, double-blind, placebo-controlled|
| Primary endpoint | Change in HbA1c at 24 weeks                  |
| Key secondary    | Percent body weight reduction at 24 weeks   |

- [x] Enrollment complete
- [ ] 24-week readout (expected Q4 2026)
- Interim DSMB review: no unexpected safety signals.

@plane z=3 label="Phase 3 - Confirmatory"
# Stage 3: Phase 3

**Objective:** Confirm efficacy and safety in large, diverse populations.

| Parameter        | Value                                          |
|------------------|------------------------------------------------|
| Population       | T2DM, broad inclusion incl. cardiovascular risk|
| N                | ~2,400 across multiple trials                   |
| Design           | RCT vs placebo and active comparator            |
| Primary endpoint | HbA1c and weight at 52 and 72 weeks             |
| Dedicated study  | Cardiovascular outcomes (MACE) sub-trial        |

- Multi-region (US, EU, APAC) to support global filing.
- Built on the dose chosen back in [[z=2|the Phase 2 stage]].

@plane z=4 label="Regulatory review"
# Stage 4: Regulatory submission and review

- Compile NDA / MAA with integrated safety and efficacy summaries.
- FDA review clock (standard ~10 months) and advisory committee, if convened.
- EMA CHMP opinion and national implementation.
- [ ] Label negotiation (indication wording, REMS if required)
- [ ] Decision: approval, complete response letter, or refusal

@plane z=5 label="Phase 4 - Post-marketing"
# Stage 5: Phase 4 (post-marketing)

**Objective:** Monitor real-world safety and long-term outcomes.

- Pharmacovigilance and spontaneous adverse event reporting.
- Required post-marketing studies (e.g., long-term thyroid surveillance registry).
- Real-world effectiveness, adherence, and health-economics data.
- Potential label expansion to adjacent indications (e.g., obesity without diabetes).

title: ECG Lead II - One Cardiac Cycle (Animated)
src:
---
3md: 1.0
axis: frame
title: ECG Lead II - One Cardiac Cycle (Animated)
lead: II
rate: ~75 bpm (normal sinus rhythm)
paper_speed: 25 mm/s
purpose: Teaching aid - play frames in order to trace one heartbeat
---
A single normal cardiac cycle on ECG lead II, drawn one frame at a time.
Each plane is an animation frame; play the Z axis in order to watch the trace
build P wave, QRS complex, and T wave. Any non-space, non-dot mark is the trace.

@plane z=0 label="Frame 0 - baseline"
# Frame 0: Isoelectric baseline

```
.........................................
.........................................
.........................................
.________________________________________
.........................................
.........................................
```

The heart is in electrical diastole. Flat baseline before atrial depolarization.

@plane z=1 label="Frame 1 - P wave"
# Frame 1: P wave (atrial depolarization)

```
.........................................
.........................................
.......-.................................
.____--.--_______________________________
.........................................
.........................................
```

Small rounded upward deflection - the atria depolarize. Then the PR segment
(the brief flat stretch) as the signal pauses at the AV node.

@plane z=2 label="Frame 2 - QRS upstroke"
# Frame 2: Q dip and R upstroke

```
.................../......................
................../.......................
.......-........./........................
.____--.--___.__/_________________________
............\\./..........................
.............V............................
```

Tiny Q dip, then the sharp tall R upstroke as the ventricles begin to depolarize.
This is the start of the [[z=3|full QRS complex]].

@plane z=3 label="Frame 3 - QRS complete"
# Frame 3: Full QRS complex

```
.................../\\.....................
................../..\\....................
.......-........./....\\...................
.____--.--___.__/......\\____.____________
............\\./........\\.../.............
.............V...........\\./..............
.........................V................
```

The complete QRS: small Q, tall R peak, deep S trough. Rapid ventricular
depolarization - the loudest electrical event of the cycle.

@plane z=4 label="Frame 4 - T wave"
# Frame 4: T wave (ventricular repolarization)

```
.................../\\.....................
................../..\\.......___..........
.......-........./....\\...../...\\.........
.____--.--___.__/......\\__/.....\\___.____
............\\./........\\./...............
.............V...........V................
```

After the ST segment (flat, on the baseline), the broad upward T wave marks
ventricular repolarization. The cycle then returns to baseline and repeats.

## Reading checklist
- [x] P before every QRS, QRS after every P (sinus rhythm)
- [x] Narrow QRS (< 120 ms)
- [x] Upright T in lead II
- Estimated rate from R-R interval: ~75 bpm.

title: Anterior Forearm - Layered Anatomy
src:
---
3md: 1.0
axis: layer
title: Anterior Forearm - Layered Anatomy
region: Right forearm, anterior (volar) aspect
purpose: Teaching reference for medical and PA students
view: anterior, from superficial to deep
---
A dissection-style walk through the anterior forearm, peeled one layer at a time.
Each plane is an anatomical layer; toggle the Z axis from skin down to bone.
Schematic cross-sections use grids - any non-space, non-dot mark is a structure.

@plane z=0 label="Layer 1 - Skin and superficial fascia"
# Layer 1: Skin and superficial fascia

```
.~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~.
.SSSSSSSSSSSSSSSSSSSSS.   S = epidermis/dermis
.fffffffffffffffffffff.   f = subcutaneous fat
.--v---------------v--.   v = superficial veins
......................
```

## Contents
- Cephalic vein (lateral), basilic vein (medial), median antebrachial vein.
- Medial and lateral antebrachial cutaneous nerves.
- Superficial lymphatics draining to the cubital and axillary nodes.

@plane z=1 label="Layer 2 - Superficial flexor muscles"
# Layer 2: Superficial flexor compartment

```
.#####################.   # = deep (antebrachial) fascia
.[PT][FCR][PL][FCU]....   muscle bellies, lateral to medial
.[--- FDS broad sheet ---].
.......................
```

## Muscles (lateral to medial)
- **Pronator teres (PT)**
- **Flexor carpi radialis (FCR)**
- **Palmaris longus (PL)** - absent in ~15% of people
- **Flexor carpi ulnaris (FCU)**
- **Flexor digitorum superficialis (FDS)** - intermediate, deep to the above

All arise from the common flexor origin on the medial epicondyle.

@plane z=2 label="Layer 3 - Deep flexors and neurovascular"
# Layer 3: Deep flexors and the neurovascular plane

```
.[==== FDP ====][FPL ].   FDP = flexor digitorum profundus
.....<UA>.....<RA>.....   RA/UA = radial / ulnar artery
......(MN).....(UN)....   MN = median n., UN = ulnar n.
.[===== PQ distal =====].  PQ = pronator quadratus (distal)
```

## Contents
- **Flexor digitorum profundus (FDP)** and **flexor pollicis longus (FPL)**.
- **Median nerve** runs between FDS and FDP; **ulnar nerve** with the ulnar artery medially.
- **Radial artery** lies lateral, palpable at the wrist.
- See deeper bone relations at [[z=3|the radius and ulna layer]].

@plane z=3 label="Layer 4 - Bones and interosseous membrane"
# Layer 4: Radius, ulna, and interosseous membrane

```
....(((RADIUS)))......(((ULNA)))....
....   O lateral        O medial   .
....    \\              //         .
....     \\== IOM fibers ==//       .
.................................. .
```

## Contents
- **Radius** (lateral) and **ulna** (medial).
- **Interosseous membrane (IOM)** transfers load from radius to ulna; fibers run obliquely distomedially.
- Anterior interosseous nerve and vessels run on the membrane, supplying deep flexors and pronator quadratus.

## Clinical note
A high median nerve lesion (between FDS/FDP) weakens FPL and the radial half of FDP, giving the
characteristic loss of the thumb and index distal flexion ("benediction" attempt to make a fist).

title: Daily Medication Schedule - Polypharmacy Plan
src:
---

Times follow a 24-hour clock. Always confirm against the official MAR.

# 07:00 - Empty stomach, before breakfast

|-----------------------|-----------|-------|------------------------------------|
