| Pantoprazole          | 40 mg     | PO    | 30 min before first meal           |

- [ ] Wait at least 4 hours before taking calcium or iron (interferes with levothyroxine absorption).

@plane z=1 label="08:00 - With breakfast"
# 08:00 - With breakfast

| Drug                  | Dose      | Route | Notes                              |
|-----------------------|-----------|-------|------------------------------------|
| Metformin XR          | 1000 mg   | PO    | Take with food to reduce GI upset  |
| Lisinopril            | 10 mg     | PO    | Monitor for dry cough              |
| Atorvastatin          | 20 mg     | PO    | Can take any time; grouped here    |

- Check fasting glucose before eating; log in diary.

@plane z=2 label="13:00 - With lunch"
# 13:00 - With lunch

| Drug                  | Dose      | Route | Notes                              |
|-----------------------|-----------|-------|------------------------------------|
| Amlodipine            | 5 mg      | PO    | For BP; watch for ankle swelling   |

- No diabetes medication at lunch on current regimen.

@plane z=3 label="18:00 - With dinner"
# 18:00 - With dinner

| Drug                  | Dose      | Route | Notes                              |
|-----------------------|-----------|-------|------------------------------------|
| Metformin XR          | 1000 mg   | PO    | Second daily dose, with food       |

- [ ] Recheck BP if home reading was > 150/90 at any point today.

@plane z=4 label="22:00 - Bedtime"
# 22:00 - Bedtime

| Drug                  | Dose      | Route | Notes                              |
|-----------------------|-----------|-------|------------------------------------|
| Aspirin               | 81 mg     | PO    | Low-dose, cardioprotective         |

## PRN (as needed)
- Acetaminophen 500 mg PO q6h PRN pain - max 3 g/day.
- **Avoid NSAIDs** (ibuprofen, naproxen) given lisinopril plus diabetes - kidney risk.

## Key interaction reminders
- Penicillin allergy on file - confirm any new antibiotic is not a beta-lactam first.
- Levothyroxine timing is the most fragile part of this schedule; see [[z=0|the 07:00 window]].

title: Migraine Symptom Diary - Episode Phases
src:
---
3md: 1.0
axis: time
title: Migraine Symptom Diary - Episode Phases
patient: self-tracked
purpose: Identify triggers and track treatment response across an attack
neurologist: Dr. R. Solberg
preventive: topiramate 50 mg BID
---
One migraine attack tracked through its natural phases, hours apart.
Each plane is a phase of the episode; scrub the Z axis to follow the attack
from the first warning signs through to recovery. Pain scale is 0-10.

@plane z=0 label="Prodrome - hours before"
# Prodrome (T minus 6 hours)

**Pain:** 0/10 (no head pain yet)

## Early warning signs
- Yawning frequently, unusual food cravings (chocolate).
- Mild neck stiffness, irritability, difficulty concentrating.
- Increased thirst and urination.

## Suspected triggers (last 24h)
- Poor sleep (5 hours), skipped lunch, weather front moving in.

## Action
- [x] Hydrate, eat a balanced snack
- [x] Note prodrome in app - early triptan window may help

@plane z=1 label="Aura - 30 to 60 min before"
# Aura (T minus 45 minutes)

**Pain:** 1/10 (building pressure)

## Aura features
- Visual: shimmering zigzag scotoma in left visual field, expanding over ~20 min.
- Mild tingling in the right hand (paresthesia).
- Aura fully reversible, lasting under an hour - typical for this patient.

## Action
- [x] Move to a dark, quiet room
- [x] Take rizatriptan 10 mg at first sign (do NOT wait for full headache)

@plane z=2 label="Headache - peak attack"
# Headache phase (T 0 to +4 hours)

**Pain:** 8/10, throbbing, left temporal, unilateral

## Associated symptoms
- Photophobia and phonophobia (light and sound sensitivity).
- Nausea, one episode of vomiting.
- Pain worse with movement; bending over intensifies it.

## Treatment given
| Time   | Medication            | Effect at 2h        |
|--------|-----------------------|---------------------|
| T 0    | rizatriptan 10 mg     | partial relief      |
| T +2h  | rizatriptan 10 mg (2nd)| pain down to 4/10  |
| T +2h  | ondansetron 4 mg ODT  | nausea controlled   |

Compare the early warning at [[z=0|the prodrome]] - acting then might have blunted this peak.

@plane z=3 label="Resolution - fading"
# Resolution (T +4 to +8 hours)

**Pain:** 3/10, dull and diffuse

- Throbbing has settled to a generalized ache.
- Light sensitivity easing; able to tolerate dim screen.
- Slept 2 hours, woke with further improvement.
- No additional triptan needed (max 2 doses/24h reached).

@plane z=4 label="Postdrome - the migraine hangover"
# Postdrome (Day after)

**Pain:** 0-1/10

## Lingering symptoms
- Fatigue, feeling "wrung out," mild difficulty concentrating.
- Tender scalp on the affected side.
- Mood low, appetite slowly returning.

## Episode review
- Total attack duration: ~26 hours including postdrome.
- Likely trigger stack: sleep deprivation + missed meal + barometric change.
- [ ] Discuss with neurologist whether to add a CGRP-targeted preventive
- [ ] Prioritize consistent sleep and regular meals this week

title: Inpatient Chart - Rivera, J. (MRN 0048-2291)
src:
---
3md: 1.0
axis: time
title: Inpatient Chart - Rivera, J. (MRN 0048-2291)
patient: Rivera, Jordan
dob: 1971-03-14
admit_dx: Community-acquired pneumonia, right lower lobe
attending: Dr. A. Okafor
unit: 4 West, Bed 12
---
Progress chart for a 5-day admission. Each plane is one hospital day.
Scrub the Z axis to follow vitals, labs, and the treatment course over time.

@plane z=0 label="Day 1 - Admission"
# Hospital Day 1 (2026-06-19)

**Chief complaint:** 3 days of productive cough, fever, right-sided pleuritic chest pain.

## Vitals
| Time  | Temp (C) | HR  | BP      | RR | SpO2  |
|-------|----------|-----|---------|----|-------|
| 08:00 | 39.1     | 112 | 104/68  | 26 | 89% RA|
| 14:00 | 38.6     | 104 | 110/70  | 22 | 94% 2L|

## Labs
- WBC 16.4 (84% neutrophils)
- CRP 188 mg/L
- Lactate 2.1 mmol/L
- Blood cultures x2 drawn before antibiotics

## Plan
- [x] Start ceftriaxone 1 g IV q24h
- [x] Start azithromycin 500 mg IV q24h
- [x] O2 via nasal cannula, titrate to SpO2 > 92%
- [x] CXR confirms RLL consolidation
- CURB-65 score 2 - admit to floor

@plane z=1 label="Day 2"
# Hospital Day 2 (2026-06-20)

## Vitals
| Time  | Temp (C) | HR  | BP      | RR | SpO2   |
|-------|----------|-----|---------|----|--------|
| 06:00 | 38.4     | 98  | 116/72  | 20 | 93% 2L |
| 18:00 | 37.9     | 92  | 118/74  | 19 | 95% 2L |

## Notes
- Patient reports cough slightly looser, pain improved with scheduled acetaminophen.
- Blood cultures: no growth at 24h.
- Continuing antibiotics. Trending in the right direction; compare with [[z=4|the discharge summary]].

@plane z=2 label="Day 3"
# Hospital Day 3 (2026-06-21)

## Vitals
| Time  | Temp (C) | HR | BP     | RR | SpO2     |
|-------|----------|----|--------|----|----------|
| 06:00 | 37.4     | 86 | 122/76 | 18 | 96% 1L   |
| 18:00 | 37.1     | 82 | 120/78 | 17 | 95% RA   |

## Labs
- WBC down to 11.2
- CRP 96 mg/L (falling)

## Plan
- [x] Wean off supplemental O2, tolerated room air this afternoon.
- [x] Transition azithromycin IV to PO.

@plane z=3 label="Day 4"
# Hospital Day 4 (2026-06-22)

## Vitals
| Time  | Temp (C) | HR | BP     | RR | SpO2   |
|-------|----------|----|--------|----|--------|
| 06:00 | 36.9     | 78 | 124/80 | 16 | 97% RA |

## Notes
- Afebrile > 24h, ambulating in hall without desaturation.
- Step down ceftriaxone to oral amoxicillin-clavulanate 875/125 mg q12h.
- WBC 8.9, CRP 41 mg/L.
- Anticipate discharge tomorrow if stable overnight.

@plane z=4 label="Day 5 - Discharge"
# Hospital Day 5 (2026-06-23) - DISCHARGE

## Discharge vitals
- Temp 36.7 C, HR 74, BP 126/78, RR 15, SpO2 98% RA

## Discharge summary
- Diagnosis: CAP, RLL, resolving.
- Hospital course uncomplicated; rapid response to beta-lactam plus macrolide.

## Discharge medications
1. Amoxicillin-clavulanate 875/125 mg PO q12h - complete 5 more days
2. Guaifenesin PRN cough
3. Resume home lisinopril 10 mg daily

## Follow-up
- [ ] PCP in 7-10 days
- [ ] Repeat CXR in 6 weeks to confirm resolution
- Return precautions: fever > 38.5, worsening dyspnea, chest pain.

title: Daily Medication Schedule
src:
---
3md: 1.0
axis: time
title: Daily Medication Schedule
patient: Margaret Holloway
prescriber: Dr. A. Okafor, Internal Medicine
updated: 2026-06-23
note: Take all doses with a full glass of water unless told otherwise.
---
This schedule stacks the day along the time axis. Each plane is one dosing
window. Read it top to bottom within a window, and move forward through the
windows as the day goes on. Pillbox compartments are labeled to match the
window names below.

Allergy: penicillin (hives). Keep this card with the pillbox.

@plane z=0 x=0 y=0 label="Morning (with breakfast)"
# Morning, 7:30 AM

Take these together with food. Do not skip breakfast on this window, two of
these are hard on an empty stomach.

| Medication | Dose | Purpose |
|------------|------|---------|
| Metformin ER | 500 mg, 1 tablet | Blood sugar |
| Lisinopril | 10 mg, 1 tablet | Blood pressure |
| Atorvastatin | 20 mg, 1 tablet | Cholesterol |
| Vitamin D3 | 2000 IU, 1 softgel | Bone health |

Checklist:

- [ ] Check blood pressure first, log the reading in the notebook
- [ ] Swallow tablets with water, stay upright for a few minutes
- [ ] Tick the Morning compartment as empty

Next dose is [[z=1|Midday with lunch]]. If you feel dizzy after the
Lisinopril, note it and tell Dr. Okafor.

@plane z=1 x=2 y=0 label="Midday (with lunch)"
# Midday, 12:30 PM

A light window. Only one scheduled tablet here, plus any as-needed relief.

| Medication | Dose | Purpose |
|------------|------|---------|
| Metformin ER | 500 mg, 1 tablet | Blood sugar (second dose) |

As needed (not daily):

- Acetaminophen 500 mg, 1 to 2 tablets for joint pain, no more than 6 in 24 hours
- Do NOT add ibuprofen, it interacts with the Lisinopril from [[z=0|the morning]]

Checklist:

- [ ] Take Metformin with the largest bite of lunch to avoid stomach upset
- [ ] Tick the Midday compartment as empty

Carry forward to [[z=2|Evening with dinner]].

@plane z=2 x=4 y=0 label="Evening (with dinner)"
# Evening, 6:00 PM

The fullest window of the day. Sertraline goes here, not at bedtime, because it
can disturb sleep for some people.

| Medication | Dose | Purpose |
|------------|------|---------|
| Metformin ER | 500 mg, 1 tablet | Blood sugar (third dose) |
| Sertraline | 50 mg, 1 tablet | Mood |
| Calcium + magnesium | 1 tablet | Bone health |

Checklist:

- [ ] Take with the main meal, calcium absorbs better with food
- [ ] Keep calcium at least 2 hours apart from any thyroid medication
- [ ] Tick the Evening compartment as empty

One window left, see [[z=3|Bedtime]].

@plane z=3 x=6 y=0 label="Bedtime (before sleep)"
# Bedtime, 10:00 PM

Wind-down dose. Warfarin lives here on its own so the timing stays consistent
day to day, consistency matters more than the exact hour.

| Medication | Dose | Purpose |
|------------|------|---------|
| Warfarin | 5 mg, 1 tablet | Blood thinner |
| Melatonin | 3 mg, 1 tablet | Sleep |

Checklist:

- [ ] Take Warfarin at the same time every night
- [ ] No grapefruit juice with this dose, it raises drug levels
- [ ] Tick the Bedtime compartment as empty
- [ ] Set out tomorrow morning's pillbox so [[z=0|the morning window]] is ready

If a Warfarin dose is missed and it is still the same evening, take it. If it is
already the next morning, skip it and resume the normal schedule, do not double
up. Call the clinic if two doses in a row are missed.

@plane z=4 x=8 y=0 label="Weekly review (Sunday)"
# Weekly review, Sunday morning

Once a week, before refilling the box.

- [ ] Refill all four daily windows for the coming week
- [ ] Count remaining tablets, request refills when under 7 days on hand
- [ ] Confirm the next INR blood test date for the Warfarin
- [ ] Note any side effects to raise at the next appointment

Current refills on file:

| Medication | Days remaining | Refill by |
|------------|----------------|-----------|
| Metformin ER | 11 | 2026-06-30 |
| Warfarin | 6 | 2026-06-25 |
| Sertraline | 18 | 2026-07-07 |

Warfarin is the most urgent. Start the day at [[z=0|the morning window]] again
once the box is filled.

title: Q3 Platform Sync - Weekly Engineering Meeting
src:
---
3md: 1.0
axis: time
title: Q3 Platform Sync - Weekly Engineering Meeting
date: 2026-06-23
location: Room 4B / Zoom (link in calendar invite)
facilitator: Priya Nadakumar
notetaker: Dani Osei
duration: 60 minutes
---
Weekly cross-team sync for the Platform group. Each plane below is a single time
slot. Keep updates tight; park deep dives for the listed follow-ups. Times are in
US Pacific. If you cannot attend, leave async notes in the shared doc before 09:00.

@plane z=0 x=0 y=0 label="09:00 - Roll call and agenda review"
# 09:00 - 09:05 - Roll call and agenda review (5 min)

**Owner:** Priya Nadakumar

- Confirm attendance and quorum (need at least one rep from API, Infra, and Mobile).
- Read out the three goals for today:
  1. Land a go / no-go on the caching rollout.
  2. Unblock the Mobile team on rate limits.
  3. Agree on owners for the on-call rotation gap.
- Approve last week's minutes (linked in the invite).

> Reminder: hard stop at 10:00. Jump ahead to [[z=4|the wrap-up]] if we finish early.

@plane z=1 x=0 y=1 label="09:05 - Caching rollout decision"
# 09:05 - 09:25 - Caching rollout: go / no-go (20 min)

**Owner:** Marcus Lin (Infra)

## Context
The read-through cache shipped to 10 percent of traffic on Monday. We need a
decision on widening to 100 percent before the Friday freeze.

## Data so far
- p99 latency down 38 percent on cached endpoints.
- Cache hit rate steady at 91 percent after warm-up.
- One incident: stale user-profile reads for ~4 minutes during a deploy
  (root cause: missing invalidation hook, now patched).

## Decision needed
- [ ] Approve ramp to 50 percent today, 100 percent Thursday, OR
- [ ] Hold at 10 percent pending a second week of data.

Open risk on rate limits feeds directly into [[z=2|the next slot]], so keep
Mobile in the room for this vote.

@plane z=2 x=1 y=1 label="09:25 - Mobile rate-limit unblock"
# 09:25 - 09:40 - Mobile rate-limit unblock (15 min)

**Owner:** Aisha Rahman (Mobile)

## The blocker
The mobile sync client is hitting 429s during the morning login spike. Current
limit is 60 requests/minute/user; the cold-start sync needs a burst of ~90.

## Options on the table
| Option | Effort | Risk |
| --- | --- | --- |
| Raise burst ceiling to 120/min | Low (config) | Abuse surface grows |
| Add a dedicated batch-sync endpoint | Medium | Two weeks of work |
| Client-side request coalescing | Medium | Ships in next app release |

## Ask
Pick one option and name an owner before we leave this slot. The caching numbers
from [[z=1|the rollout discussion]] suggest the batch endpoint would cache well.

@plane z=3 x=1 y=2 label="09:40 - On-call rotation gap"
# 09:40 - 09:55 - On-call rotation gap (15 min)

**Owner:** Dani Osei

## Problem
Two engineers rotate off next month and we have a coverage hole for weeks 31-33.

## Proposal
- Pull in two volunteers from the API team for secondary coverage.
- Shorten the primary shift from 7 days to 4 during the gap to spread load.
- Buy out the holiday week (week 33) with comp time.

## Action items
- [ ] Marcus to confirm Infra can cover week 32 primary.
- [ ] Priya to update PagerDuty schedule by EOD Wednesday.
- [ ] HR to approve comp-time arrangement (Dani to file ticket).

@plane z=4 x=0 y=3 label="09:55 - Wrap-up and action review"
# 09:55 - 10:00 - Wrap-up and action review (5 min)

**Owner:** Priya Nadakumar

## Decisions logged
- Caching: see the vote from [[z=1|the rollout slot]].
- Rate limits: option chosen in [[z=2|the Mobile slot]].
- On-call: owners assigned in [[z=3|the rotation slot]].

## Consolidated action items
1. Update PagerDuty schedule (Priya, Wed).
2. File comp-time ticket (Dani, Wed).
3. Spike the batch-sync endpoint if selected (Aisha, next sprint).
4. Post caching ramp plan to #platform (Marcus, today).

## Next meeting
Same time next week. Send agenda topics to Priya by Monday 17:00.
Back to [[z=0|the top]] for the goals we set out to hit.

title: The Lunar Cycle, Phase by Phase
src:
---
3md: 1.0
axis: time
title: The Lunar Cycle, Phase by Phase
synodic_period: 29.53 days
hemisphere: northern
illumination_note: percentages are approximate mid-phase values
---
The Moon does not make its own light. Every phase you see is sunlight reflected
off the half of the Moon that faces the Sun, viewed from a slowly shifting angle
as the Moon orbits Earth. One full trip through the named phases is a synodic
month of about 29.53 days, measured from one New Moon to the next.

Read the planes below in Z order: time runs from day 0 (New Moon) forward through
one complete cycle. The waxing half (growing light) sits to the left of center,
the waning half (shrinking light) to the right, so the stack mirrors the way the
terminator sweeps across the disc.

@plane z=0 x=0 y=0 label="New Moon"
# New Moon (day 0)

The Moon sits between Earth and the Sun, so its lit face points away from us and
the disc is effectively invisible. Illumination near 0 percent.

- **Rises:** around sunrise
- **Sets:** around sunset
- **Visible:** essentially not; lost in the Sun's glare
- **Tides:** spring tides (Sun and Moon pull in line)

This is the only moment a solar eclipse can happen, and only when the alignment
is exact enough for the Moon's shadow to touch Earth. From here the lit edge
begins to creep into view. Continue to [[z=3.7|the Waxing Crescent]].

@plane z=3.7 x=-2 y=1 label="Waxing Crescent"
# Waxing Crescent (about day 3 to 4)

A slim, curved sliver of light appears on the **right** side of the disc as seen
from the northern hemisphere. Illumination roughly 1 to 49 percent and climbing.

- **Best seen:** in the western sky just after sunset
- **Earthshine:** the dark portion often glows faintly, sunlight bounced off
  Earth and back onto the Moon (sometimes called "the old Moon in the new Moon's
  arms")

The lit fraction grows a little each evening. When the disc reaches exactly half
lit, we call it First Quarter: jump ahead to [[z=7.4|First Quarter]].

@plane z=7.4 x=-1 y=0 label="First Quarter"
# First Quarter (about day 7 to 8)

Half the disc is lit, the right half from the northern view. Despite looking like
a "half moon," it is called First Quarter because the Moon has completed one
quarter of its orbit. Illumination near 50 percent.

- **Rises:** around noon
- **Sets:** around midnight
- **Visible:** high in the southern sky in the evening, a great time for binocular
  viewing along the **terminator** (the day/night line) where crater shadows are
  longest

Light keeps spilling across the disc, swelling into the bulging Waxing Gibbous
over the next several nights until the whole near side blazes at
[[z=14.8|Full Moon]].

@plane z=14.8 x=0 y=2 label="Full Moon"
# Full Moon (about day 14 to 15)

Earth now sits between the Sun and the Moon, so the full near side is lit and the
disc is a complete bright circle. Illumination near 100 percent.

- **Rises:** around sunset
- **Sets:** around sunrise
- **Visible:** all night, the brightest natural object in the night sky
- **Tides:** spring tides again (Sun and Moon aligned, opposite sides)

A lunar eclipse can occur only at Full Moon, when Earth's shadow falls across the
disc. After tonight the light begins to retreat from the right edge, thinning
through the Waning Gibbous toward [[z=22.1|Last Quarter]].

@plane z=22.1 x=2 y=0 label="Last Quarter"
# Last Quarter (about day 22 to 23)

Half lit again, but now the **left** half glows (northern view), the opposite
side from First Quarter. The Moon has completed three quarters of its orbit.
Illumination near 50 percent.

- **Rises:** around midnight
- **Sets:** around noon
- **Visible:** high in the sky before dawn and into the morning

The terminator once more throws dramatic shadows for observers, this time across
the opposite landscape. From here only a fading curve remains: finish with
[[z=29.53|the Waning Crescent]].

@plane z=29.53 x=2 y=1 label="Waning Crescent"
# Waning Crescent (about day 25 to 28)

A thin curve of light clings to the **left** edge and thins night by night.
Illumination roughly 49 down toward 0 percent. Earthshine often returns, softly
lighting the dark portion just before dawn.

- **Best seen:** in the eastern sky just before sunrise
- **Sometimes called:** the "old Moon"

When the last sliver dissolves into the Sun's glare, the Moon returns to the
alignment it started from and the synodic month closes. The next New Moon begins
the cycle anew: loop back to [[z=0|New Moon]].

title: The Meridian Museum - Self-Guided Walking Tour
src:
---
3md: 1.0
axis: space
title: The Meridian Museum - Self-Guided Walking Tour
docent: Recorded narration available at each numbered stop
duration: about 55 minutes
accessibility: step-free route via the west elevator
---
Welcome to the Meridian Museum. This self-guided tour moves you through five
connected galleries on the second floor, west to east. Each plane below is one
room. Follow the numbered floor markers, and use the cross-references to double
back when a later room rewards a second look at an earlier one.

Start at the top of the grand stair and turn left into the Atrium.

@plane z=0 x=0 y=0 label="Atrium and Orientation"
# Stop 1 - The Atrium

You are standing under the glass oculus. The terrazzo compass inlaid in the
floor points true north, which is the direction you are now facing.

- **Look up.** The oculus is 14 meters above you. On a clear morning a bright
  disc of light tracks across the north wall like a slow sundial.
- **Look down.** The brass meridian line crossing the compass is the museum's
  namesake. It was surveyed in 1911 and is accurate to within a few centimeters.

**Where to go next:** the wide doorway on your right leads east into the Hall of
Antiquities. If you only have twenty minutes, skip ahead to
[[z=3|the Impressionist Room]], which is the collection's centerpiece.

> Tip: benches line the north wall. The coat check is behind you, under the
> stair.

@plane z=1 x=12 y=0 label="Hall of Antiquities"
# Stop 2 - The Hall of Antiquities

Twelve meters east of the atrium, the light drops and the ceilings lower. This
is deliberate: the basalt and limestone pieces here were made for dim interiors.

## Don't miss

1. **The Sailor's Stele (case 4).** A carved limestone slab listing a merchant
   ship's crew, names and all, from a Mediterranean port around 300 BCE.
2. **The Banquet Fresco fragment (north wall).** Pull the small light switch
   beside it. Raking light reveals brush marks invisible under flat lighting.
3. **The bronze hand mirror (case 9).** Still faintly reflective after two
   thousand years of burial.

The fresco's palette - ochre, charcoal, a costly Egyptian blue - reappears
upstairs in a surprising place. Hold that thought until
[[z=3|the Impressionists]], who chased the very same blue.

**Where to go next:** continue east through the low archway into the Cloister.

@plane z=2 x=24 y=0 label="The Cloister Garden"
# Stop 3 - The Cloister Garden

You have stepped outside. This reconstructed cloister was shipped, stone by
numbered stone, from a dissolved abbey and rebuilt around a working herb garden.

- The covered walk on all four sides is original twelfth-century stonework.
- The herbs are planted by use: **culinary** along the south walk, **medicinal**
  along the north, **dye plants** (woad, madder, weld) in the east beds.
- Listen. The fountain at the center is fed by the same cistern that served the
  abbey kitchen.

This is the quietest stop on the tour and the best place to rest. The madder
beds, by the way, are the source of the red lake pigment you will see squeezed
straight from the tube [[z=3|in the next room]].

**Where to go next:** the glass doors on the east side return you indoors and
deliver you, blinking, into full daylight again.

@plane z=3 x=36 y=0 label="The Impressionist Room"
# Stop 4 - The Impressionist Room

The centerpiece of the museum. After the cloister's hush, this room is loud with
light. The north-facing skylights were rebuilt in 2019 to match the diffuse
studio light these painters prized.

## The three to study

| Work | Artist | Why it matters |
| --- | --- | --- |
| *Harbor at Dawn* | Berthe Vasseur | Note the unmixed strokes of orange and violet that blend only at viewing distance. Step back six paces. |
| *The Glasshouse* | Henri Lacombe | The Egyptian blue from [[z=1|the Antiquities fresco]] returns here as cobalt, two thousand years later. |
| *Madder Field* | Clara Reyes | Painted with the same madder-root red grown in [[z=2|the cloister beds]]. |

**A trick worth trying:** stand close enough to see the brushwork as raw paint,
then walk slowly backward. There is a distance, usually around six paces, where
the marks snap into a coherent scene. Each visitor's distance is slightly
different.

**Where to go next:** the final room, the Reading Terrace, is through the
curtained doorway on the east wall.

@plane z=4 x=48 y=0 label="The Reading Terrace"
# Stop 5 - The Reading Terrace

The tour ends where it is meant to: somewhere to sit and let it settle. This
glazed terrace overlooks the cloister garden you walked through earlier.

- **The reading wall** holds open-access folios. Handle them with the cotton
  gloves provided in the basket.
- **The view** frames [[z=2|the cloister]] from above. You can pick out the
  three herb zones by color from up here.
- **The guest book** is on the lectern by the exit. We read every entry.

## Before you leave

- The cafe is one floor down, reached by the west elevator near
  [[z=0|the atrium]] where you began.
- Members get a second-visit pass at the desk. The Impressionist Room rewards a
  return trip in different weather, when the skylights tell a different story.

Thank you for walking with us. Mind the step on your way out.

title: Ode to Joy - First Phrase by Measure
src:
---
3md: 1.0
axis: frame
title: Ode to Joy - First Phrase by Measure
key: D major
tempo: 120 BPM
meter: 4/4
clef: treble
composer: Ludwig van Beethoven (Symphony No. 9, 4th movement)
arrangement: single-line melody, public domain
---
This is the opening eight-bar phrase of the "Ode to Joy" theme, sliced one
measure per frame. Play the frames in order at 120 beats per minute and you get
the full antecedent-consequent period. Each plane carries the notes of one bar
(note name, scale degree, and beat), plus the lyric syllable that lands on it.

Frame timing: at 120 BPM a quarter note is 0.5 seconds, so each 4/4 measure
lasts 2.0 seconds. Frames 1 through 4 are the question (it ends unresolved on
the dominant); frames 5 through 8 are the answer (it lands home on the tonic).

@plane z=1 x=0 y=0 label="m.1 - Freu-de, schö-ner"
# Measure 1

```
beat:   1     2     3     4
note:   F#    F#    G     A
degree: 3     3     4     5
lyric:  Freu- de,  schö- ner
```

Four steady quarter notes. The tune does not start on the tonic D; it opens on
the third (F#) and walks up by step to A. That gentle rise is the whole hook.

Dynamics: **mf**, legato. Keep the quarters even, no rush into [[z=2|measure 2]].

@plane z=2 x=1 y=0 label="m.2 - Göt-ter-fun-ken"
# Measure 2

```
beat:   1     2     3     4
note:   A     G     F#    E
degree: 5     4     3     2
lyric:  Göt-  ter-  fun-  ken
```

A mirror of [[z=1|measure 1]]: it reaches the peak on A, then steps back down
through G and F# to E. Up four notes, down four notes - a tidy arch.

Watch the E on beat 4; it is the leading tension that leans toward the cadence
shape coming in [[z=3|measure 3]].

@plane z=3 x=2 y=0 label="m.3 - Toch-ter aus E-ly-"
# Measure 3

```
beat:   1     2     3     4
note:   E     E     F#    D
degree: 2     2     3     1
lyric:  Toch- ter   aus   E-
```

Here is the one rhythmic wrinkle most singers trip on. Beats 3 and 4 are NOT
two plain quarters: F# is a dotted quarter (1.5 beats) and D is an eighth note
(0.5 beats). Count it "one, two, three, four-and" with D landing on the "and".

The line finally touches the tonic D, but only in passing - the phrase is not
done. It pushes on to [[z=4|measure 4]].

@plane z=4 x=3 y=0 label="m.4 - si-um (half cadence)"
# Measure 4

```
beat:   1     2     3     4
note:   D     E     (E    -)
degree: 1     2      2
lyric:  ly-   si-    um
```

The antecedent closes. D rises to E, and the E is held for two beats (a half
note) on "-um". Ending on the second degree over a dominant chord is a **half
cadence**: it sounds like a comma, not a period.

Frames 1 through 4 asked the musical question. The answer begins at
[[z=5|measure 5]], which is an exact repeat of bar 1.

@plane z=5 x=0 y=1 label="m.5 - Wir be-tre-ten"
# Measure 5

```
beat:   1     2     3     4
note:   F#    F#    G     A
degree: 3     3     4     5
lyric:  Wir   be-   tre-  ten
```

The consequent phrase reuses the opening note-for-note. Compare it side by side
with [[z=1|measure 1]]: identical pitches and rhythm, new lyric. Beethoven gets
a whole second sentence almost for free by repeating the first.

@plane z=6 x=1 y=1 label="m.6 - feu-er-trun-ken"
# Measure 6

```
beat:   1     2     3     4
note:   A     G     F#    E
degree: 5     4     3     2
lyric:  feu-  er-   trun- ken
```

Again an exact echo, this time of [[z=2|measure 2]]: up to A, then the stepwise
descent back to E. The arch returns, setting up the only real difference, which
lands in [[z=7|measure 7]].

@plane z=7 x=2 y=1 label="m.7 - Him-mli-sche, dein"
# Measure 7

```
beat:   1     2     3     4
note:   E     E     F#    D
degree: 2     2     3     1
lyric:  Him-  mli-  sche, dein
```

Same dotted-quarter-then-eighth rhythm as [[z=3|measure 3]] (F# dotted, D as the
eighth on the "and" of 3). But now the melody is aiming for a real ending in
[[z=8|measure 8]] rather than the half cadence it took before.

@plane z=8 x=3 y=1 label="m.8 - Hei-lig-tum! (full cadence)"
# Measure 8

```
beat:   1     2     3     4
note:   D     C#    (D    -)
degree: 1     7      1
lyric:  Hei-  lig-  tum!
```

Home at last. C# (the leading tone, degree 7) resolves up to D held for two
beats - a **perfect authentic cadence** over the tonic chord. That is the period
finished: the open comma of [[z=4|measure 4]] is answered with a full stop here.

Loop back to [[z=1|measure 1]] to play the eight-bar theme again, or hand it to
the cellos an octave down the way the symphony does.

title: City Council Approves Riverside Flood Barrier
src:
---
3md: 1.0
axis: depth
title: City Council Approves Riverside Flood Barrier
byline: Maya Okonkwo, Staff Reporter
dateline: HARLOW BEND, June 23, 2026
section: Local Government
---
An inverted pyramid stacks a news story by importance. The shallowest plane
(`z=0`) holds the lede, the single sentence a reader skims first. Each deeper
plane carries detail that fewer readers will reach: context, supporting quotes,
and finally the dry procedural background that only the most committed reader
wants. Drop off at any depth and you still have the story.

@plane z=0 label="Lede" y=0
# Council approves $48M flood barrier

The Harlow Bend City Council voted 6-1 on Monday night to fund a $48 million
flood barrier along the Riverside district, ending a decade of stalled proposals
and clearing the way for construction to begin next spring.

For who needs more, the [[z=1|key facts]] follow. The full vote breakdown sits
[[z=4|at the bottom]].

@plane z=1 label="Key facts" y=1
## What was decided

- **The project:** A 2.3-mile concrete-and-earthen levee plus three pumping
  stations, designed to hold back a "hundred-year" flood crest of 31 feet.
- **The cost:** $48 million, split between a $30 million state grant and $18
  million in municipal bonds.
- **The timeline:** Design finalizes by October, groundbreaking in March 2027,
  completion targeted for late 2029.
- **The vote:** 6 in favor, 1 opposed (Council member Reyes), no abstentions.

The decision reverses years of inaction. See [[z=2|why it matters]] for the
flooding history that forced the council's hand.

@plane z=2 label="Why it matters" y=2
## Why it matters

Riverside has flooded four times since 2014, most severely in 2021 when the
river crested at 29.4 feet and forced the evacuation of roughly 1,200 residents.
That flood caused an estimated $62 million in property damage and left two of the
district's three schools closed for a full semester.

Insurers have since classified much of Riverside as high-risk, pushing annual
premiums up by as much as 40 percent and depressing home values across the
neighborhood. City planners argue the barrier will pay for itself within fifteen
years through avoided damage and recovered property-tax revenue.

The human stakes drew sharp testimony at the meeting, captured [[z=3|in the
voices below]].

@plane z=3 label="Reaction and quotes" y=3
## What people said

More than forty residents signed up to speak during the two-hour public comment
period.

> "I have rebuilt my kitchen twice. I am sixty-eight years old. I do not have a
> third rebuild in me," said Carla Mendes, a Riverside homeowner since 1989.

Mayor Tomás Whitfield framed the vote as overdue:

> "We have studied this river to death. Tonight we finally do something about
> it."

The lone dissenter, Council member Daniel Reyes, objected to the bond financing
rather than the barrier itself:

> "I support protecting Riverside. I cannot support putting $18 million on the
> city's credit card without a clearer plan to pay it back."

The mechanics of that financing, and what happens next, are detailed
[[z=4|in the procedural record]].

@plane z=4 label="Background and next steps" y=4
## The fine print

The $18 million municipal bond will be repaid over 20 years through a dedicated
stormwater fee added to Riverside-area water bills, averaging an estimated $7.50
per household per month. The state grant, awarded in April under the Coastal and
Riverine Resilience Program, requires the city to break ground within 24 months
or forfeit the funds, a deadline that shaped Monday's urgency.

**Next steps:**

1. The city engineer submits final designs to the state by October 15.
2. A public works contract goes out to bid in December.
3. The council must ratify the winning bid in a second vote, expected in
   February 2027.

The full text of Resolution 2026-114 is available on the city clerk's website.
A previous version of this barrier, proposed in 2016 at $22 million, died in
committee after a dispute over easements with the Harlow Bend Rail Authority,
which the current plan resolves by routing the levee behind the rail yard.

Return to [[z=0|the top]] for the summary.

title: Meridian Tower - Building Directory
src:
---
3md: 1.0
axis: floor
title: Meridian Tower - Building Directory
building: Meridian Tower, 410 Harbor Street
hours: Lobby staffed 6:00 AM to 9:00 PM, Mon-Fri
emergency: Dial 0 from any house phone, or 911
---
Welcome to Meridian Tower. This directory is organized by floor, from the
ground-level lobby up to the rooftop terrace. Take Elevator Bank A (floors 1-6)
or Bank B (floors 5 and up). Visitors must check in at the lobby desk on
[[z=1|floor 1]] before proceeding.

| 110 | Package Room | FedEx, UPS, USPS drop and pickup |
| 115 | Bike Storage | Key-fob access only |

**Getting around**
- Elevator Bank A is straight ahead past the security desk.
- Elevator Bank B is to the left, behind the cafe.
- Restrooms are off the corridor to the right of reception.

All visitors receive a day badge here. For tenant amenities and the fitness
center, continue to [[z=2|floor 2]]. Building management is on [[z=6|floor 6]].

**Conference rooms (half-day blocks)**
- Cypress (seats 12, video wall)
- Alder (seats 8, whiteboard walls)

Need to reserve the Town Hall or a conference room? Contact building management
on [[z=6|floor 6]]. Tenant offices begin on [[z=3|floor 3]].

Single-tenant floor. Data and research firm.

| Area | Team | Contact |
|------|------|---------|
| North wing | Reception & visitor lounge | x3100 |
| East wing | Data Engineering | x3120 |
| West wing | Research & Modeling | x3140 |
| South wing | Sales & Client Success | x3160 |

**Visitor notes**
- Check in at the floor 3 reception desk (turn left off the elevator).
- Guest Wi-Fi password is printed on the lounge placards.
- The pantry and quiet phone booths are along the east corridor.

Shipping and deliveries route through the [[z=1|floor 1 package room]]. The
shared Town Hall for all-hands meetings is on [[z=2|floor 2]].

@plane z=4 label="Lumen Design Co." x=0 y=0
# Floor 4 - Lumen Design Co.

Single-tenant floor. Product and brand design studio.

| Area | Team | Contact |
|------|------|---------|
| Studio floor | Open design bays | x4100 |
| Gallery | Client presentations | x4110 |
| Lab | Prototyping & 3D printing | x4130 |
| Library | Materials & samples | x4150 |

**Visitor notes**
- The gallery entrance is directly across from the elevators.
- Prototyping lab access is restricted; ask a host to escort you.
- Coffee bar is open to guests in the studio's front lounge.

For larger client events, Lumen books the [[z=2|floor 2 Town Hall]]. Catering
deliveries should be staged at the [[z=1|lobby package room]].

@plane z=5 label="Coworking - The Loft" x=0 y=0
# Floor 5 - The Loft (Coworking)

Flexible memberships, hot desks, and private suites. Both elevator banks stop
here.

| Zone | Use | Booking |
|------|-----|---------|
| Open commons | Hot desks (first come) | Drop-in |
| Suites 501-512 | Private offices (1-6 people) | Monthly |
| Focus pods | Solo deep-work booths | Hourly, in app |
| Kitchen | Shared, stocked weekday mornings | Members |

**Day-pass guests**
- Sign in at the Loft front desk, suite 500.
- Printing is in the alcove behind the kitchen.
- Rooftop access for members is one floor up.

Day passes are issued after lobby check-in on [[z=1|floor 1]]. Members head up to
the [[z=6|rooftop terrace on floor 6]] for events.

@plane z=6 label="Management & Rooftop" x=0 y=0
# Floor 6 - Management & Rooftop Terrace

Building operations and the shared rooftop.

| Suite | Office | Contact |

| 605 | Facilities & Engineering | x6010 |
| 610 | Leasing Office | x6020 |
| Terrace | Rooftop event space & garden | Reserve via management |

**Rooftop terrace**
- Open to all tenants 8:00 AM - 8:00 PM (weather permitting).
- Private event bookings handled by management in suite 600.
- Grills and the covered pavilion require an advance reservation.

This is the top of the directory. To return to the entrance and visitor desk,
go back to [[z=1|floor 1]].

title: Progressive Product Onboarding
src:
---
3md: 1.0
axis: depth
title: Progressive Product Onboarding
product: Cadence (team scheduling app)
audience: new workspace owner
goal: first scheduled event within 10 minutes
strategy: surface one decision per depth layer, defer the rest
---
Onboarding stacked by depth. The front plane is what the new user sees the
instant they sign in: one screen, one action, nothing else competing for
attention. Each plane behind it holds the next thing we reveal, but only after
the user is ready for it. Nothing in the back planes is shown up front; depth is
how we defer complexity instead of dumping it.

Read front to back. The closest plane (z=0) is the empty workspace. The deepest
plane (z=4) is full power-user territory most people never need on day one.

Start at the [[z=0|first screen]] and only descend when the user earns the next
layer by completing the one in front of it.

@plane z=0 x=0 y=0 label="First screen (empty workspace)"
# Layer 0: The First Screen

The user just confirmed their email and landed in an empty workspace. This is
the highest-stakes moment, so the screen does exactly one thing.

## What is visible

- A single headline: "Let's schedule your first event."
- One primary button: **Create event**
- One muted skip link: "I'll explore on my own"

That is the entire screen. No sidebar, no settings, no team invites, no billing.

## Why only one action

The empty state is where people bounce. A workspace with zero events has nothing
to look at, so an empty dashboard reads as "broken" rather than "new." Replacing
the dashboard with a single call to action turns confusion into momentum.

## The one rule for this layer

> Do not show a feature until the user has a reason to care about it. On an empty
> workspace, the only thing they can care about is creating something.

When they click **Create event**, we reveal the next layer of depth: the
[[z=1|guided event builder]]. If they skip, we still drop them at
[[z=1|the builder]] but with a dismissable tip instead of a forced flow.

@plane z=1 x=0 y=0 label="Guided first event"
# Layer 1: Guided First Event

The user committed to creating something. Now we walk them through it with
smart defaults so the first event takes seconds, not minutes.

## Pre-filled so nothing is blank

| Field    | Default we provide          | Why                                  |
|----------|-----------------------------|--------------------------------------|
| Title    | "Team sync"                 | Most common first event             |
| Duration | 30 minutes                  | Safe, editable, one tap to change   |
| When     | Tomorrow, 10:00 local time  | Avoids "right now" scheduling panic |
| Invitees | Empty, with a hint to add   | The only field we ask them to touch |

The user can accept every default and ship a real event with a single tap on
**Schedule**, or edit any field inline.

## The teaching moment

As they hover the **When** field, a small inline note appears:

- "Cadence reads your working hours and never books outside them."

This is the first time we mention working hours, and we mention it exactly when
it becomes relevant, not in a settings tour up front.

## After they schedule

The event lands on a real calendar and we surface a single follow-up:

> Nice. Want Cadence to find times automatically next time? Turn on
> [[z=2|availability rules]].

That link is the doorway to the next depth layer. Most users tap it within their
first session because they just felt the pain of picking a time by hand.

@plane z=2 x=0 y=0 label="Availability rules"
# Layer 2: Availability Rules

This layer appears only after the first event exists. Now the user has felt the
manual flow and is ready to automate it. We introduce one new concept: rules
that describe when they are bookable.

## What we ask for, in order

1. **Working hours.** "When are you usually available?" Pre-set to 9:00 to 17:00,
   Monday through Friday. Two taps to adjust.
2. **Buffer time.** "How long between meetings?" Default 10 minutes. One slider.
3. **Daily limit.** "Most meetings per day?" Default unlimited, optional cap.

Each question is its own card. The user answers one, it slides away, the next
appears. We never show all three at once.

## Connecting back

These rules quietly upgrade the experience the user already knows. The next time
they open the [[z=1|event builder]], the **When** field stops suggesting a fixed
slot and starts suggesting only times that fit these rules.

## The payoff line

> You set this up once. From now on, Cadence only offers times that work for you.

When availability rules are saved, and only then, we unlock sharing: the user can
hand out a [[z=3|booking link]] so other people schedule against these rules.

@plane z=3 x=0 y=0 label="Shareable booking link"
# Layer 3: Shareable Booking Link

Depth here means power that depends on everything in front of it. A booking link
is useless without availability rules, so we gated it behind
[[z=2|Layer 2]] on purpose.

## The reveal

A new panel slides in with a ready-to-copy link:

```
cadence.app/u/your-handle
```

Below it, a one-line explanation:

- "Send this to anyone. They pick a time, Cadence books it inside your rules,
  and it never double-books you."

## One optional refinement

We offer a single advanced toggle, collapsed by default:

- **Event types.** Split the link into "15 min intro," "30 min review," and
  "60 min deep dive," each with its own duration and rules.

Most users leave this collapsed. Power users expand it. That choice is theirs to
make later, which is exactly why it lives this deep.

## Where it leads

Once a real person books through the link, the user has completed the core loop:
create, automate, share, receive. At that point we stop teaching and step back,
surfacing the rest of the product only through the [[z=4|reference layer]] when
they go looking for it.

@plane z=4 x=0 y=0 label="Reference and power features"
# Layer 4: Reference Layer (Pull, Not Push)

The deepest plane is everything we deliberately did not teach. It exists, it is
searchable, but it is never pushed into the new user's face. They reach it by
pulling: opening the command bar, searching help, or clicking into settings on
their own schedule.

## What lives back here

- **Team scheduling.** Round-robin assignment, shared team links, load
  balancing across colleagues.
- **Integrations.** Two-way sync with external calendars, video links auto
  attached, CRM and payment hooks.
- **Workflows.** Reminder sequences, follow-up emails, no-show handling, custom
  intake questions.
- **Analytics.** Booking rates, busiest hours, time reclaimed per week.

## The principle that put these here

> Discoverable on demand, invisible by default. A feature in the reference layer
> earns its way forward only when the user's behavior signals they need it.

For example, if Cadence notices a user manually copying the same
[[z=3|booking link]] to five teammates, it can offer to promote team scheduling
from this layer up to the surface. The depth never collapses on its own; the
product just raises a feature when the data says it is finally relevant.

## Closing the loop

A user who never descends past [[z=1|the first event]] still has a working
product. A user who reaches this layer has grown into it one decision at a time.
That gradient, front to back, is the whole point of progressive onboarding.

title: Order Fulfillment Pipeline
src:
---
3md: 1.0
axis: status
title: Order Fulfillment Pipeline
order: "#48217"
sla: "ship within 24h of payment capture"
warehouse: BUF-1 (Buffalo, NY)
---
Lifecycle of a single customer order as it moves through the fulfillment
pipeline. Each plane is one status; an order advances along the Z axis from
left (intake) to right (delivered). The `x` offset spaces the stages out for a
left-to-right board view, and the `y` offset nudges exception states below the
happy path.

Start at [[z=0|Placed]] and follow the line to [[z=4|Delivered]].

@plane z=0 x=0 y=0 label="Placed"
# Placed

Order **#48217** created by customer at 2026-06-23 09:14 ET.

| Field | Value |
|-------|-------|
| Items | 3 SKUs, 5 units |
| Subtotal | $142.00 |
| Shipping | Standard ground (3-5 day) |
| Destination | Austin, TX 78701 |

## What happens here

- Cart is converted to an order record and an idempotency key is minted.
- Inventory is **soft-reserved** for 30 minutes so two carts cannot claim the
  last unit.
- A fraud pre-score runs; anything above 0.80 is flagged for manual review.

Payment has not been captured yet. If the authorization fails, the order never
leaves this state and the soft reservation expires on its own.

Next stop: [[z=1|Paid]].

@plane z=1 x=3 y=0 label="Paid"
# Paid

Authorization captured at 2026-06-23 09:14:48 ET. The 24h ship SLA clock
starts now.

## Ledger entry

```
charge   txn_9f2a   $142.00   captured
tax      TX state   $11.72    collected
total                $153.72
```

## Gate checks before release

- [x] Payment captured (not just authorized)
- [x] Fraud score 0.31 (under the 0.80 threshold)
- [x] Address validated against USPS, deliverable
- [ ] Stock confirmed at a single warehouse

The soft reservation from [[z=0|Placed]] is now converted to a **hard
allocation**. If any line cannot be allocated, the order branches to the
backorder handler instead of advancing.

@plane z=2 x=6 y=0 label="Picked & Packed"
# Picked and Packed

Work released to warehouse **BUF-1** as pick task `PT-55129`.

## Pick list

1. `SKU-204` Ceramic pour-over dripper - bin A12 - qty 1
2. `SKU-118` Burr grinder, hand crank - bin C07 - qty 1
3. `SKU-061` House blend, 12oz bag - bin D33 - qty 3

Picker scans each bin and unit; a mismatch halts the task rather than shipping
the wrong item. Packed into a single 12x9x6 box, 4.3 lb gross.

## Carton contents

- Packing slip (no prices, per gift-order flag)
- Items above, void-fill on top
- Return QR label tucked inside the flap

Ready for carrier handoff. See [[z=3|Shipped]].

@plane z=3 x=9 y=0 label="Shipped"
# Shipped

Carrier manifested the parcel and the tracking number went live.

| Field | Value |
|-------|-------|
| Carrier | UPS Ground |
| Tracking | 1Z999AA10123456784 |
| Picked up | 2026-06-23 17:40 ET |
| Est. delivery | 2026-06-26 by 9:00 PM |

## Customer-facing events

- Shipment confirmation email sent with the tracking link.
- Webhook `order.shipped` fired to the storefront and the reviews service.
- SLA met: shipped 8h 25m after payment, well under the 24h target.

If the carrier reports a lost or damaged scan, the order drops to the
exception lane at [[z=2.5|Exception: Reship]] for a replacement.

@plane z=4 x=12 y=0 label="Delivered"
# Delivered

Marked delivered at 2026-06-26 14:02 ET, left at front door, photo on file.

## Post-delivery automation

- `order.delivered` webhook closes the SLA case as **on time**.
- A review request is scheduled for 3 days out (2026-06-29).
- The return window opens: 30 days, until 2026-07-26.

## Final ledger

```
revenue recognized   $142.00
shipping recognized   included
status                CLOSED
```

Nothing further is required unless the customer opens a return, which would
re-open a reverse-logistics flow outside this pipeline.

@plane z=2.5 x=9 y=-3 label="Exception: Reship"
# Exception: Reship

The off-path lane for parcels that are lost, damaged, or refused after
[[z=3|Shipped]].

## Trigger conditions

- No carrier scan for 4 days after pickup, **or**
- Carrier event `damaged` / `returned_to_sender`, **or**
- Customer reports non-delivery within the SLA window.

## Resolution playbook

1. Open a claim with the carrier (does not block the customer).
2. Re-allocate stock and create a **zero-charge** replacement order.
3. The replacement re-enters the happy path at [[z=2|Picked & Packed]].
4. Notify the customer with the new tracking number once it is live.

Only after the replacement is delivered does the original order reach
[[z=4|Delivered]] and close.

title: Folding the Orizuru (Paper Crane)
src:
---
3md: 1.0
axis: step
title: Folding the Orizuru (Paper Crane)
paper: 15cm x 15cm kami, color side down
difficulty: intermediate
folds: 22
---
The orizuru is the classic Japanese paper crane. Start with a square sheet,
color side facing down on the table. Each plane below is one stage in the
sequence; crease every fold sharply with a fingernail or bone folder before
moving on. Begin at [[z=0|the bird base]] and work upward to the finished crane.

@plane z=0 label="Bird base" x=0 y=0
# Step 1 - The bird base

Everything starts from the bird base, the most reused base in origami.

1. Fold the square in half along both diagonals, then unfold. You now have an X.
2. Flip the paper over. Fold in half left-to-right and top-to-bottom, then unfold.
   These are valley folds on one side, mountain folds on the other.
3. Using the existing creases, collapse the sheet into a **preliminary base**: a
   small square with four free flaps, opening downward.
4. Lift one lower edge of the top layer to the center crease and squash it into a
   narrow kite, forming a petal fold. Repeat on all four flaps, front and back.

The result is a slim base with two long points (the future neck and tail) and
two shorter points (the future wings). Next, narrow the body in
[[z=1|the diamond fold]].

@plane z=1 label="Diamond fold" x=1 y=0
# Step 2 - Narrow to a diamond

The bird base is still wide at the bottom. Tuck those edges in.

- Orient the base so the two long open points face down toward you.
- On the top layer, fold the lower-left and lower-right raw edges in to meet the
  vertical center line. The shape tightens into a kite, then a slim diamond.
- Flip the model over and repeat the same two folds on the back.

Crease hard. These four folds set the body width, so keep the edges flush with
the center line. The two skinny bottom points are what you will shape in
[[z=2|the neck and tail]].

@plane z=2 label="Neck and tail" x=2 y=0
# Step 3 - Inside reverse folds

Now define which point is the neck and which is the tail.

1. Take the two long, narrow points at the bottom and fold them up as high as you
   like, then unfold. This crease is the pivot for the reverse fold.
2. Perform an **inside reverse fold** on each point: open the point slightly,
   invert the crease so the paper swings up between the two body layers, and
   flatten. Both points now stand upright.

Set them at a gentle angle, one leaning forward for the neck and one back for the
tail. A reverse fold hides the raw end inside the body, which is why the crane
looks clean from every side. The head comes next in
[[z=3|the head and wings]].

@plane z=3 label="Head and wings" x=3 y=0
# Step 4 - The head and the spread

Time to finish the bird.

- **Head:** near the tip of the neck point, make one more small inside reverse
  fold to bend it forward into a beak. Aim the beak slightly downward so it reads
  as a crane and not a swan.
- **Wings:** find the two wide flaps on the sides of the body. Fold each one down
  so it lies flat and horizontal, creasing along the top edge of the body.

The folding is done. To bring the crane to life, hold the base of both wings and
gently pull them apart while the body puffs open from underneath. The back
inflates into a rounded body and the wings spread wide.

Stuck on a point? Jump back to [[z=2|the neck and tail step]] and recheck the
reverse folds before re-spreading the wings.

title: Sparse Attention Routing for Long-Context Transformers
src:
---
3md: 1.0
axis: depth
title: Sparse Attention Routing for Long-Context Transformers
authors: A. Okonkwo, R. Mehta, L. Bjornsdottir
venue: Preprint, under review
keywords: long-context, sparse attention, routing, transformers
---
Read this paper by depth. The surface plane is the abstract: the one-paragraph
claim a busy reader skims. Each deeper plane unpacks that claim into more
detail, ending at the reproducible methods a reviewer needs. Drill down with the
cross-plane links, or surface back up to the abstract at any time.

@plane z=0 label="Abstract"
# Abstract

Transformers degrade on long inputs because full self-attention costs grow
quadratically with sequence length, forcing a hard cap on context. We present
**Sparse Attention Routing (SAR)**, a learned router that selects, per query, a
small set of key blocks to attend to, reducing attention cost from O(n^2) to
roughly O(n * sqrt(n)) while keeping the model end-to-end differentiable. On
the PG-19 and arXiv-Long benchmarks, SAR matches dense attention within 0.3
perplexity at 16k tokens and extends usable context to 128k tokens on a single
80 GB GPU. The router adds 1.8% parameters and is trained jointly with the base
model, with no separate retrieval index.

Skim deeper for the framing in [[z=1|the introduction]], the precise mechanism
in [[z=2|the method]], or jump straight to [[z=4|reproducible methods]].

@plane z=1 label="Introduction" x="0" y="-1"
# 1. Introduction

Long-context modeling is bottlenecked by attention, not by parameters. A
12-layer model attending over 64k tokens spends more compute moving keys and
values than it spends in its feed-forward blocks. Existing remedies fall into
three camps, each with a cost:

- **Fixed sparsity** (strided, block-local, dilated windows) is cheap but blind:
  the pattern is chosen before training and cannot adapt to the input.
- **Retrieval augmentation** sidesteps attention with an external index, but the
  index is trained separately and is hard to keep consistent with the model.
- **Linear-attention approximations** lower the asymptotic cost but trail dense
  attention in quality on tasks that need sharp, content-based lookups.

Our claim, stated plainly in [[z=0|the abstract]], is that the selection of what
to attend to can itself be learned cheaply and trained jointly with the model.
The next plane defines exactly how. See [[z=2|the method]].

@plane z=2 label="Method: Sparse Attention Routing"
# 2. Method

Let a sequence of n tokens be partitioned into b contiguous **blocks** of size
s, so b = ceil(n / s). For each query position i, instead of attending to all n
keys, SAR attends to the keys in a chosen subset of blocks.

## 2.1 The router

Each block j produces a summary vector m_j, the mean of its key projections. A
lightweight router scores query i against every block summary:

```
score(i, j) = (q_i . W_r m_j) / sqrt(d)
```

The router keeps the top-k blocks per query (we use k = 8). Attention then runs
densely, but only over the k * s keys in those blocks, so cost scales with
k * s rather than n. Choosing s ~ sqrt(n) gives the O(n * sqrt(n)) figure quoted
in [[z=0|the abstract]].

## 2.2 Keeping it differentiable

Hard top-k is not differentiable, so during training we relax it: we attend over
the top-k blocks but weight each by a softmax over its router score, and we add a
small load-balancing loss so no block is starved. At inference the relaxation is
dropped and hard top-k is used. The deeper plane covers why this matters
empirically. See [[z=3|design rationale]].

@plane z=3 label="Design rationale" x="1" y="0"
# 3. Why these choices

**Why block-level, not token-level, routing?** Token-level selection multiplies
the router cost by n and produces a noisier signal. Block summaries are 64x
cheaper to score (at s = 64) and, because nearby tokens are correlated, lose
little recall.

**Why a load-balancing loss?** Without it, the router collapses early in
training, sending every query to the same two or three high-norm blocks. The
auxiliary loss (the squared coefficient of variation of block-selection
frequency, weighted at 0.01) keeps utilization even and recovers 0.4 perplexity.

**Why train jointly rather than distill?** A router trained against a frozen
teacher inherits the teacher's attention habits and cannot exploit the sparsity
budget. Joint training lets the base model adapt its representations so that the
information a query needs is concentrated in few blocks. The exact recipe is in
[[z=4|reproducible methods]].

@plane z=4 label="Reproducible methods" x="0" y="1"
# 4. Reproducible methods

Everything needed to reproduce the headline numbers in [[z=0|the abstract]].

## 4.1 Data

- **PG-19**: Project Gutenberg books pre-1919, official train/validation split,
  documents concatenated and chunked to the target context length.
- **arXiv-Long**: full-text arXiv papers (LaTeX source stripped to plain text),
  filtered to documents of at least 12k tokens, 90/5/5 split by paper id.

## 4.2 Model and training

- Base: 1.3B-parameter decoder, 24 layers, hidden size 2048, 16 heads.
- Block size s = 64, top-k blocks per query k = 8, router rank d_r = 128.
- Optimizer: AdamW, peak learning rate 3e-4, cosine decay, 2000 warmup steps,
  weight decay 0.1, gradient clip 1.0.
- Schedule: 100k steps at 16k context, then 20k steps at 64k context.
- Hardware: 8x A100 80 GB, bf16, activation checkpointing on attention blocks.
- Load-balancing loss weight 0.01; router relaxation temperature annealed from
  1.0 to 0.1 over the first 10k steps.

## 4.3 Evaluation

Perplexity is computed on held-out documents at each context length with a
sliding window of stride 512. The 128k-token result uses hard top-k inference
with KV-cache eviction of unselected blocks. Code, configs, and the exact
evaluation harness are released under Apache-2.0.

Surface back to [[z=0|the abstract]] or revisit [[z=2|the method]].

title: Cedar Street Garage - Levels P1 to P5
src:
---
3md: 1.0
axis: floor
title: Cedar Street Garage - Levels P1 to P5
structure: Cedar Street Municipal Parking
spaces: 612
clearance: 6 ft 8 in
operator: City Mobility Authority
---
Five-level municipal garage on Cedar Street. The Z axis is the floor: P1 (street
level) at the bottom, the roof deck at the top. Cross-plane links follow the
natural flow a driver takes hunting for an open space, from the full lower decks
up toward the open roof.

@plane z=1 x=0 y=0 label="P1 - Street Level"
# P1 - Street Level (Entry)

**Spaces:** 96 | **Typical fill by 8:30 AM:** 100%

Entry gate and ticket spitter are at the southeast corner off Cedar Street.
Exit gate and the cashier booth are at the southwest corner onto 4th Avenue.

## Reserved zones
- Bays 1 to 12: monthly permit holders only (license plate enforced)
- Bays 13 to 18: 15-minute loading, validated for the pharmacy
- Bays 19 to 22: ADA accessible, nearest the street elevator

## Notes
P1 fills first and stays full. If the gate sign reads LOT FULL P1-P2, keep the
ramp and head straight to [[z=3|P3]], which is where mid-morning arrivals
usually find their first real opening. Bike racks are mounted on the north wall
past the stairwell.

@plane z=2 x=0 y=0 label="P2 - Compact and EV"
# P2 - Compact Cars and EV Charging

**Spaces:** 128 | **Typical fill by 9:00 AM:** 95%

Reached by the helical ramp at the northeast corner. The whole east row is
sized for compacts, so full-size pickups should continue up to [[z=4|P4]] where
the bays run wider.

## EV charging
- 8 Level 2 chargers (J1772) along the west wall, bays 40 to 47
- 2 DC fast chargers (CCS) at bays 48 and 49, 30-minute session cap
- Idle fee of $0.40 per minute starts 10 minutes after a full charge

## Wayfinding
Color code on this level is **amber**. The pedestrian door at the south end
connects to the skybridge over Cedar Street. Stairwell B is the fastest path
down to the [[z=1|street exit]].

@plane z=3 x=0 y=0 label="P3 - General Parking"
# P3 - General Parking (Best Odds Midday)

**Spaces:** 132 | **Typical fill by 9:00 AM:** 70%

The sweet spot. P3 is the first level where a driver arriving after the morning
rush reliably finds a space without circling. Color code is **green**.

## Layout
- Two-way central aisle, head-in 90-degree bays both sides
- North end: cart corral and a covered waiting bench
- South end: restrooms and the parking-payment kiosk (card and mobile only)

## If P3 is full
It rarely is before noon. When it is, the open spaces are one ramp up on
[[z=4|P4]], and the roof deck on [[z=5|P5]] is almost always wide open. Heading
out instead? Take Stairwell A straight down to the [[z=1|cashier and exit gate]].

@plane z=4 x=0 y=0 label="P4 - Oversize and Vans"
# P4 - Oversize Vehicles, Vans, and Trucks

**Spaces:** 104 | **Typical fill by noon:** 55%

Wider bays (9 ft 6 in) and a taller posted clearance of 7 ft 2 in on this
level, so vehicles too tall for the lower decks belong here. Color code is
**blue**.

## Made for big vehicles
- Bays 70 to 79: extended-length, fit full-size pickups with a bed cap
- Bays 80 to 83: van-accessible ADA spots with 8 ft access aisles
- No overhead pipes across the center two rows (the tallest clear span)

## Heads up
Cargo vans over 7 ft 2 in must use the surface lot on 4th Avenue, not this
garage. Need a charger instead? They are two levels down on [[z=2|P2]]. The
nearest open standard spaces are usually right above on [[z=5|the roof deck]].

@plane z=5 x=0 y=0 label="P5 - Roof Deck"
# P5 - Roof Deck (Open Air)

**Spaces:** 152 | **Typical fill:** rarely above 60%

Open-air top level with no height limit, the only deck that fits anything tall.
Plenty of space almost any time of day, with a clear view of the river to the
east. Color code is **white**.

## Roof-specific notes
- Surface is uncovered: expect snow and ice clearing delays after storms
- Solar canopy over rows 1 and 2 gives the only shaded spots
- Drainage runs to the southwest scupper; avoid the low corner in heavy rain

## Getting back down
Both elevators and all three stairwells serve the roof. For the fastest exit,
the express elevator at the core drops straight to the [[z=1|street-level
cashier]]. Looking for a charger before you leave? Stop on [[z=2|P2]] on the
way down.

title: Cedar Ridge Garage - Section Map
src:
---
3md: 1.0
axis: space
title: Cedar Ridge Garage - Section Map
facility: Cedar Ridge Parking Structure
levels: "5"
total_spaces: "612"
clearance_m: "2.1"
units: metric
---
A walk-through of the Cedar Ridge garage, one plane per level along the Z (space)
axis. Z increases as you go up: P1 is the ground entrance, P5 is the rooftop. The
in-plane x and y offsets track roughly where each level's elevator core and ramp
sit, since the ramp shifts north as it climbs.

Start at the [[z=1|ground level]] entrance, follow the spiral ramp up, and exit
from the [[z=5|rooftop]] or back down past the [[z=1|exit gate]].

@plane z=1 x=0 y=0 label="P1 - Ground / Entry"
# P1 - Ground Level (Entry)

The street-level deck. Both the entry and exit gates live here, so every trip
begins and ends on this plane.

## Section breakdown

| Section | Spaces | Type                 | Notes                          |
|---------|--------|----------------------|--------------------------------|
| 1A      | 18     | Accessible + Family  | Nearest the lobby, van-width   |
| 1B      | 44     | Standard             | Faces the entry lane           |
| 1C      | 30     | EV charging (Level 2)| 6 stalls metered, 24 standard  |
| 1D      | 22     | Compact only         | Tight bay near the exit gate   |

- **Entry gate:** northwest corner, ticket dispenser and license-plate camera
- **Exit gate:** southwest corner, pay-on-foot kiosk 10 m before the arm
- **Clearance:** 2.1 m at the entry beam (oversized vehicles use the surface lot)
- **Elevator core:** centered, doors face section 1A

The ramp to the next level rises along the east wall. Follow it up to
[[z=2|P2 - Blue]]. Returning drivers come down the same spiral and queue at the
exit gate here.

@plane z=2 x=0 y=8 label="P2 - Blue"
# P2 - Blue Level

First level above grade. Painted columns are blue end to end, so a lost driver
only has to remember a color. This is the busiest commuter level because it is one
short ramp from the [[z=1|exit gate]].

## Section breakdown

| Section | Spaces | Type        | Reserved?                    |
|---------|--------|-------------|------------------------------|
| 2A      | 52     | Standard    | No                           |
| 2B      | 48     | Standard    | Monthly permit, columns 5-12 |
| 2C      | 26     | Compact     | No                           |

- **Fill pattern:** typically full by 08:30 on weekdays
- **Color cue:** blue columns, blue floor numerals at each elevator door
- **Pillar count:** 24 numbered columns, odd on the north run, even on the south

> Wayfinding: if the blue level is full, the overhead counter at the ramp mouth
> sends you straight up to [[z=3|P3 - Green]] without stopping.

The spiral ramp continues up the east wall to [[z=3|P3 - Green]].

@plane z=3 x=0 y=16 label="P3 - Green"
# P3 - Green Level

Mid-stack level, green columns. This is the overflow level for the blue commuters
and the default for visitor parking validated at the lobby desk.

## Section breakdown

| Section | Spaces | Type             | Notes                       |
|---------|--------|------------------|-----------------------------|
| 3A      | 50     | Standard         | Visitor validation accepted |
| 3B      | 50     | Standard         | Visitor validation accepted |
| 3C      | 14     | Motorcycle bays  | Half-width, no SUV          |

- **Color cue:** green columns, green arrows on the deck
- **Visitor desk link:** validated tickets get the first 2 hours free here
- **Restroom:** elevator lobby, north side (only level with one besides P1)

Headed for the long-stay deck or the roof? Keep climbing past
[[z=4|P4 - Amber]]. Visitors leaving within 2 hours should drop straight to the
[[z=1|exit gate]] before validation expires.

@plane z=4 x=0 y=24 label="P4 - Amber"
# P4 - Amber Level

Long-stay level, amber columns. Quietest deck during the day, which makes it the
reservation and airport-park-and-fly tier. Rates here are flat per 24 hours.

## Section breakdown

| Section | Spaces | Type                  | Notes                       |
|---------|--------|-----------------------|-----------------------------|
| 4A      | 60     | Reserved (app booking)| QR scan at the bay entrance |
| 4B      | 34     | Standard long-stay    | Flat daily rate             |
| 4C      | 6      | Oversize / van        | 2.4 m clearance, north wall |

- **Color cue:** amber columns, amber floor numerals
- **Booking:** sections 4A bays open only to a scanned reservation QR code
- **Security:** extra camera coverage, call point at each elevator door

One more turn of the ramp reaches the open-air [[z=5|rooftop]]. To leave, the
spiral runs all the way back down to the [[z=1|exit gate]].

@plane z=5 x=0 y=32 label="P5 - Rooftop"
# P5 - Rooftop (Open Air)

The top deck, open to the sky. No clearance limit, so this is where trucks,
roof-box vehicles, and trailers go. Spaces are wider and the surface is graded for
drainage.

## Section breakdown

| Section | Spaces | Type                   | Notes                        |
|---------|--------|------------------------|------------------------------|
| 5A      | 28     | Oversize / truck       | Pull-through bays            |
| 5B      | 6      | EV fast charge (DC)    | Solar canopy, 150 kW         |

- **Surface:** weather-exposed, sealed concrete, painted in high-visibility white
- **Solar canopy:** covers section 5B and feeds the DC fast chargers
- **Wind note:** roof box and bike-rack vehicles should park nose-in along 5A

> Sunset view aside, remember the only way down is the spiral ramp. Allow an extra
> few minutes at peak; the queue forms back at the [[z=1|exit gate]] on the ground
> level.

title: Swinging Pendulum
src:
---
3md: 1.0
axis: frame
title: Swinging Pendulum
fps: 12
loop: ping-pong
length: 0.95 m
amplitude: 18 deg
---
A six-frame loop of a simple gravity pendulum released from the left.
Frames advance left to right, then play back in reverse to complete the swing.
The bob accelerates toward the bottom and decelerates at each turning point,
so the spacing between extreme frames is wide and the spacing near the
bottom is tight.

Pivot sits at the top center of every frame. Read the loop in order:
[[z=0|far left]] to [[z=2|bottom]] to [[z=4|far right]], then the viewer
reverses back through [[z=2|bottom]] again.

@plane z=0 x=-18 y=0 label="Frame 0: left extreme"
# Frame 0 - Left turning point

The bob hangs at its leftmost angle, +18 degrees from vertical.

- Velocity: 0 (instantaneous rest)
- Acceleration: maximum, pointing back toward center
- String drawn taut up and to the left of the pivot

```
   o      <- pivot
  /
 /
(@)       <- bob, far left
```

This is the start of the cycle. The next sampled position is
[[z=1|frame 1]], where the bob has begun to fall.

@plane z=1 x=-10 y=-6 label="Frame 1: falling left"
# Frame 1 - Descending from the left

Angle is now about +10 degrees. The bob has dropped and picked up speed.

- Velocity: rising (roughly half of peak)
- Acceleration: still inward, but smaller than at the extreme
- Notice the bob has moved closer to the bottom than frame 0 moved from rest

```
   o
   |\
   | \
   (@)
```

Gravity is doing positive work here. Continue to [[z=2|frame 2]] for the
fastest point of the swing.

@plane z=2 x=0 y=-9 label="Frame 2: bottom (max speed)"
# Frame 2 - Bottom of the arc

Angle is 0 degrees, the bob is directly below the pivot.

- Velocity: maximum of the whole swing
- Acceleration (tangential): 0, the string is vertical
- All of the height lost since [[z=0|frame 0]] is now kinetic energy

```
   o
   |
   |
   |
  (@)      <- straight down, moving fast
```

From here the bob climbs the right side and starts to slow. See
[[z=3|frame 3]].

@plane z=3 x=10 y=-6 label="Frame 3: rising right"
# Frame 3 - Climbing the right side

Angle is about -10 degrees. Mirror image of [[z=1|frame 1]], but moving
the other way.

- Velocity: falling (decelerating)
- Acceleration: inward again, now pointing back toward center from the right
- Kinetic energy is converting back into height

```
   o
  /|
 / |
   (@)
```

One more frame to the right extreme: [[z=4|frame 4]].

@plane z=4 x=18 y=0 label="Frame 4: right extreme"
# Frame 4 - Right turning point

Angle is -18 degrees, the mirror of [[z=0|frame 0]].

- Velocity: 0 (instantaneous rest)
- Acceleration: maximum, pointing back toward center
- Height matches the left extreme (energy conserved in the ideal case)

```
   o
    \
     \
     (@)   <- bob, far right
```

In a perfect pendulum this is the end of the forward pass. The loop
setting `ping-pong` now replays [[z=3|frame 3]], [[z=2|frame 2]],
[[z=1|frame 1]], and back to [[z=0|frame 0]].

@plane z=5 label="Notes: timing and damping"
# Animation notes

Not a drawn frame. This plane documents how to play the stack.

- **Order:** play z=0 through z=4, then reverse to z=0 for one full period.
- **Easing:** do not space frames evenly in time. The bob is slow at the
  extremes ([[z=0|frame 0]], [[z=4|frame 4]]) and fast at the bottom
  ([[z=2|frame 2]]), so ease-in-out on the angle reads as natural.
- **Damping:** for a decaying swing, shrink the x offsets a few percent
  each period so the extremes creep inward toward the pivot.
- **Frame rate:** at 12 fps a single pass takes under half a second; hold
  one extra tick on each turning point to sell the pause.

Jump back to the start at [[z=0|frame 0]].

title: Coastal Sunset - Adjustment Layer Stack
src:
---
3md: 1.0
axis: layer
title: Coastal Sunset - Adjustment Layer Stack
photo: DSC_4471.RAW
base: ProPhoto RGB, 16-bit
editor: Leif Albrecht
---
A non-destructive edit of a single coastal sunset frame. Each plane below is one
adjustment layer in the stack, ordered from the raw capture at the bottom
(z=0) up to the final output sharpening at the top (z=5). Higher z sits closer
to the viewer and is composited last. Toggle any plane to see its contribution
in isolation; nothing here bakes into the pixels until export.

@plane z=0 label="Base capture" x=0 y=0
# Base Capture (RAW)

The untouched starting point. Everything above this plane is non-destructive.

- **File:** `DSC_4471.RAW`, 6048 x 4024, 14-bit sensor data
- **Exposure as shot:** 1/200 s, f/8, ISO 100
- **White balance:** as-shot, 5200 K / +3 tint
- **Histogram:** highlights clipped in the sun disc, shadows lifted off the floor

The sky is roughly two stops brighter than the foreground rocks, so the
foreground reads almost as a silhouette. We fix the dynamic range in
[[z=1|the exposure layer]] and recover the warmth in [[z=2|color grading]].

> Keep this layer locked. If a later edit looks wrong, hide everything above
> and compare against this plane to find where the stack drifted.

@plane z=1 label="Exposure + tone" x=0 y=0
# Exposure and Tone Curve

Blend mode: **Normal**, opacity 100%. This is the workhorse layer.

| Control          | Value   | Why                                  |
|------------------|---------|--------------------------------------|
| Exposure         | +0.35   | Lift overall midtones                |
| Highlights       | -62     | Recover detail in the cloud edges    |
| Shadows          | +48     | Open the foreground rocks            |
| Whites           | -15     | Pull the sun disc back from clipping |
| Blacks           | -8      | Restore contrast lost from lifting   |

The tone curve adds a gentle S: a control point at (64, 56) to deepen the
low midtones and one at (192, 205) to brighten the upper midtones. Net result
is a recovered foreground that no longer reads as a silhouette.

Masked with a linear gradient (top dark, bottom clear) so the highlight pull
only touches the sky. The foreground inherits the shadow lift but not the
highlight crush. Color work continues in [[z=2|the grade]].

@plane z=2 label="Color grade" x=0 y=0
# Color Grade

Blend mode: **Color**, opacity 85%. Affects hue and saturation only, leaving
the luminance from [[z=1|the exposure layer]] intact.

Split-toning the sunset:

- **Shadows:** hue 215 (cool blue), saturation 18 - the rocks and wet sand
- **Midtones:** hue 28 (amber), saturation 12 - the haze band over the water
- **Highlights:** hue 42 (gold), saturation 25 - the sun and its reflection

HSL targeted tweaks:

1. Orange luminance +10 so the sky glow separates from the clouds
2. Blue saturation -20 to stop the upper sky going electric
3. Aqua hue shifted +8 toward teal for the shallow water

The warm/cool contrast does most of the emotional lifting here. Local dodging
that depends on these colors happens in [[z=3|the local adjustments]].

@plane z=3 label="Local adjustments" x=120 y=-40
# Local Adjustments (Dodge and Burn)

Blend mode: **Soft Light**, opacity 70%. A 50% gray layer painted with low-flow
white (dodge) and black (burn). The x/y offset marks the centroid of the edits,
which cluster around the sun and the foreground tide pool.

- **Dodge:** the sun's reflection trail on the water, two passes at 8% flow
- **Dodge:** the rim light on the largest foreground rock
- **Burn:** the top corners of the frame to keep the eye centered
- **Burn:** a distracting bright pebble at lower right, painted to match

A radial mask centered on the sun (feather 600 px) adds a subtle +0.2 stop
glow, sold as atmosphere rather than a vignette. Compare with the global
exposure in [[z=1|tone]] to confirm the local moves do not double up.

@plane z=4 label="Cleanup + heal" x=-200 y=80
# Cleanup and Healing

Blend mode: **Normal**, opacity 100%. Spot-level retouching on its own layer
so it can be audited or removed wholesale.

- Removed 3 sensor dust spots in the upper sky (visible only at f/8)
- Healed a stray footprint in the wet sand, lower left (the x/y offset points here)
- Cloned out a distant boat on the horizon that pulled focus
- Patched a lens flare ghost just left of the sun

Each fix samples from clean neighboring texture, and the healing layer is
clipped so it never reaches past the frame edges. Nothing structural changes;
this plane only subtracts distractions before the final pass in
[[z=5|output sharpening]].

@plane z=5 label="Output sharpen + grain" x=0 y=0
# Output Sharpening and Grain

The top of the stack, applied last so it reads on the final composite of every
layer below.

- **Capture sharpening:** already applied at import (radius 0.8, amount 40)
- **Output sharpening:** Unsharp Mask, radius 1.2, amount 80, threshold 3,
  tuned for a 24-inch print at 300 ppi
- **Grain:** monochromatic, size 25, roughness 50, amount 12 - hides any
  banding in the sky gradient from [[z=2|the grade]]
- **Edge mask:** sharpening restricted to detected edges so the smooth sky
  stays clean

Export targets: 16-bit TIFF for print, plus a 2048 px sRGB JPEG for web. To
revisit the look from scratch, hide this plane and the four above
[[z=0|the base capture]], then re-enable them one at a time.

title: Driftwood - Inventory Intelligence for Independent Cafes
src:
---
3md: 1.0
axis: slide
title: Driftwood - Inventory Intelligence for Independent Cafes
company: Driftwood
stage: Seed
ask: $1.5M
deck-version: v7
presenter: Mara Okonkwo, CEO
---
Driftwood seed round, slide deck. Jump to the [[z=5|ask]] if you are short on time.

@plane z=0 label="Title" x=0 y=0
# Driftwood

### Inventory intelligence for independent cafes
