Stop guessing what to order. Driftwood reads your point-of-sale feed, predicts demand by the cup, and places supplier orders before you run dry.

Mara Okonkwo, CEO and co-founder
Seed round, raising $1.5M

San Francisco, CA - driftwood.coffee

@plane z=1 label="Problem" x=1 y=0
# The 4am order is a coin flip

A typical independent cafe runs on the owner's memory. At close, the owner eyeballs the shelves and texts the roaster and the dairy supplier with a rough guess.

- Over-order and milk spoils, oat cartons expire, beans go stale. The average shop we surveyed throws out 11% of perishable spend every month.
- Under-order and the espresso machine sits idle by 2pm on a sunny Saturday. A single "sorry, we are out of oat milk" costs roughly $90 in walked customers.
- The owner spends 5 to 7 hours a week on ordering and supplier back-and-forth instead of running the floor.

There are 38,000 independent cafes in the US. None of the tools built for restaurant chains fit a 2-person shop. See how we fix this on [[z=2|the solution slide]].

@plane z=2 label="Solution" x=2 y=0
# Order the right amount, automatically

Driftwood connects to the POS in one click (Square, Toast, Clover) and learns each shop's rhythm.

1. **Predict** - We forecast demand per ingredient using the shop's own sales history, weather, local events, and day-of-week patterns.
2. **Suggest** - Every evening the owner gets one text: "Order 12 gallons whole milk, 4 cases oat, 8 lbs house blend. Tap to confirm."
3. **Place** - One tap sends purchase orders straight to existing suppliers over email or EDI. No new supplier accounts required.

The owner stays in control and approves every order. Driftwood just removes the guesswork. The numbers behind this are on [[z=3|the traction slide]].

@plane z=3 label="Traction" x=3 y=0
# Cafes are paying and staying

We launched the paid product in September 2025.

- **47 paying cafes** across the Bay Area and Portland
- **$9,400 MRR**, growing 22% month over month for the last five months
- **Net revenue retention 118%** - shops add locations and upgrade tiers
- **Logo churn under 2%** monthly

What customers see after 90 days on Driftwood:

| Metric | Before | After |
| --- | --- | --- |
| Perishable waste | 11% of spend | 4% of spend |
| Hours/week on ordering | 6.0 | 1.5 |
| Stockout days/month | 7 | 1 |

"I got my Sunday mornings back." - Dani R., owner, Tidepool Coffee, Oakland

@plane z=4 label="Market and model" x=4 y=0
# A wedge into a $4B back office

**Market**

- 38,000 independent US cafes (SAM) at $1,200/year ACV = roughly $46M.
- Expand to bakeries, juice bars, and ghost kitchens: 210,000 small food businesses (TAM), a $4B+ back-office software opportunity.

**Business model**

- SaaS subscription, $79 to $149/month by volume tier.
- Phase two: take a small rebate on supplier orders routed through Driftwood, aligning our revenue with the spend we already manage.

**Unit economics today**

- CAC $310, blended payback 4.1 months.
- Gross margin 81%. LTV:CAC of 6.2 at current churn.

Where the $1.5M goes is on [[z=5|the ask]].

@plane z=5 label="The ask" x=5 y=0
# Raising $1.5M to reach 500 cafes

We are raising a $1.5M seed to hit $1M ARR and Series A metrics within 18 months.

**Use of funds**

- 50% - Engineering: supplier integrations and a self-serve onboarding flow.
- 30% - Go-to-market: two regional reps and roaster-partner referrals.
- 20% - Operations and runway buffer.

**Milestones this funding buys**

- 500 paying cafes and $1M ARR
- Automated supplier rebate revenue live in 3 metro areas
- Onboarding time cut from 2 days to under 1 hour

**The team**

- Mara Okonkwo, CEO - ran ops for a 9-location cafe group, scaled it from 2 shops.
- Theo Lindqvist, CTO - ex-Square, built the forecasting platform for seller analytics.

Back to [[z=0|the top]]. Let's talk: mara@driftwood.coffee

title: Signal Drift - Season 2 Episode Guide
src:
---
3md: 1.0
axis: time
title: "Signal Drift - Season 2 Episode Guide"
show: Signal Drift
season: "2"
host: Mara Okonkwo
producer: Devin Reyes
network: Tidewater Audio
release_cadence: weekly (Thursdays)
---
**Signal Drift** is a six-part documentary podcast tracing how a single mislabeled
weather buoy off the Oregon coast quietly broke a decade of climate models. Season 2
follows the people who noticed, the institution that ignored them, and the fix that
almost did not happen.

Each plane below is one episode, ordered along the **time** axis by release week.
Jump straight to the finale at [[z=5|Episode 6]], or start at the top.

@plane z=0 label="Ep 1 - The Buoy That Lied"
# Episode 1 - The Buoy That Lied

**Released:** September 5, 2024 | **Runtime:** 38 min

A retired oceanographer in Newport notices that Buoy 46050 has been reporting sea
surface temperatures a steady 1.4 degrees too cold since 2013. Nobody downstream
ever checked. We open on the morning she pulls the raw logs.

## Guests
- **Dr. Helen Vasquez**, retired oceanographer, NOAA (1988-2019)
- **Toby Lin**, buoy maintenance technician, Pacific Marine

## Key moments
- (04:12) Helen describes the "too clean" data that first bothered her
- (19:30) The 1.4 degree offset, and why a cold bias hides in plain sight
- (33:05) A voicemail to the regional office that is never returned

> "A sensor does not lie on purpose. It just keeps saying the wrong thing politely,
> forever." - Dr. Helen Vasquez

Continue to [[z=1|Episode 2]] to follow the data trail upstream.

@plane z=1 label="Ep 2 - Garbage In"
# Episode 2 - Garbage In

**Released:** September 12, 2024 | **Runtime:** 41 min

Helen's offset was not just wrong, it was load-bearing. We trace how one buoy fed a
regional average, which fed a national reanalysis, which fed three published models.
Producer Devin Reyes maps the pipeline on a whiteboard that grows alarming.

## Guests
- **Dr. Priya Anand**, climate modeler, University of Washington
- **Anonymous**, data engineer, federal reanalysis project (voice altered)

## Key moments
- (07:45) How a single station can skew a 200-mile grid cell
- (22:18) The reanalysis team explains why nobody re-validates old inputs
- (36:50) Priya runs the model both ways, on air, and the coastline shifts

The cold bias quietly cooled a stretch of ocean that was, in reality, warming.
See where this started in [[z=0|Episode 1]], or where it lands in [[z=4|Episode 5]].

@plane z=2 label="Ep 3 - The Paper Trail"
# Episode 3 - The Paper Trail

**Released:** September 19, 2024 | **Runtime:** 44 min

If the data was off for eleven years, somebody should have caught it. This episode is
about the three times somebody did, and what happened to their reports. The answer
involves a reorganization, a departed manager, and a shared drive nobody owned.

## Guests
- **Marcus Bell**, former QA contractor (filed the 2016 ticket)
- **Dr. Helen Vasquez** (returning)

## Key moments
- (06:00) Marcus reads his own 2016 bug report aloud, eight years later
- (24:40) The ticket's status: "Won't Fix - low priority"
- (39:12) Who actually owned data quality? Nobody could name a person.

> "It was not a cover-up. It was worse. It was nobody's job." - Marcus Bell

@plane z=3 label="Ep 4 - On the Record"
# Episode 4 - On the Record

**Released:** September 26, 2024 | **Runtime:** 47 min

Mara sits down with the agency. For the first time, an official responds on tape. It
is tense, careful, and more honest than expected. We also hear from a policy aide who
used the flawed coastal estimates to argue against a fishery protection rule.

## Guests
- **Director Joanne Pratt**, regional data office
- **Eli Sandoval**, former legislative aide

## Key moments
- (11:20) Pratt acknowledges the offset and the missed tickets
- (28:55) Eli on building policy atop a number he assumed was solid
- (43:10) The question Pratt would not answer

This connects the quiet error in [[z=2|Episode 3]] to real consequences. The repair
effort begins in [[z=4|Episode 5]].

@plane z=4 label="Ep 5 - The Fix"
# Episode 5 - The Fix

**Released:** October 3, 2024 | **Runtime:** 42 min

Correcting one buoy is easy. Re-running eleven years of dependent models, re-issuing
corrected datasets, and telling everyone downstream is not. We embed with the small
team that volunteers to do it, on nights and weekends, with no mandate.

## Guests
- **Dr. Priya Anand** (returning)
- **Sam Whitfield**, open-data volunteer, Coastwatch collective

## Key moments
- (09:30) Building a corrected dataset from a borrowed laptop
- (25:15) The first downstream user to actually update their model
- (38:40) What "fixed" really means when the old numbers are already published

The corrected coastline finally matches reality from [[z=1|Episode 2]]. We close the
season in [[z=5|Episode 6]].

@plane z=5 label="Ep 6 - What the Buoy Knew"
# Episode 6 - What the Buoy Knew

**Released:** October 10, 2024 | **Runtime:** 51 min

The finale. Buoy 46050 is recalibrated, the datasets are corrected, and Helen returns
to Newport to watch the new readings come in live. We ask the larger question: how
many other quiet errors are still out there, politely saying the wrong thing?

## Guests
- **Dr. Helen Vasquez** (final interview)
- **Director Joanne Pratt** (follow-up)
- The full team, together for the first time

## Key moments
- (08:00) The recalibration, recorded dockside in real time
- (31:25) Helen reads the first correct temperature in eleven years
- (47:50) The new audit rule named, unofficially, after the buoy

> "It took one retired woman with a spreadsheet. Imagine what we are still
> missing." - Mara Okonkwo, host

## Season wrap
- Full corrected dataset and show notes at the season page
- Where to start over: [[z=0|Episode 1]]
- Season 3 announcement coming in November 2024

title: Helios Sync - Build Timeline
src:
---
3md: 1.0
axis: time
title: Helios Sync - Build Timeline
project: Helios Sync (cross-device note sync)
team: 4 engineers, 1 designer
quarter: Q1 2026
---
A six-month delivery plan for Helios Sync, our offline-first note synchronization
service. Each plane is one calendar month. Read top to bottom for the narrative,
or jump straight to the [[z=5|GA launch]] if you want the finish line first.

@plane z=0 label="January - Foundations" x=0 y=0
# January: Foundations

The month we turn a one-page proposal into a running skeleton.

## Goals
- Stand up the monorepo, CI, and a deployable "hello world" sync server.
- Lock the conflict model: last-writer-wins per field, vector clocks for ordering.
- Hire the second backend engineer (offer out by the 15th).

## Milestones
- **Jan 10** - CI green on an empty service (build, lint, unit harness).
- **Jan 17** - Architecture decision record approved for the CRDT layer.
- **Jan 31** - First end-to-end write replicated between two test clients.

## Risks
- The CRDT choice blocks everything downstream. If the ADR slips, so does
  [[z=1|February]]. We time-box the spike to two weeks.

## Exit criteria
A document can be created on client A and observed on client B within 5 seconds
on a LAN. No auth, no encryption yet - that is fine for this month.

@plane z=1 label="February - Sync Core" x=0 y=1
# February: Sync Core

Foundations exist (see [[z=0|January]]). Now we make sync correct under conflict.

## Goals
- Implement vector-clock merge with deterministic tie-breaking.
- Add a replay log so a client offline for days can catch up cleanly.
- 200 property-based tests covering concurrent edits.

## Milestones
- **Feb 7** - Merge function passes the concurrent-edit fuzz suite (10k cases).
- **Feb 14** - Catch-up replay handles a 30-day offline gap in under 2 seconds.
- **Feb 28** - Soak test: 50 simulated clients editing for 6 hours, zero divergence.

## Dependencies
- Needs the CRDT ADR from January. Locked on Jan 17, so we are clear.

## Notes
We deliberately defer encryption to [[z=2|March]] to keep the merge logic
readable while it is still changing daily.

@plane z=2 label="March - Security and Auth" x=1 y=1
# March: Security and Auth

The sync core is correct. Before anyone outside the team touches it, we make it safe.

## Goals
- End-to-end encryption: per-note keys, wrapped by a device keychain.
- OAuth device flow for sign-in, refresh-token rotation on every use.
- Threat model review with an outside reviewer.

## Milestones
- **Mar 6** - Notes encrypted at rest and in transit; server stores ciphertext only.
- **Mar 13** - Device-flow login working on desktop and mobile reference clients.
- **Mar 27** - External security review complete, findings triaged.

## Risks
- Key rotation interacts with the offline replay from [[z=1|February]]. A client
  that wakes up after a key change must re-derive note keys. We add a migration
  step and test it explicitly.

## Exit criteria
A new device can enroll, decrypt existing notes, and sync, with the server never
seeing plaintext.

@plane z=3 label="April - Closed Beta" x=2 y=1
# April: Closed Beta

First real users. The goal shifts from "is it correct" to "is it usable".

## Goals
- Onboard 50 internal beta users across desktop and mobile.
- Ship a crash and metrics pipeline (opt-in, privacy-reviewed).
- Cut p99 sync latency from 5s to under 1.5s on cellular.

## Milestones
- **Apr 4** - Beta build live behind a feature flag, 10 users.
- **Apr 18** - 50 users, daily-active above 60 percent.
- **Apr 30** - Top 5 reported bugs fixed; latency target met.

## Feedback loop
Every Friday we triage beta reports. Anything touching the merge path goes back
through the [[z=1|February]] property suite before it ships.

## Exit criteria
A non-engineer can install, sign in, and sync three devices without help.

@plane z=4 label="May - Scale and Hardening" x=2 y=0
# May: Scale and Hardening

Beta proved it works for 50. May proves it works for 50,000.

## Goals
- Load test to 50k concurrent clients; shard the replay log by account.
- Add rate limiting and abuse protection on the sync endpoint.
- Write the runbook: on-call rotation, alerts, rollback procedure.

## Milestones
- **May 9** - Sustained 50k concurrent clients in staging, p99 under 1.5s.
- **May 16** - Automated rollback verified (deploy a bad build, recover in <5 min).
- **May 30** - Runbook reviewed, on-call schedule published.

## Risks
- Sharding the replay log can reorder catch-up under failover. We re-run the
  March migration tests against the sharded layout to be sure.

## Gate to launch
Everything here must be green before [[z=5|June]] opens the gates to the public.

@plane z=5 label="June - General Availability" x=3 y=0
# June: General Availability

The finish line. Helios Sync ships to everyone.

## Goals
- Public launch with a staged rollout: 1 percent, then 10, then 100.
- Publish docs, pricing, and the data-export tool (no lock-in).
- Hand off day-2 ownership to the standing on-call rotation.

## Milestones
- **Jun 6** - 1 percent rollout, dashboards watched live for 48 hours.
- **Jun 13** - 10 percent, support queue staffed.
- **Jun 20** - 100 percent. Launch announcement goes out.
- **Jun 27** - Retrospective; backlog groomed for Q3.

## Definition of done
- Five months of work, from the [[z=0|January]] skeleton to GA, with no open
  P0 or P1 issues.
- p99 sync latency under 1.5s at full production load.
- A user can export every note as plaintext and Markdown in one click.

## After GA
Q3 planning starts the following week. Candidate themes: shared notebooks,
selective sync, and a web client.

title: Q2 2026 Quarterly Business Review
src:
---
3md: 1.0
axis: slide
title: Q2 2026 Quarterly Business Review
company: Northwind Analytics
presenter: Dana Okafor, VP Operations
period: Apr 1 - Jun 30, 2026
deck-version: final
---
Northwind Analytics quarterly business review for Q2 2026. Each plane is one slide; advance along the slide axis from the title through results, finances, risks, and the Q3 plan.

@plane z=0 label="Title"
# Q2 2026 Business Review

**Northwind Analytics**

Prepared by Dana Okafor, VP Operations
Reviewed with the executive team on July 9, 2026

Agenda for today:

1. [[z=1|Where we landed against plan]]
2. [[z=2|The financial picture]]
3. [[z=3|Risks we are watching]]
4. [[z=4|What we are committing to in Q3]]

> One-line summary: we beat the revenue target by 6 percent, missed on net new logos, and enter Q3 with a tighter pipeline and one staffing gap to close.

@plane z=1 x=0 y=0 label="Results vs Plan"
# Results vs Plan

| Metric | Q2 Target | Q2 Actual | Delta |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| Net new ARR | $1.80M | $1.91M | +6.1% |
| Net new logos | 42 | 35 | -16.7% |
| Gross retention | 90% | 92.4% | +2.4 pts |
| Net revenue retention | 108% | 114% | +6 pts |

What went well:

- **Expansion carried the quarter.** Existing accounts upgraded faster than modeled, pushing net revenue retention to 114 percent.
- The mid-market segment renewed at 96 percent, our best result in eight quarters.

What missed:

- New logo acquisition came in at 35 against a target of 42. Two enterprise deals slipped to July rather than being lost.
- See the pipeline detail under [[z=3|Risks]] for the slipped-deal exposure.

@plane z=1.5 x=2 y=0 label="Segment Detail"
# Segment Detail

A breakout of the same quarter by customer segment, sitting beside the headline results.

| Segment | ARR added | Logos | Avg deal size |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| Enterprise | $0.94M | 6 | $156K |
| Mid-market | $0.71M | 14 | $51K |
| SMB | $0.26M | 15 | $17K |

Reading the table:

- Enterprise drove half of new ARR from a quarter of the deal count, confirming the move upmarket is working.
- SMB volume held, but average deal size keeps drifting down. We should revisit packaging before Q4.

Back to the [[z=1|headline numbers]].

@plane z=2 x=0 y=0 label="Financials"
# Financials

| Line item | Q1 2026 | Q2 2026 | QoQ |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| Revenue | $7.4M | $7.9M | +6.8% |
| Gross margin | 71% | 73% | +2 pts |
| Operating expense | $5.1M | $5.0M | -2.0% |
| Cash runway | 19 mo | 22 mo | +3 mo |

Highlights:

- Gross margin improved two points as we retired the legacy hosting contract in May.
- Runway extended to 22 months on stronger collections and the expense discipline noted last quarter.
- Burn multiple fell to 0.8, down from 1.1 in Q1.

The runway gain assumes we hold hiring to the plan described in [[z=4|the Q3 plan]].

@plane z=3 x=0 y=0 label="Risks"
# Risks We Are Watching

1. **Slipped enterprise deals.** Two deals worth a combined $310K in ARR moved to July. Both have verbal commitment; legal review is the gating step. Owner: Sales.
2. **Support staffing gap.** We are one senior engineer short on the support rotation, pushing median first response from 4 to 7 hours. Owner: Support, with a backfill req already open.
3. **Concentration.** Our top five accounts now represent 28 percent of ARR. No single renewal is at risk this quarter, but we are tracking it.

Mitigation status:

- Deal slippage: high confidence both close in Q3, reflected in [[z=4|the Q3 commitments]].
- Staffing: offer out to a candidate, expected start mid-August.

@plane z=4 x=0 y=0 label="Q3 Plan"
# Q3 2026 Plan

Three commitments, each with an owner and a number.

| Commitment | Owner | Target |
| --- | --- | --- |
| Close the two slipped deals | Sales | $310K ARR by Aug 15 |
| Fill the support engineer role | People Ops | Start by Aug 18 |
| Launch usage-based SMB tier | Product | GA by Sep 30 |

Quarter goals:

- Net new ARR of $2.05M, weighted toward the recovered enterprise pipeline.
- Hold gross retention at or above 92 percent.
- Bring support median first response back under 5 hours once the role is filled.

Closing note: the fundamentals (retention, margin, runway) are strong. Q3 is about converting [[z=3|the deals we already know about]] and closing [[z=3|the staffing gap]] so delivery quality does not slip.

Return to the [[z=0|agenda]].

title: Falling Rain
src:
---
3md: 1.0
axis: frame
title: Falling Rain
fps: 8
loop: true
charset: ascii
description: A five-frame ASCII rain loop. Drops fall, land, and splash.
---
A short looping animation of rain falling on flat ground. Each plane is one
frame; play them in `z` order at the rate given by `fps` (8 frames per second),
then jump from the last frame back to [[z=0|the first]] to loop.

The drop glyphs are `'` (high, just spawned), `.` (mid-air), and `:` (low, about
to land). The ground line is a row of `_`. A landing is drawn as `v`, and the
splash that follows is a spray of `*` and `^`. Read the frames in order to see a
single column of rain complete one fall-and-splash cycle.

@plane z=0 label="Spawn" x=0 y=0
# Frame 1 - drops spawn at the top

New drops appear near the cloud line. Nothing has fallen far yet, so the lower
rows are still clear.

```
   '     '      '
      '       '
   .      '
        .
                 .
.

________________________
```

Next: the drops begin to [[z=1|fall]].

@plane z=1 label="Falling" x=0 y=1
# Frame 2 - the column descends

Every drop has moved down one row. The high `'` glyphs have become mid-air `.`
glyphs, and a fresh row of `'` spawns up top to keep the loop seamless.

```
   '     '      '
      .       .
   .      .
        :
                 :
     .
________________________
```

The leading drop is now a low `:`, one row above the ground. Watch it
[[z=2|land]] next.

@plane z=2 label="Impact" x=0 y=2
# Frame 3 - first contact

The leading drop reaches the ground and flattens into a `v`. The rest of the
column keeps descending behind it.

```
   .     '      .
      :       :
   :      :
        v
                 .
     :
_______v________________
```

A `v` marks the exact impact point. The energy of that hit becomes a
[[z=3|splash]].

@plane z=3 label="Splash" x=0 y=3
# Frame 4 - the splash blooms

The landing throws droplets outward and upward. `^` is the rising spray, `*` is
the scatter at ground level.

```
   :     .      :
      .       .
   .      .
       ^^^
                 v
     .
______*^^^*_____________
```

The splash is at its widest here. In the final frame it [[z=4|settles]].

@plane z=4 label="Settle" x=0 y=4
# Frame 5 - the splash settles, the loop resets

The spray falls back, leaving only faint `*` marks that will fade. The column
above has marched down one more row, ready to feed the next cycle.

```
   .     :      .
      .       :
   :      :
        :
                 ^
     :
_______*________________
```

That is one full cycle. Loop back to [[z=0|Frame 1]] and the rain keeps
falling. At 8 fps the whole sequence runs in roughly 0.6 seconds per drop.

title: One-Pan Lemon Garlic Chicken
src:
---
3md: 1.0
axis: depth
title: One-Pan Lemon Garlic Chicken
serves: 4
total_time: 35 minutes
diet: gluten-free
---
The same recipe at four depths. Start shallow when you are hungry and in a
hurry, then go deeper when you want to understand why each step works.

@plane z=0 label="At a Glance"
# One-Pan Lemon Garlic Chicken

Sear 4 chicken thighs skin-side down, add garlic and lemon, finish in the oven.
Done in about 35 minutes, one pan, four servings.

- Chicken thighs, garlic, lemon, butter, thyme.
- Sear, deglaze, roast.
- Spoon the pan sauce over everything.

Hungry now? Jump to [[z=1|the quick steps]]. Want the full method and the
reasoning, see [[z=3|the deep dive]].

@plane z=1 label="Quick Steps" x=2
# Quick Steps (about 35 min)

**Ingredients**

- 4 bone-in, skin-on chicken thighs
- 6 garlic cloves, smashed
- 1 lemon (half juiced, half sliced)
- 2 tbsp butter
- 4 sprigs thyme
- Salt, pepper, olive oil

**Method**

1. Heat oven to 220 C (425 F).
2. Pat thighs dry, salt well. Sear skin-side down in oil, 7 min, until golden.
3. Flip, add garlic, lemon slices, thyme, and butter.
4. Roast 15 min until the thickest thigh reads 74 C (165 F).
5. Squeeze the juiced lemon half over the top, rest 5 min, serve.

Need exact gear and prep counts first? See [[z=2|the full ingredient and tool list]].
For the why behind drying and searing, see [[z=3|the deep dive]].

@plane z=2 label="Full Mise en Place" x=4
# Full Ingredient and Tool List

Everything measured, prepped, and staged before the pan gets hot. This is the
restaurant habit that makes the cook feel calm.

## Ingredients (weights for accuracy)

| Item | Amount | Notes |
| --- | --- | --- |
| Bone-in skin-on chicken thighs | 4 (about 700 g) | Room temperature, 20 min out |
| Garlic | 6 cloves (30 g) | Smashed, skins removed |
| Lemon | 1 (about 100 g) | Half sliced thin, half for juice |
| Unsalted butter | 2 tbsp (28 g) | Cold, cubed |
| Fresh thyme | 4 sprigs | Or 1/2 tsp dried |
| Kosher salt | 1.5 tsp total | Half on each side |
| Black pepper | To taste | Coarse ground |
| Olive oil | 1 tbsp | For the sear |

## Tools

- 10 to 12 inch oven-safe skillet (cast iron is ideal)
- Instant-read thermometer
- Tongs and a spoon for basting
- Plate for resting

Steps assume this is staged. Follow them at [[z=1|the quick steps]]. The
technique notes for each item live in [[z=3|the deep dive]].

@plane z=3 label="Deep Dive" x=6 y=2
# Deep Dive: Why Each Step Works

## Dry skin is crisp skin

Water is the enemy of browning. Patting the thighs bone-dry and salting them
early pulls surface moisture out so the skin can hit the Maillard reaction
instead of steaming. If you have time, salt them uncovered in the fridge for an
hour (or overnight) for even crisper results.

## Start in a cold-ish pan, do not move them

Lay the thighs in oil over medium-high and leave them alone. The skin releases
itself from the pan only once it is properly seared, so resist flipping early.
Rendered fat from the skin becomes the cooking medium and the base of the sauce.

## Build the sauce on the fond

Those browned bits stuck to the pan (the fond) are concentrated flavor. Adding
garlic, lemon, butter, and thyme after the flip lets them bloom in the rendered
fat and lift the fond as the butter melts. Lemon slices caramelize slightly and
mellow; the reserved juice goes in at the end so its brightness survives.

## Temperature, not time, is the truth

Ovens lie and thighs vary. Pull the pan when the thickest part reads 74 C
(165 F) at the bone. Carryover heat climbs a few degrees during the rest, which
also lets juices redistribute so the first cut does not bleed onto the plate.

## Variations

- **Crispier finish:** broil the last 2 minutes, watching closely.
- **More sauce:** add 1/4 cup stock with the butter and reduce.
- **Herb swap:** rosemary or oregano for thyme, both hold up to the oven.

Ready to cook from memory? Collapse back to [[z=0|the glance]] or run
[[z=1|the quick steps]] with all of this in mind.

title: Weeknight Lemon Garlic Chicken Thighs
src:
---
3md: 1.0
axis: step
title: Weeknight Lemon Garlic Chicken Thighs
servings: 4
total_time: 45 minutes
hands_on: 25 minutes
---
A one-pan dinner built around crisp-skinned chicken thighs and a bright pan
sauce. Read top to bottom, or step through one plane at a time. Each step lists
only what you touch right then, so nothing piles up.

Walk the steps in order: start at [[z=0|the prep]] and finish at [[z=4|the rest and plate]].

@plane z=0 label="Prep and season"
# Step 1 - Prep and season

Pull **6 bone-in, skin-on chicken thighs** out of the fridge 20 minutes early so
they cook evenly. Pat every piece bone-dry with paper towels; dry skin is the
whole secret to crisp skin.

- Season both sides with **1.5 tsp kosher salt** and **0.5 tsp black pepper**.
- Mince **4 cloves garlic** and set aside (you use half now, half later).
- Zest **1 lemon**, then halve it for juicing in [[z=3|the sauce]].

While the thighs sit, set a heavy oven-safe skillet (cast iron is ideal) over
medium-high and heat your oven to **425F**.

> Tip: salt early if you can. Even 20 minutes of dry brining firms the skin.

@plane z=1 x=0 y=0 label="Sear skin-side down"
# Step 2 - Sear, skin-side down

Add **1 tbsp olive oil** to the hot pan. Lay the thighs in **skin-side down** and
then do not touch them. Crowding or fidgeting steams the skin instead of browning
it.

- Sear **8 to 10 minutes** until the skin is deep golden and releases on its own.
- The fat will render and pool; that rendered fat is flavor for [[z=3|the sauce]].
- Flip once, cook **2 minutes** on the flesh side, then transfer the pan to the
  oven.

Roast **12 to 15 minutes**, until an instant-read thermometer reads **175F** at
the thickest part near the bone.

@plane z=2 x=2 y=0 label="Build aromatics (parallel)"
# Step 3 - Aromatics, while the oven works

This happens during the 12 to 15 minute roast from [[z=1|the sear step]], so you
lose no time. Work on a second small pan or a cutting board.

- Thinly slice **1 shallot**.
- Reserve the **other half of the minced garlic** from [[z=0|prep]].
- Pull leaves from **4 sprigs fresh thyme**.
- Measure **0.5 cup chicken stock** and keep the lemon halves within reach.

Keep these grouped by the stove. The window between pulling the pan and pouring
the sauce is short, so having everything ready matters.

@plane z=3 x=2 y=2 label="Deglaze and reduce"
# Step 4 - Pan sauce

Move the cooked thighs to a plate. The skillet now holds rendered fat and browned
bits (the fond), which is where the sauce lives. Pour off all but about **1 tbsp**
of fat.

1. Set the pan over medium. Add the shallot and reserved garlic from
   [[z=2|the aromatics step]]; cook **1 minute** until fragrant.
2. Pour in the **0.5 cup stock** and scrape up every browned bit with a wooden
   spoon.
3. Squeeze in the juice of the **lemon halves** from [[z=0|prep]] and add the
   thyme leaves.
4. Simmer **3 to 4 minutes** until slightly syrupy, then swirl in **1 tbsp cold
   butter** off the heat for shine.

Taste and adjust with a pinch of salt.

@plane z=4 x=4 y=2 label="Rest and plate"
# Step 5 - Rest and plate

Let the thighs rest **5 minutes** so the juices settle; plating straight from the
oven leaves them dry. Resting is not optional.

- Spoon the warm pan sauce from [[z=3|the deglaze step]] over each thigh.
- Finish with the **reserved lemon zest** from [[z=0|prep]] and a little flaky
  salt.
- Serve over rice, mashed potatoes, or crusty bread to catch the sauce.

**Leftovers:** refrigerate up to 3 days. Reheat skin-side up in a 375F oven for
10 minutes to re-crisp; the microwave will soften the skin.

title: Weeknight Shakshuka
src:
---
3md: 1.0
axis: step
title: Weeknight Shakshuka
servings: "4"
total_time: "35 min"
---
Each plane is one step of the recipe. Scrub the Z axis to walk forward and
backward through cooking. Step z=0 is mise en place; the last plane plates up.

@plane z=0 label="prep"
# Mise en place
Gather and prep before the heat goes on.

- 1 large onion, diced
- 1 red bell pepper, sliced thin
- 3 cloves garlic, minced
- 1 tsp cumin, 1 tsp smoked paprika, pinch chili flakes
- 1 (28 oz) can crushed tomatoes
- 4–6 eggs
- Feta, parsley, crusty bread to serve

@plane z=1 label="aromatics"
# Soften the aromatics
Heat 2 tbsp olive oil in a wide skillet over medium.

Add the onion and pepper. Cook 8–10 min until soft and just starting to color.
Stir in the garlic and spices; cook 1 min until fragrant.

@plane z=2 label="sauce"
# Build the sauce
Pour in the crushed tomatoes. Season with salt and a pinch of sugar.

Simmer 10 min, stirring now and then, until thick enough to hold a spoon-track.

@plane z=3 label="eggs"
# Poach the eggs
Make wells in the sauce with the back of a spoon. Crack one egg into each well.

Cover and cook 6–8 min, until the whites are set but the yolks still wobble.

@plane z=4 label="serve"
# Serve
Off the heat, scatter feta and parsley over the top.

Bring the skillet to the table with bread for dunking. Eat immediately.

title: Operation Northwind - Progressive Redaction
src:
---
3md: 1.0
axis: layer
title: Operation Northwind - Progressive Redaction
classification: declassified-with-redactions
case_number: NW-1998-0447
review_authority: Office of Historical Records
---
This memorandum is published in four review layers. Each plane is the same
document at a different declassification stage, from the fully sealed copy that
left the archive to the unredacted text approved for public release in 2026.

Start at the most restricted copy in [[z=0|the sealed layer]] and peel back one
layer at a time to watch the black bars disappear.

@plane z=0 label="Sealed (FOIA denied)"
# MEMORANDUM - SEALED COPY

**To:** [REDACTED]
**From:** [REDACTED]
**Date:** [REDACTED]
**Re:** [REDACTED]

1. On [REDACTED] this office authorized [REDACTED] to proceed with
   [REDACTED] under the budget line described in [REDACTED].

2. The estimated cost was [REDACTED] and the responsible vendor was
   [REDACTED], operating out of [REDACTED].

3. [REDACTED]

> Release status: DENIED IN FULL. Exemptions (b)(1), (b)(3), (b)(6).

Peel forward to [[z=1|the partial release]] to see what the first review let through.

@plane z=1 label="Partial release (2009)" x=2
# MEMORANDUM - PARTIAL RELEASE

**To:** Director, [REDACTED]
**From:** [REDACTED], Program Office
**Date:** March [REDACTED], 1998
**Re:** Renewal of the [REDACTED] maintenance contract

1. On March 4, 1998 this office authorized the Program Office to proceed with
   renewal of the [REDACTED] under the budget line described in Appendix C.

2. The estimated cost was [REDACTED] and the responsible vendor was
   [REDACTED], operating out of Reston, Virginia.

3. The renewal carries a one year option that may be exercised by [REDACTED].

> Release status: GRANTED IN PART. Names and dollar figures withheld under
> (b)(6) personal privacy and (b)(4) trade secrets.

Names and amounts return in [[z=2|the appeal release]].

@plane z=2 label="Appeal release (2017)" x=4
# MEMORANDUM - APPEAL RELEASE

**To:** Director, Office of Facilities Management
**From:** Karen Albright, Program Office
**Date:** March 4, 1998
**Re:** Renewal of the Building 7 HVAC maintenance contract

1. On March 4, 1998 this office authorized the Program Office to proceed with
   renewal of the Building 7 HVAC maintenance contract under the budget line
   described in Appendix C.

2. The estimated cost was $214,500 and the responsible vendor was
   Tidewater Mechanical Services, operating out of Reston, Virginia.

3. The renewal carries a one year option that may be exercised by [REDACTED].

> Release status: GRANTED IN PART after appeal. One sentence remains withheld
> under (b)(5) deliberative process.

The last black bar comes off in [[z=3|the full release]].

@plane z=3 label="Full release (2026)" x=6
# MEMORANDUM - FULL RELEASE

**To:** Director, Office of Facilities Management
**From:** Karen Albright, Program Office
**Date:** March 4, 1998
**Re:** Renewal of the Building 7 HVAC maintenance contract

1. On March 4, 1998 this office authorized the Program Office to proceed with
   renewal of the Building 7 HVAC maintenance contract under the budget line
   described in Appendix C.

2. The estimated cost was $214,500 and the responsible vendor was
   Tidewater Mechanical Services, operating out of Reston, Virginia.

3. The renewal carries a one year option that may be exercised by the Deputy
   Director without further board review, a delegation we recommend revisiting
   given the cost growth on the prior contract.

> Release status: GRANTED IN FULL. All exemptions lifted on the 25 year
> automatic declassification review.

Compare against [[z=0|the sealed copy]] to see how much of an ordinary
facilities memo stayed hidden for nearly three decades.

title: Ada Byron - Resume
src:
---
3md: 1.0
axis: depth
title: Ada Byron - Resume
subject: "Senior Backend Engineer"
---
A layered resume. z=0 is the at-a-glance summary recruiters read first; deeper
planes drill into experience, projects, and the full detail a hiring manager
wants. Scrub the Z axis to go from skim to deep-dive.

@plane z=0 label="summary"
# Ada Byron
Senior Backend Engineer · Edinburgh, UK · ada@example.com

Distributed-systems engineer with 9 years building payment and data
infrastructure. I like correctness proofs, boring deploys, and small APIs.

**Core:** Rust · Go · PostgreSQL · Kafka · Kubernetes

@plane z=1 label="experience"
## Experience

### Staff Engineer - Difference Engine Ltd (2021–present)
- Led the rewrite of the ledger service; cut p99 settlement latency 240ms → 31ms.
- Owned the move from a monolith to 6 services without a single customer-visible outage.

### Senior Engineer - Analytical Co (2017–2021)
- Built the streaming aggregation pipeline processing 4B events/day.
- Mentored 5 engineers; two are now tech leads.

@plane z=2 label="projects"
## Selected Projects

- **looms** - open-source Raft implementation in Rust, 2.1k stars.
- **note-engine** - append-only event store with point-in-time recovery.
- **3md tooling** - contributor to the layered-document format and CLI.

@plane z=3 label="detail"
## Full Detail

### Education
- B.Sc. Mathematics, University of London, 1st class.

### Speaking
- "Proving Your Ledger Balances" - RustConf 2023.
- "Boring Deploys on Purpose" - SREcon EMEA 2022.

### References
Available on request.

title: Anatomy of a Three-Stage Launch Vehicle
src:
---
3md: 1.0
axis: floor
title: Anatomy of a Three-Stage Launch Vehicle
vehicle: Generic LEO heavy lifter
height: 70 m
liftoff_mass: 549 t
flight_profile: ascending stage separation
---
Read this stack from the bottom up, the way the rocket actually flies. Each
plane is one physical section of the vehicle, ordered along the `floor` axis
from the pad (z=0) to the spacecraft riding on top (z=4). The numbers are
representative of a modern kerosene-and-oxygen first stage with a cryogenic
upper stage, not any one real vehicle.

Start at the engines on [[z=0|the launch table]] and climb to the
[[z=4|payload]] at the nose.

@plane z=0 label="Stage 1 - Engine Section" y=0
# Stage 1: Engine Section (the base)

This is what sits on the launch table and does the heavy work of getting off
the ground. Nine sea-level engines are clustered in an octaweb: eight in a
ring, one in the center.

| Property | Value |
| --- | --- |
| Engines | 9 (gas-generator, kerosene/LOX) |
| Sea-level thrust | 7,600 kN total |
| Burn time | ~162 s |
| Gimbal range | +/- 5 deg (outer engines) |

**What happens here, in order:**

1. T-3 s: ignition sequence start, engines spin up to ~60% thrust.
2. T-0: hold-down clamps release once all nine confirm nominal.
3. Thrust vectoring steers the whole stack by tilting the outer engines.
4. After the boostback and entry burns, three center engines relight for
   landing.

The thrust structure transfers all of that force into the tank wall directly
[[z=1|above this section]]. If the load path here fails, nothing else matters.

> Design note: the center engine carries the landing burn alone, so it is rated
> for more relights than its eight siblings.

@plane z=1 label="Stage 1 - Propellant Tanks" y=14
# Stage 1: Propellant Tanks

The tall silver body of the booster is almost entirely propellant. Two tanks
share a common bulkhead to save mass and length.

- **Lower tank: kerosene (RP-1)**, roughly 123 t, kept at ambient temperature.
- **Upper tank: liquid oxygen (LOX)**, roughly 287 t, chilled to -207 C
  (sub-cooled to pack more mass into the same volume).

Pressurization keeps the thin walls from buckling under their own weight before
flight. The tanks are part of the structure, not just containers: this is a
**balloon-style stage-frame** where internal pressure provides stiffness.

```
[ LOX  ]  <- denser, sits on top, feeds the turbopumps
[=======]  common bulkhead
[ RP-1 ]  <- fuel, drained slightly slower
```

Fuel and oxidizer fall to the [[z=0|engine section]] below; the empty mass that
remains is what makes the booster light enough to fly back and land.

@plane z=2 label="Stage Separation - Interstage" y=42
# Interstage and Stage Separation

The interstage is a hollow composite cylinder that connects the booster to the
upper stage and houses the second-stage engine bell until it is needed.

**Separation sequence (MECO + a few seconds):**

1. **MECO**: main engine cutoff on [[z=0|stage one]].
2. Pneumatic pushers release the clamp band joining the two stages.
3. Cold-gas nitrogen thrusters shove the booster backward and clear.
4. The upper stage ignites once it is a safe distance away.

This is the single most dramatic moment of the flight: the rocket splits in
two. Everything below this plane comes home. Everything [[z=3|above it]]
continues to orbit.

| Component | Role |
| --- | --- |
| Clamp band | Holds the stages together under thrust |
| Pusher rods | Provide clean separation impulse |
| Grid fins | Stow here; deploy for booster reentry steering |

@plane z=3 label="Stage 2 - Upper Stage" y=50
# Stage 2: Upper Stage

A single vacuum-optimized engine with a large expansion nozzle finishes the job
of reaching orbital velocity. In near-vacuum it can run a much wider bell than
anything on [[z=0|the first stage]].

| Property | Value |
| --- | --- |
| Engine | 1 (vacuum-optimized, kerosene/LOX) |
| Vacuum thrust | 980 kN |
| Burn time | ~397 s (can relight) |
| Job | Circularize and place the payload precisely |

The upper stage is where the mission is actually won or lost. It can:

- **Relight** to raise, lower, or circularize the orbit.
- Perform a **coast phase** between burns for a target injection point.
- Deorbit itself afterward so it does not become debris.

Its tanks are smaller cryogenic versions of the ones [[z=1|in the booster]],
sized for the much lower mass it has to push once the heavy first stage is gone.

@plane z=4 label="Payload - Fairing and Spacecraft" y=62
# Payload: Fairing and Spacecraft (the top)

At the very top sits the reason the whole stack exists. The payload fairing is a
two-piece aerodynamic shell that protects the spacecraft through the dense lower
atmosphere.

**Fairing jettison** happens early in the [[z=3|second-stage burn]], once the
air is thin enough that the shell is just dead weight. The two halves split
along a vertical seam and fall away.

Inside, the payload can be:

- A communications or imaging **satellite** on a payload adapter.
- A **crew or cargo capsule** (in which case there is no fairing, and an abort
  system caps the stack instead).
- A **rideshare stack** of many small satellites on a dispenser.

```
        /\        <- nose cap
       /  \
      | SAT |     <- spacecraft
       \  /
   ----====----   <- payload adapter
```

This is the payload's only ride. Every plane below, from the
[[z=0|engines on the pad]] upward, exists to put this one section into orbit.

title: Outbound Sales Call Script - Acme Analytics
src:
---
3md: 1.0
axis: slide
title: Outbound Sales Call Script - Acme Analytics
product: Pulse (revenue analytics platform)
persona: VP of Sales at a 200-500 seat B2B SaaS company
call_length: 18 minutes
---
A discovery-led cold call script for Pulse. Each slide is one phase of the call.
Move forward only when the prospect engages; if they push back, jump to
[[z=4|the objection slide]] and come back.

@plane z=0 label="Open" x=0 y=0
# Slide 0: The Opener (first 20 seconds)

Goal: earn the next 30 seconds. Be human, be quick, ask permission.

> "Hi Dana, this is Sam from Acme. I know I'm calling out of the blue, so I'll be
> brief. Can I take 30 seconds to tell you why I called, and you can decide if it's
> worth continuing?"

- Smile before you dial. It changes your tone.
- If they say "I'm busy": "Totally fair, are mornings or afternoons better for a
  quick 10 minutes this week?"
- Do NOT pitch yet. Permission first.

Once you get the yes, go to [[z=1|the reason for the call]].

@plane z=1 label="Reason" x=1 y=0
# Slide 1: Reason for the Call (the hook)

Anchor to a specific, recognizable pain. Reference something real about their world.

> "I work with VPs of Sales at SaaS companies around your size, and the pattern I
> keep hearing is that forecast numbers and what actually closes are two different
> stories. Reps update the CRM on Friday, the forecast is stale by Tuesday, and the
> board meeting becomes a guessing game. Does any of that sound familiar, or is your
> forecasting already airtight?"

The last clause matters: give them an easy, honest out. If it lands, move to
[[z=2|discovery questions]]. If they say "we're fine," that is still data, dig
into it.

@plane z=2 label="Discover" x=2 y=0
# Slide 2: Discovery (the heart of the call)

Spend most of your time here. Ask, listen, follow the thread. Take notes on their
exact words; you will quote them back later.

1. "Walk me through how you build the forecast today. Who touches it before it
   gets to the CFO?"
2. "When the number is wrong, when do you usually find out, and how far off is it?"
3. "What does a missed quarter actually cost the team, beyond the dollars?"
4. "If you could see deal risk two weeks earlier, what would you do differently?"

Listening rules:

- Talk less than 30 percent of this slide.
- After they answer, count to two before you respond.
- Mirror their last few words to keep them going ("two weeks earlier?").

When you have a quantified pain, advance to [[z=3|the value bridge]].

@plane z=3 label="Value" x=3 y=0
# Slide 3: Connect Pulse to Their Pain

Now, and only now, tie the product to what they told you. Use their numbers.

> "You said reps update late and you find out the forecast is off about ten days
> into the quarter. Pulse pulls activity and pipeline signals automatically, so the
> forecast updates itself daily instead of waiting on Friday entries. Teams your
> size usually cut forecast error from around 20 percent down to single digits in
> the first quarter."

- Lead with the outcome, not the feature list.
- One proof point, not five: "Northstar Software went from a 22 percent miss to 6
  percent in 90 days."
- Then check in: "Is that the kind of shift that would matter to you?"

If they lean in, head to [[z=5|the close]]. If they hesitate, expect an objection
and go to [[z=4|the objection slide]].

@plane z=4 label="Objection" x=2 y=1
# Slide 4: Handle the Objection

Most calls stall here. Acknowledge, ask, then respond. Never argue.

"We already use Salesforce."

> "Makes sense, most of our customers do too. Pulse sits on top of Salesforce, it
> doesn't replace it. Think of it as the layer that reads your CRM so your reps
> don't have to babysit it. Worth seeing how that looks with your own data?"

"We don't have budget right now."

> "Understood. Help me size it: if the forecast were off by even half this quarter,
> what does that gap look like? I'd rather show you the math than push a price."

"Send me some info."

> "Happy to. So I send the right thing and not a generic deck, what is the one
> question you'd want answered? I'll tailor it and we can grab 15 minutes after."

Once the objection clears, return to [[z=5|the close]].

@plane z=5 label="Close" x=4 y=0
# Slide 5: Close for the Next Step

Do not close for the sale on a cold call. Close for a concrete, calendared step.

> "Based on what you've shared, the logical next step is a 25-minute working
> session where I plug Pulse into a slice of your real pipeline and show you the
> forecast it produces. Worst case, you get a free second opinion on your number.
> How's Thursday at 10, or does Friday morning work better?"

Closing checklist:

- Offer two specific times, not "when are you free."
- Confirm the calendar invite goes out before you hang up.
- Restate the value in one line: "I'll show you your real forecast risk, live."
- Send the recap email within 10 minutes, quoting their words from
  [[z=2|the discovery slide]].

If they decline entirely: "No problem. Can I check back next quarter?" A soft yes
is still a pipeline entry.

title: Orders Database Schema Migrations
src:
---
3md: 1.0
axis: version
title: Orders Database Schema Migrations
database: orders_prod
engine: PostgreSQL 15
tool: golang-migrate
---
The Z axis is the schema version. Each plane is one applied migration in the
`orders` service database. Read bottom-up (z=0 is the baseline) to see how the
schema evolves. Every plane shows the forward (`up`) migration, the rollback
(`down`), and the reasoning behind the change.

Rollouts are gated: a migration only ships once the plane below it is fully
deployed and the application code that depends on it is live.

@plane z=0 label="0001 baseline" x=0 y=0
# Version 0001: Baseline schema

The initial schema captured from the existing monolith before extraction. Two
tables, no foreign keys yet, integer surrogate keys.

```sql
-- up
CREATE TABLE customers (
    id          SERIAL PRIMARY KEY,
    email       TEXT NOT NULL,
    created_at  TIMESTAMPTZ NOT NULL DEFAULT now()
);

CREATE TABLE orders (
    id          SERIAL PRIMARY KEY,
    customer_id INTEGER NOT NULL,
    total_cents INTEGER NOT NULL,
    created_at  TIMESTAMPTZ NOT NULL DEFAULT now()
);
```

```sql
-- down
DROP TABLE orders;
DROP TABLE customers;
```

Known gaps fixed later: no unique constraint on `email` (see [[z=1|0002]]) and
no referential integrity between `orders` and `customers` (see [[z=2|0003]]).

@plane z=1 label="0002 unique email" x=1 y=0
# Version 0002: Enforce unique customer email

Duplicate accounts were slipping in during signup retries. We add a unique
constraint, but the table already has duplicates, so the migration de-dupes
first, keeping the lowest `id` per email.

```sql
-- up
DELETE FROM customers a
USING customers b
WHERE a.email = b.email
  AND a.id > b.id;

CREATE UNIQUE INDEX CONCURRENTLY idx_customers_email_unique
    ON customers (lower(email));
```

Note the `CONCURRENTLY` build so writes are not blocked on the live table. This
means the migration cannot run inside a transaction, so the runner is configured
with `--no-tx` for this version only.

```sql
-- down
DROP INDEX CONCURRENTLY idx_customers_email_unique;
```

Builds on [[z=0|the baseline]]. The next step adds the foreign key that the
baseline deliberately omitted: [[z=2|0003]].

@plane z=2 label="0003 FK + cascade" x=2 y=0
# Version 0003: Add foreign key to orders

With duplicates gone, we link `orders.customer_id` to `customers.id`. We first
validate there are no orphan rows, then add the constraint as `NOT VALID` and
validate it separately to avoid a long table lock.

```sql
-- up
ALTER TABLE orders
    ADD CONSTRAINT fk_orders_customer
    FOREIGN KEY (customer_id) REFERENCES customers (id)
    ON DELETE RESTRICT
    NOT VALID;

ALTER TABLE orders VALIDATE CONSTRAINT fk_orders_customer;

CREATE INDEX CONCURRENTLY idx_orders_customer_id
    ON orders (customer_id);
```

`ON DELETE RESTRICT` is intentional: deleting a customer with live orders should
fail loudly rather than silently removing order history.

```sql
-- down
DROP INDEX CONCURRENTLY idx_orders_customer_id;
ALTER TABLE orders DROP CONSTRAINT fk_orders_customer;
```

Depends on [[z=1|0002]] having removed duplicate customers. The status column
arrives in [[z=3|0004]].

@plane z=3 label="0004 order status" x=3 y=0
# Version 0004: Order status enum

Orders need a lifecycle. We introduce a native enum type and a column defaulting
to `pending`. Backfilling existing rows is implicit through the default.

```sql
-- up
CREATE TYPE order_status AS ENUM ('pending', 'paid', 'shipped', 'cancelled');

ALTER TABLE orders
    ADD COLUMN status order_status NOT NULL DEFAULT 'pending';
```

Adding a column with a constant default is metadata-only in PostgreSQL 11+, so
this does not rewrite the table and runs in milliseconds even on large tables.

```sql
-- down
ALTER TABLE orders DROP COLUMN status;
DROP TYPE order_status;
```

Caution for rollback: dropping the enum type fails if any other object still
references it, so the column drop must come first (as written above).

@plane z=4 label="0005 soft delete" x=4 y=0
# Version 0005: Soft-delete customers

`ON DELETE RESTRICT` from [[z=2|0003]] blocks hard deletes, which is what we
want, but support still needs to deactivate accounts. We switch to soft deletes
with a partial index so active lookups stay fast.

```sql
-- up
ALTER TABLE customers
    ADD COLUMN deleted_at TIMESTAMPTZ;

-- active-customer lookups only scan non-deleted rows
CREATE INDEX CONCURRENTLY idx_customers_active
    ON customers (id)
    WHERE deleted_at IS NULL;
```

Application reads now filter `WHERE deleted_at IS NULL`. This migration ships
only after that filter is live in production, so the index matches real query
shapes.

```sql
-- down
DROP INDEX CONCURRENTLY idx_customers_active;
ALTER TABLE customers DROP COLUMN deleted_at;
```

This is the current head of the migration stack. The unique email index from
[[z=1|0002]] still applies to soft-deleted rows, which is acceptable because a
freed email can be reclaimed by a future hard-purge job.

title: Hale & Okonkwo Wedding Seating Chart
src:
---
3md: 1.0
axis: space
title: Hale & Okonkwo Wedding Seating Chart
venue: The Glasshouse at Marrow Hill
date: 2026-09-19
guests: 96
planes: 4
---
The reception room is a long rectangle with the head table on the garden side
(near the windows) and the bar on the entrance side. Each plane below is one
band of the room as you walk in from the doors toward the garden. The `x`/`y`
offsets place each band roughly where it sits on the floor: `y` grows toward the
garden, `x` runs left (dance floor) to right (bar wall).

Start at the doors on [[z=0|the entrance band]] and walk forward to the
[[z=3|head table]] by the windows.

@plane z=0 x=0 y=0 label="Entrance Band (Tables 1-3)"
# Entrance Band

You come in through the double doors here. Coat rack on the left, guest book and
the framed engagement photo on the small console to the right.

## Table 1 - left of the doors
- Priya Hale (bride's aunt)
- Devon Hale (bride's uncle)
- Marcus Whitlock (college roommate, no nut allergy note needed, ignore the old card)
- Sofia Whitlock
- Reverend Allen Pace (officiant) - seat nearest the aisle so he can slip out early

## Table 2 - center
- The Okonkwo cousins: Ada, Chidi, Ngozi, Emeka
- Hannah Pereira (plus one of Emeka)
- Open seat held for late RSVP (Tobias) - confirm by Sept 12

## Table 3 - right, by the bar queue
- Work friends of Nia: Raymond Foss, Lena Foss, Kasper Lund, Dell Moreno
- Note: Dell is vegetarian, kitchen flagged

Kids' activity table is tucked behind Table 1. The crayons live in the basket
under the gift table. The next band forward is [[z=1|the middle, Tables 4-6]].

@plane z=1 x=0 y=4 label="Middle Band (Tables 4-6)"
# Middle Band

The cake table sits against the right wall in this band, wheeled in after the
toasts. Keep a clear lane between Table 5 and the wall for the cake cart.

## Table 4 - left
- Grandma Eunice Hale (closest to the dance floor edge, she likes to watch)
- Walter Hale
- The Bassey family: Ime, Grace, and their daughter Zara (booster seat needed)

## Table 5 - center
- High school crew: Jamie Ortega, Bex Ortega, Sam Idowu, Priscilla Quist
- Two open seats for the photographer and videographer between courses

## Table 6 - right, near the cake
- Nia's running club: Coach Bert, Marisol Vega, Tunde Bakare, Hollis Crane
- Marisol is gluten free, the kitchen has a separate plated dessert for her

Sightline check: from Table 4 the head table at [[z=3|the garden windows]] is
fully visible. The bar at [[z=2|the rear band]] is directly behind you.

@plane z=2 x=2 y=4 label="Bar & Lounge Wall (right side)"
# Bar and Lounge Wall

This is not a seated band so much as the right edge that runs the length of the
room. No assigned seats here; it is standing and lounge space.

## The bar
- Two bartenders from 5:30 PM
- Signature drinks: "The Marrow Hill" (bourbon, fig, lemon) and a zero-proof
  hibiscus spritz
- Last call 11:15 PM

## Lounge corner (rear right)
- Two low sofas and a coffee table for grandparents and anyone stepping away from
  the music
- This is where Grandma Eunice will end up around 9 PM, save her a sofa spot

## Logistics
- Gift table and card box at the rear, between the bar and the doors
- Accessible restroom is through the door past the lounge corner

Walk back to [[z=0|the doors]] or forward along the windows to
[[z=3|the head table]].

@plane z=3 x=0 y=8 label="Head Table (garden windows)"
# Head Table

Against the garden windows, facing the room. Long single table, everyone on one
side so the room sees the couple. Seating left to right as the guests see it.

## Order along the table
1. Best man: Femi Okonkwo
2. Groomsman: Lucas Hale (bride's brother)
3. Maid of honor: Tessa Okonkwo
4. **Adaeze Okonkwo** (one of the two of honor)
5. **Nia Hale** (one of the two of honor)
6. Bridesmaid: Camille Dubois
7. Groomsman: Oba Adeyemi
8. Bridesmaid: Iris Lin

## Notes
- Champagne pre-poured at these seats only before the toasts
- Femi (seat 1) gives the first toast, mic is on the stand at the left end
- The couple (seats 4-5) face the dance floor for the first dance hand-off

The reception flow runs from [[z=0|the doors]] through
[[z=1|the middle tables]] to here. Photographers stage at the
[[z=2|lounge wall]] for the toast wide shots.

title: The Prodigal Son - Coming Home
src:
---
3md: 1.0
axis: slide
title: The Prodigal Son - Coming Home
speaker: Pastor J. Reyes
passage: Luke 15:11-32
service: Sunday Morning
duration: 32 minutes
---
Sermon outline for the message on Luke 15:11-32, "The Prodigal Son."
Advance one slide per section. Speaker notes live under each heading.

@plane z=0 label="Title" x=0 y=0
# Coming Home

### The Prodigal Son - Luke 15:11-32

> "But while he was still a long way off, his father saw him and was
> filled with compassion for him; he ran to his son, threw his arms
> around him and kissed him." (v. 20)

**Big idea:** No distance is too far for the Father's welcome.

Open with the question: where do you picture God standing when you finally
turn back toward home? Move to [[z=1|the younger son's demand]] to begin.

@plane z=1 label="The Demand" x=-2 y=1
# 1. The Demand (vv. 11-13)

- The younger son asks for his share of the estate **while the father is
  still living** - in that culture, a wish that the father were dead.
- The father grants it. Love does not cage; it gives room to choose.
- The son "set off for a distant country" and squandered everything in
  reckless living.

**Application:** Sin is rarely a single leap. It is a slow drift toward
a country far from the Father's table.

Next: what the far country actually costs in [[z=2|The Famine]].

@plane z=2 label="The Famine" x=2 y=1
# 2. The Famine (vv. 14-16)

- A severe famine strikes; the son ends up feeding pigs - for a Jewish
  listener, the lowest, most defiling work imaginable.
- "He longed to fill his stomach with the pods... but no one gave him
  anything." (v. 16)
- The far country always over-promises and under-delivers.

**Application:** Emptiness is not the enemy here. Sometimes the famine is
the mercy that turns us back toward home.

@plane z=3 label="The Turning" x=0 y=2
# 3. The Turning (vv. 17-20a)

- "When he came to his senses..." (v. 17) - repentance begins with seeing
  clearly again.
- He rehearses a speech: "I have sinned against heaven and against you."
- Then the decisive verb: "So he got up and went to his father." (v. 20)

**Application:** Repentance is not just feeling sorry. It is standing up
and walking in a new direction - toward the Father, not away.

Set up the surprise that meets him in [[z=4|The Welcome]].

@plane z=4 label="The Welcome" x=2 y=3
# 4. The Welcome (vv. 20b-24)

- The father **runs** - undignified for an elder in that day - and embraces
  him before the speech is even finished.
- Robe, ring, sandals, fattened calf: full restoration, not probation.
- "This son of mine was dead and is alive again; he was lost and is found."

**Application:** Grace runs to meet you. You do not clean yourself up first;
you come home, and the cleaning happens in the Father's arms.

@plane z=5 label="The Invitation" x=0 y=4
# 5. The Invitation (vv. 25-32)

- The older brother is angry and will not go in. He has obeyed for years
  yet never felt like a son.
- The father goes out to him too: "You are always with me, and everything
  I have is yours." (v. 31)
- The story ends unresolved - an open door, and the question hanging in the
  air: will you come in?

**Closing call:** Whether you ran to the far country or stayed home and grew
bitter, the Father is outside, pleading. Come to the table.

Return to [[z=0|the opening question]] and pray.

title: MS Corvid Aurora - Deck Plan
src:
---
3md: 1.0
axis: floor
title: MS Corvid Aurora - Deck Plan
vessel: MS Corvid Aurora
gross_tonnage: "142,000 GT"
guest_capacity: "3,560"
length_overall: 330 m
---
Deck plan for the **MS Corvid Aurora**, read from the keel upward. Each plane is one
deck; the `z` value is the deck number. Public spaces sit low and amidships, cabins
stack in the middle, and the open recreation decks crown the ship.

Muster stations are on Deck 4 (see [[z=4|the Promenade muster map]]). For dining,
jump to [[z=5|Deck 5]].

@plane z=1 label="Deck 1 - Crew and Machinery" x=0 y=0
# Deck 1 - Crew and Machinery

The lowest guest-adjacent deck. Most of this level is off-limits to passengers.

- **Engine control room** (frames 40-72): twin medium-speed diesel-electric plants.
- **Crew mess and galley** (amidships): three sittings, 0600 to 2200.
- **Provision stores**: refrigerated and dry holds, restocked at every turnaround port.
- **Laundry**: 14,000 kg of linen processed per sea day.

Guest access is limited to the **medical center** forward (frames 12-20), staffed
around the clock with two physicians and four nurses. The infirmary connects by a
dedicated lift to the gangway on [[z=4|Deck 4]] for shoreside transfers.

@plane z=4 label="Deck 4 - Promenade and Muster" x=0 y=0
# Deck 4 - Promenade and Muster

The teak **Promenade** wraps the full hull at this level, a continuous 0.4 km loop
(three laps to the mile). This is the ship's safety hub.

## Muster stations

| Station | Location | Assembly area |
|---------|----------|---------------|
| A | Forward, port | Aurora Theater stalls |
| B | Forward, starboard | Aurora Theater balcony |
| C | Amidships | Atrium, decks 4-6 |
| D | Aft, port | Horizon Lounge |
| E | Aft, starboard | Card Room and Library |

Lifeboats hang from davits along both sides at this deck. The **gangway** is here
at frame 16, port side, used for tendering and port calls.

Take the central stair up one level to reach the restaurants on [[z=5|Deck 5]],
or continue to the cabins beginning on [[z=7|Deck 7]].

@plane z=5 label="Deck 5 - Dining and Atrium" x=0 y=0
# Deck 5 - Dining and Atrium

The social spine of the ship. A three-story **Atrium** rises through decks 4 to 6,
crowned by a sculptural glass chandelier.

## Where to eat

- **The Meridian** (aft, frames 64-88): main dining room, two seatings plus open
  flexible dining. Floor-to-ceiling stern windows.
- **Saffron** (port, midship): pan-Asian a la carte, reservations suggested.
- **Helm & Anchor** (starboard, midship): grill and raw bar.
- **The Pantry** (forward): casual marketplace buffet, open 24 hours.

The **Guest Services** and **Shore Excursions** desks ring the Atrium base. For the
show after dinner, the Aurora Theater spans down to the muster level on
[[z=4|Deck 4]]; head up to the cabins on [[z=7|Deck 7]] to turn in.

@plane z=7 label="Deck 7 - Staterooms" x=0 y=0
# Deck 7 - Staterooms

The first of the accommodation decks. Cabin numbers run **7001-7088** port and
**7501-7588** starboard, lowest forward.

## Cabin categories on this deck

- **Interior** (no window): frames 8-28, forward.
- **Oceanview** (picture window): along the hull, amidships.
- **Balcony** (private veranda): aft two-thirds of the deck.

Odd numbers are port, even numbers are starboard. A self-service **launderette**
sits at the midship lift lobby, along with ice and a vending alcove.

Three banks of guest lifts connect every passenger deck. From the lobby you can
drop to dining on [[z=5|Deck 5]] or ride up to the pools on [[z=14|Deck 14]].

@plane z=14 label="Deck 14 - Pool and Lido" x=0 y=0
# Deck 14 - Pool and Lido

The open-air recreation deck, wind shadowed by the superstructure forward.

## Layout, bow to stern

1. **Sanctuary** (forward, adults only): shaded loungers and a quiet bar.
2. **Main pool and two whirlpools** (amidships): retractable glass roof for cool weather.
3. **The Galley Grill** and **Scoops** ice cream (poolside).
4. **Wave Stage** (aft): live music and the evening movie screen.

A jogging track and the **Vista Spa** sun terrace sit one half-level up at the
forward mast house. Loungers fill fast on sea days, so claim one early. To return
to your room, the aft lifts run straight down to [[z=7|Deck 7]]; for a late bite,
The Pantry on [[z=5|Deck 5]] stays open all night.

title: Stormcaller Skill Tree
src:
---
3md: 1.0
axis: depth
title: Stormcaller Skill Tree
class: Stormcaller (lightning mage)
notation: SP means skill points
---
The Stormcaller tree spends from the surface inward. Tier 1 sits at the
foreground (the skills you reach first), and each deeper plane gates behind the
one above it. Read from `z=0` down to `z=3`; deeper tiers cost more SP and demand
prerequisites from the tier above.

Total to fully clear: 31 SP across four tiers.

@plane z=0 label="Tier 1 - Foundations" x=0 y=0
# Tier 1: Foundations

No prerequisites. Available at level 1. Spend here first.

| Skill        | Cost | Effect                                            |
|--------------|------|---------------------------------------------------|
| Spark        | 1 SP | Bolt for 12 damage, 4m range, no cooldown         |
| Static Field | 2 SP | Standing still builds a charge that shocks on hit |
| Quick Hands  | 1 SP | Cast speed +8%                                     |

Spend a point in Spark to open the path to [[z=1|Tier 2: Conduction]].

> Tip: Static Field pairs with kiting. Stop, soak a charge, then move.

@plane z=1 label="Tier 2 - Conduction" x=2 y=0
# Tier 2: Conduction

Requires 3 SP spent in [[z=0|Tier 1]] and character level 8.

| Skill        | Cost | Prerequisite | Effect                                  |
|--------------|------|--------------|-----------------------------------------|
| Chain Bolt   | 2 SP | Spark        | Lightning arcs to 2 nearby enemies      |
| Grounding    | 2 SP | Static Field | -20% damage taken while channeling      |
| Overload     | 3 SP | Quick Hands  | Crits refund 1 charge of Static Field   |

Chain Bolt is the backbone of every later build. Take it before anything else
here, then descend to [[z=2|Tier 3: Tempest]].

@plane z=2 label="Tier 3 - Tempest" x=4 y=0
# Tier 3: Tempest

Requires 7 SP spent across Tiers 1 and 2, plus Chain Bolt, and level 16.

| Skill         | Cost | Prerequisite | Effect                                       |
|---------------|------|--------------|----------------------------------------------|
| Thunderhead   | 4 SP | Chain Bolt   | Summon a cloud that strikes every 2 seconds  |
| Insulation    | 3 SP | Grounding    | Immune to chain damage from your own bolts   |
| Resonance     | 4 SP | Overload     | Each chain jump adds +15% damage to the next |

Two viable splits from here:

- **Burst:** Thunderhead plus Resonance for stacking arc damage.
- **Sustain:** Thunderhead plus Insulation for safer crowd clears.

The capstone at [[z=3|Tier 4: Apex]] unlocks once Thunderhead is maxed.

@plane z=3 label="Tier 4 - Apex" x=6 y=0
# Tier 4: Apex (Capstone)

Requires Thunderhead maxed, 14 SP spent in the tree, and level 24. Pick exactly
one of the two capstones below. The choice is permanent until a respec.

## Stormlord (offense)

- Cost: 6 SP
- Thunderhead becomes mobile and follows you.
- Resonance stacks no longer reset between targets.
- Builds on [[z=2|Resonance]] for runaway scaling against packs.

## Tempest Warden (defense)

- Cost: 6 SP
- Grounding and Insulation now apply to your whole party within 8m.
- Killing a shocked enemy grants the party a 3-second damage shield.
- Builds on [[z=2|Insulation]] for raid survivability.

Either capstone closes the tree. Respec at any shrine for 500 gold to walk back
up to [[z=0|Tier 1]] and rebuild.

title: The Solar System by Distance
src:
---
3md: 1.0
axis: space
title: The Solar System by Distance
subtitle: Eight planets, ordered outward from the Sun
units: astronomical units (1 AU is the Earth-Sun distance)
author: CorvidLabs Gallery
---
A walk outward from the Sun. Each plane sits at the planet's mean orbital
distance, mapped so that `z` equals the distance in astronomical units (AU).
The inner rocky worlds are packed close together near `z=1`, while the outer
gas and ice giants spread out across tens of AU. Start at [[z=0.39|Mercury]]
and travel to the edge at [[z=30.07|Neptune]].

@plane z=0.39 x=0 y=0 label="Mercury"
# Mercury (0.39 AU)

The smallest planet and the closest to the Sun. Mercury races around its orbit
in just 88 Earth days, the fastest year in the system.

- **Type:** rocky (terrestrial)
- **Day length:** 59 Earth days (a single rotation)
- **Surface:** scorched by day, frozen by night, swinging from +430 C to -180 C
- **Moons:** none

No atmosphere worth the name means no blanket to trap heat, so the temperature
swing between the sunlit and shadowed sides is the most extreme of any planet.

Next stop outward: [[z=0.72|Venus]].

@plane z=0.72 x=0.33 y=0 label="Venus"
# Venus (0.72 AU)

Earth's near-twin in size, and the hottest planet in the system. A thick
carbon-dioxide atmosphere traps heat so efficiently that the surface sits at
about 465 C, hotter than Mercury despite being farther from the Sun.

- **Type:** rocky (terrestrial)
- **Atmosphere:** 96 percent CO2, surface pressure about 92 times Earth's
- **Spin:** retrograde (the Sun rises in the west) and very slow
- **Moons:** none

A day on Venus (243 Earth days) is longer than its year (225 Earth days).

Inward to [[z=0.39|Mercury]], or onward to [[z=1.0|Earth]].

@plane z=1.0 x=0.66 y=0 label="Earth"
# Earth (1.00 AU)

The reference point. One AU is defined by Earth's average distance from the Sun,
about 150 million kilometers, which is why this plane sits at exactly `z=1`.

- **Type:** rocky (terrestrial)
- **Atmosphere:** 78 percent nitrogen, 21 percent oxygen
- **Surface:** the only known world with stable liquid water and life
- **Moons:** 1 (the Moon, at roughly 384,000 km)

Sunlight takes about 8 minutes and 20 seconds to reach here.

The next world out is the red one: [[z=1.52|Mars]].

@plane z=1.52 x=1.0 y=0 label="Mars"
# Mars (1.52 AU)

The red planet, colored by iron oxide dust. Mars is half Earth's diameter and
hosts the tallest volcano in the system, Olympus Mons, rising about 22 km.

- **Type:** rocky (terrestrial)
- **Day length:** 24 hours 37 minutes (close to Earth's)
- **Atmosphere:** thin CO2, about 0.6 percent of Earth's surface pressure
- **Moons:** 2 (Phobos and Deimos, both small and lumpy)

Beyond Mars lies the asteroid belt, the boundary between the rocky inner system
and the giants. Cross it to reach [[z=5.2|Jupiter]].

@plane z=5.2 x=2.5 y=0 label="Jupiter"
# Jupiter (5.20 AU)

The giant. Jupiter holds more than twice the mass of every other planet
combined, and its Great Red Spot is a storm wide enough to swallow Earth.

- **Type:** gas giant
- **Day length:** under 10 hours (the fastest spin in the system)
- **Composition:** mostly hydrogen and helium, no solid surface
- **Moons:** 95 confirmed, including the four large Galilean moons (Io, Europa,
  Ganymede, Callisto)

Note the jump in scale: Jupiter sits more than three times farther out than
Mars, and the spacing only grows from here.

Onward to the ringed world: [[z=9.58|Saturn]].

@plane z=9.58 x=4.5 y=0 label="Saturn"
# Saturn (9.58 AU)

Famous for its rings, a vast disk of ice and rock chunks only tens of meters
thick but spanning hundreds of thousands of kilometers. Saturn is the least
dense planet, lighter than water on average.

- **Type:** gas giant
- **Day length:** about 10.7 hours
- **Rings:** seven main groups, mostly water ice
- **Moons:** 146 confirmed, including Titan (thicker atmosphere than Earth's)

From here the system turns cold and blue. Continue to [[z=19.22|Uranus]].

@plane z=19.22 x=8 y=0 label="Uranus"
# Uranus (19.22 AU)

The tilted ice giant. Uranus rotates almost on its side, tipped about 98
degrees, so each pole spends roughly 42 years in sunlight and 42 in darkness.

- **Type:** ice giant
- **Color:** pale cyan from methane in the upper atmosphere
- **Day length:** about 17 hours, retrograde
- **Moons:** 28 confirmed, named for characters from Shakespeare and Pope

Nearly twice as far out as Saturn. One step remains to the edge: [[z=30.07|Neptune]].

@plane z=30.07 x=12 y=0 label="Neptune"
# Neptune (30.07 AU)

The outermost planet, a deep-blue ice giant with the fastest winds in the
system, reaching about 2,000 km/h. Sunlight here is roughly 900 times fainter
than on Earth, and a single Neptune year lasts about 165 Earth years.

- **Type:** ice giant
- **Discovery:** found in 1846 by mathematical prediction before it was seen
- **Day length:** about 16 hours
- **Moons:** 16 confirmed, including Triton, which orbits backward

This is the end of the planetary road. Beyond lies the Kuiper Belt, home to
Pluto and other dwarf planets.

Jump back to the start at [[z=1.0|Earth]] or all the way in to [[z=0.39|Mercury]].

title: Midnight Driver - Stem Breakdown
src:
---
3md: 1.0
axis: layer
title: "Midnight Driver - Stem Breakdown"
artist: The Sodium Lamps
bpm: 102
key: A minor
time_signature: 4/4
sample_rate: 48 kHz
session_length: 3:48
mix_engineer: R. Okafor
---
"Midnight Driver" is a 102 BPM track in A minor, printed here as separate
instrument stems instead of a single flat mix. Each plane is one stem: solo it
to study a part, stack several to rebuild the arrangement. The planes are ordered
bottom to top the way an engineer thinks about a mix, from the low-end foundation
up to the vocal that sits on top.

A few shared facts hold across every stem: the tempo is steady at 102 BPM (no
click drift), the song stays in A minor throughout, and all stems are exported
at 48 kHz, 24-bit, peak-normalized to -1 dBFS with no master-bus processing so
they sum cleanly. To hear the rhythmic core on its own, start with the
[[z=0|Drums]]; to hear what everything is built to support, jump to the
[[z=4|Lead Vocal]].

@plane z=0 x=0 y=0 label="Drums"
# Drums (the floor)

The rhythmic foundation. Recorded as a full kit, then bounced to one stem with
the room mics folded in so the groove keeps its natural ambience.

- **Pattern:** straight backbeat, kick on 1 and 3, snare on 2 and 4
- **Hi-hats:** eighth notes in the verse, opening to quarters in the chorus
- **Fills:** two-bar crash fill into each chorus, a longer tom run into the bridge
- **Processing baked in:** light bus compression (4:1, slow attack) for glue

The kick is tuned low and short so it leaves room underneath. Everything else in
the song is timed against this stem, so it is the natural reference when lining up
parts. The next thing to lock to the kick is the [[z=1|Bass]].

@plane z=1 x=0 y=-1 label="Bass"
# Bass (locks to the kick)

A fingered electric bass that doubles the kick pattern and outlines the chord
roots. This is the bridge between rhythm and harmony.

- **Tone:** rounded, slight high-mid bump so it reads on small speakers
- **Note choice:** roots on the downbeats, walking eighths into chord changes
- **Root motion (verse):** Am - F - C - G, two bars each
- **Performance note:** muted ghost notes on the "and" of beat 2 to push the groove

Solo the drums and bass together and you already have the whole rhythm section.
On top of that foundation we stack the harmony, played by the
[[z=2|Rhythm Guitars and Keys]].

@plane z=2 x=-1 y=1 label="Harmony"
# Rhythm Guitars and Keys (the harmonic bed)

The chord layer that fills the middle of the spectrum. Two parts share this stem:
a clean electric guitar panned left and an electric piano panned right, so the
harmony spreads across the stereo field.

- **Guitar:** muted eighth-note strums, capo at the 2nd fret, panned 40 left
- **Electric piano:** sustained chords with a slow tremolo, panned 35 right
- **Chords (chorus):** F - C - G - Am, brighter voicings than the verse
- **Arrangement trick:** the piano drops out in verse 2 to make space, then
  returns for the final chorus to lift the energy

This bed is deliberately quiet under the vocal and louder in the instrumental
sections. It leans on the [[z=1|Bass]] for its low notes and clears the high end
for the [[z=4|Lead Vocal]]. The hooks on top of it come from the
[[z=3|Lead Instruments]].

@plane z=3 x=1 y=1 label="Leads"
# Lead Instruments (the hooks)

The melodic counter-lines that answer the vocal: a saturated lead guitar and a
short synth motif. They mostly play in the gaps between vocal phrases.

- **Lead guitar:** the chorus riff, a four-note hook on the high E and B strings
- **Synth:** a plucky arpeggio in the intro and outro, doubled an octave up
- **Solo:** sixteen bars over the bridge, lead guitar with a long delay tail
- **Mix placement:** centered but pushed back with reverb so it sits behind vocals

When the [[z=4|Lead Vocal]] rests, this stem steps forward; when the vocal sings,
it tucks back. Mute it and the song still works but loses its signature riff.

@plane z=4 x=0 y=2 label="Lead Vocal"
# Lead Vocal (the top)

The focal point. A single performer, double-tracked only in the choruses for
width, sitting on top of everything else in the mix.

- **Range:** roughly A3 to E5, comfortable chest voice with a few falsetto lifts
- **Verses:** single take, dry and close, intimate
- **Choruses:** double-tracked and panned slightly wide, with a harmony a third up
- **Effects baked in:** plate reverb and a slap delay on phrase endings only

This is the part every other stem is arranged to support: the [[z=0|Drums]] set
the pace, the [[z=1|Bass]] and [[z=2|Rhythm Guitars and Keys]] hold the harmony,
and the [[z=3|Lead Instruments]] fill the spaces around the words. Solo this stem
to study phrasing; unmute the rest to hear it back in context.

title: ASCII Jump Sprite Sequence
src:
---
3md: 1.0
axis: frame
title: ASCII Jump Sprite Sequence
fps: 12
loop: false
direction: rightward
gravity: 9.8 (stylized)
artist: CorvidLabs
---
A five-frame character jump drawn in monospace ASCII, the kind you would wire to
the jump button in a side-scrolling platformer. Play the frames in order
(0, 1, 2, 3, 4), holding [[z=2|the apex]] for a beat or two of hang time, then
either snap back to [[z=0|the idle stance]] on landing or chain into a run.

Each plane is one drawn cel plus the engine notes (vertical velocity, collision
box, and which input or trigger advances to the next frame). Render at 12 fps for
a snappy arcade feel; stretch frames 1 and 3 by a frame each for a floatier,
moon-gravity jump. The `x`/`y` offsets trace the arc of the body's center so a
spatial viewer can lay the cels out along the jump path.

@plane z=0 x=0 y=0 label="Crouch / Anticipation"
# Frame 0 - Crouch (Anticipation)

The jump button is pressed and the character coils down for one frame before
leaving the ground. This squash is the windup that sells the launch: the more
the knees bend here, the higher the jump reads.

```
   ___
  (o.o)
  /[ ]\
 _|   |_
```

- Vertical velocity: 0 (still grounded)
- Pose: knees bent, arms drawn back, weight low over the feet
- Collision box: full width, short height (crouched)
- Advance on: jump input held for 1 frame, then release into [[z=1|the launch]]

Tip: hold this frame an extra tick if the player taps jump while moving fast, so
a running jump still gets its windup.

@plane z=1 x=3 y=-4 label="Launch / Push-off"
# Frame 1 - Launch (Push-off)

Legs snap straight and the body stretches upward as the feet leave the floor.
This is the fastest-moving frame, so a slight vertical smear or a single-pixel
trail sells the speed. Velocity is at its peak and positive (rising).

```
  \o/
   |
  / \
   ' '
```

- Vertical velocity: maximum upward (initial jump impulse)
