- Pose: arms thrown up, legs extending, toes pointing down
- Collision box: tall and narrow (stretched)
- Advance on: one frame elapsed, gravity begins to subtract from velocity

Coming up off the floor leads into the slowing rise of [[z=2|the apex]].

@plane z=2 x=6 y=-7 label="Apex / Hang Time"
# Frame 2 - Apex (Hang Time)

The top of the arc. Vertical velocity has decayed to roughly zero, so this is the
frame to hold for hang time. Tucking the knees here reads as control and gives
the player a clear "now I am floating" beat before the fall.

```
   o
  /|\
  / \
```

- Vertical velocity: near 0 (rising has stopped, falling has not begun)
- Pose: knees tucked, arms out for balance, body compact at the peak
- Collision box: compact (tucked)
- Advance on: hold 1 to 3 frames, then let gravity pull into [[z=3|the fall]]

This is the only frame you should ever pause on; everything before it is
[[z=1|the rise]] and everything after is the drop.

@plane z=3 x=9 y=-4 label="Fall / Descent"
# Frame 3 - Fall (Descent)

Gravity wins and the character drops. The body unfolds and the legs reach down,
anticipating the ground. Velocity is now negative and growing as the fall
accelerates, the mirror of the launch frame.

```
  \ /
   |
  /o\
  | |
```

- Vertical velocity: increasing downward (accelerating fall)
- Pose: arms lifting from the drop, legs extending toward the floor
- Collision box: tall again, feet leading the descent
- Advance on: collision with ground detected, snap to [[z=4|the landing]]

If the player holds a direction here, carry horizontal momentum so the landing
lands forward of [[z=0|the original crouch]].

@plane z=4 x=12 y=0 label="Land / Recovery"
# Frame 4 - Land (Recovery)

The feet hit and the body absorbs the impact with a deep squash, the heaviest
pose of the sequence. A small dust puff or a one-frame screen nudge here makes
the landing feel weighty. After this recovery the character either eases back to
neutral or buffers straight into a run.

```
  ___
 (-.-)
 /[ ]\
=|   |=
```

- Vertical velocity: 0 (grounded, impact absorbed)
- Pose: deep crouch, arms low, dust kicking out at the feet
- Collision box: widest and shortest of the sequence (impact squash)
- Advance on: 1 to 2 recovery frames, then return to [[z=0|the idle crouch]]

Loop note: this jump does not auto-repeat. Recovery resolves to idle and waits
for the next jump input, so playback ends here unless the player presses again.

title: A Doll's House, Act III - Stage Set Design
src:
---
3md: 1.0
axis: space
title: A Doll's House, Act III - Stage Set Design
production: Henrik Ibsen, revival
venue: Proscenium house, 38-foot opening
designer: scenic department
scale: 1/2 inch = 1 foot
---
A depth-staged scenic plan for the Helmer drawing room. Each plane is a slice of the
set measured upstage from the plaster line (the front edge of the playing space).
The `z` value is feet of depth: z=0 sits at the apron, larger `z` recedes away from
the audience. Read it front to back to walk the eye from the orchestra rail to the
painted backing behind the rear window.

@plane z=0 label="Apron and Footlights" y=-2
# Apron (z = 0 ft, plaster line)

The thrust of bare stage between the audience and the proscenium arch. Nothing is
built here; it is the actor's closest approach to the house.

- **Floor:** scrubbed pine planking, stained warm honey, raked 1:24 toward the seats
- **Footlight trough:** a row of shielded warm-white LEDs, dimmed to 15 percent for
  the final confrontation
- **Sightline note:** anyone standing forward of this line is lit hard from below;
  keep blocking upstage of the arch except for Nora's exit

The whole room beyond reads as a box framed by the arch. The set proper begins at
[[z=4|the parlor furniture]] one stride upstage.

@plane z=4 label="Parlor Furniture" x=-3
# Downstage parlor (z = 4 ft)

The intimate playing area, where most of Act III is staged. Furniture is real and
load-bearing because actors sit, lean, and slam things.

| Piece | Position | Note |
| --- | --- | --- |
| Round table | stage left of center | the letter is read here |
| Two armchairs | flanking the table | upholstered horsehair, period-correct |
| Small sofa | stage right | Nora's "doll" corner |

- The stove is practical: a flicker-bulb behind mica glass throws orange onto the
  rug when the room dims.
- A porcelain lamp on the table is the warm key light for the tarantella aftermath.

Sightlines from the side seats are tight here; nothing taller than 30 inches lives
between the sofa and [[z=8|the rear wall]].

@plane z=8 label="Rear Wall and Doors" x=0 y=1
# Rear wall (z = 8 ft)

The architectural backbone of the box set: a papered flat wall pierced by two
doorways. This is the "fourth side" of the room turned away from us.

1. **Hall door, stage right.** The famous door Nora walks through. Built heavy,
   hung on a real mortise lock so the closing slam carries to the back row.
2. **Study door, stage left.** Torvald's room; backed with a warm amber glow so it
   reads as occupied even when shut.

Wall treatment:

- Wallpaper: muted teal damask, knocked back with a grey wash so costumes pop
- A framed engraving and a wall clock (set to 11:00) dress the stage-right panel
- Crown molding returns 18 inches onto the side walls to fake the corner

Through the gap between the doors you glimpse [[z=12|the window and its backing]].

@plane z=12 label="Window and Snow Backing" y=2
# Upstage window (z = 12 ft)

The deepest built element: a tall casement window centered between the two doors,
opening onto the painted exterior.

- **Window:** double casement, real muntins, gauze sheers that catch a cold blue
  side-light from offstage left
- **Practical snow:** a slow drift of paper flakes tumbles behind the gauze on a
  loop, lit cold so winter presses against the warm room
- The contrast of the cold window against [[z=4|the warm lamp downstage]] is the
  visual spine of the act: home versus the world Nora is about to enter

@plane z=16 label="Painted Drop and Cyclorama"
# Far backing (z = 16 ft)

The last plane, hung behind the window opening. The audience never sees it whole,
only the slice framed by the casement, which is exactly why it can be cheap and
broad.

- **Backdrop:** a soft-focus painted street of Norwegian townhouses under snow,
  values kept low so it never competes with the actors
- **Cyclorama:** a plain muslin cyc behind the drop, washed in deep indigo to
  suggest a winter night sky
- **Glow:** a faint gaslamp warm spot painted into the drop, the only point of
  exterior light, sitting dead center in [[z=12|the window frame]]

Striking order at the end of the run: this drop flies out first, then the rear
wall breaks apart, then the parlor clears toward [[z=0|the apron]].

title: Account Settings Screen, Theme Variants
src:
---
3md: 1.0
axis: layer
title: Account Settings Screen, Theme Variants
component: SettingsCard
viewport: 375x812
tokens: design-tokens/v3
---
One screen, the account settings card, described three times. Each plane is the
same layout and the same copy; only the resolved design tokens change. The light
layer is the default, the dark layer remaps surfaces and text, and the
high-contrast layer pushes ratios past WCAG AAA for accessibility audits.

Read the planes as overlays of one another: identical geometry, different
treatment. Cross-plane links point at the matching region in a sibling layer so
you can diff a single control across themes.

@plane z=0 label="Light" x=0 y=0
# Settings Card, Light

The baseline theme. Warm neutral surfaces on white, ink-dark text, a single
brand accent for the primary action.

## Resolved tokens

| Token              | Value     | Role                          |
| ------------------ | --------- | ----------------------------- |
| `--bg-canvas`      | `#FFFFFF` | Page behind the card          |
| `--bg-surface`     | `#F6F5F2` | Card fill                     |
| `--text-primary`   | `#1A1A1E` | Headings, labels              |
| `--text-muted`     | `#5C5C66` | Helper text, captions         |
| `--accent`         | `#2F6BFF` | Primary button, focus ring    |
| `--border-hair`    | `#E2E0DB` | Dividers, input outlines      |

## Layout

```
+--------------------------------------+
|  Account                             |
|  jamie@corvidlabs.dev                |
|  --------------------------------    |
|  [ Email notifications      (on) ]   |
|  [ Two-factor auth         (off) ]   |
|                                      |
|         ( Save changes )             |
+--------------------------------------+
```

- Surface to text contrast: `#F6F5F2` on `#1A1A1E` is about 14.8:1.
- The accent (`#2F6BFF`) carries the only saturated color, so the eye lands on
  Save changes first.
- Toggle "on" state uses the accent fill; "off" uses `--border-hair`.

Compare this exact card in [[z=1|the dark layer]] or push it to
[[z=2|high contrast]].

@plane z=1 label="Dark" x=0 y=0
# Settings Card, Dark

Same card, remapped for low-light. We do not simply invert: surfaces sit a step
above pure black so the card reads as an elevated panel, and the accent is
lightened so it still pops against a dark field.

## Resolved tokens

| Token              | Value     | Role                          |
| ------------------ | --------- | ----------------------------- |
| `--bg-canvas`      | `#0B0B0F` | Page behind the card          |
| `--bg-surface`     | `#17171C` | Card fill (elevation 1)       |
| `--text-primary`   | `#F2F2F5` | Headings, labels              |
| `--text-muted`     | `#9A9AA4` | Helper text, captions         |
| `--accent`         | `#6E9BFF` | Primary button, focus ring    |
| `--border-hair`    | `#2A2A31` | Dividers, input outlines      |

## What changed from the light layer

- Canvas drops to near-black (`#0B0B0F`), the card lifts to `#17171C` so the
  edge is visible without a hard border.
- Accent shifts from `#2F6BFF` to `#6E9BFF`. The darker blue would muddy against
  the dark surface, so we raise lightness to keep a 5.1:1 ratio on the button.
- Muted text is recomputed (`#9A9AA4`) to hold above 4.5:1 on the card fill,
  matching the muted role in [[z=0|the light layer]].

## Layout

```
+--------------------------------------+
|  Account                             |
|  jamie@corvidlabs.dev                |
|  --------------------------------    |
|  [ Email notifications      (on) ]   |
|  [ Two-factor auth         (off) ]   |
|                                      |
|         ( Save changes )             |
+--------------------------------------+
```

Geometry is byte-for-byte identical to light; only fills and strokes resolve
differently. For the audited version, see [[z=2|high contrast]].

@plane z=2 label="High Contrast" x=0 y=0
# Settings Card, High Contrast

The accessibility layer, tuned for low-vision users and forced-colors mode. Every
foreground-on-background pair clears WCAG AAA (7:1 for body text), borders become
explicit rather than hairline, and the focus ring is doubled in weight.

## Resolved tokens

| Token              | Value     | Role                              |
| ------------------ | --------- | --------------------------------- |
| `--bg-canvas`      | `#000000` | Page behind the card              |
| `--bg-surface`     | `#000000` | Card fill (flat, no elevation)    |
| `--text-primary`   | `#FFFFFF` | Headings, labels                  |
| `--text-muted`     | `#E8E8E8` | Helper text (still about 17:1)    |
| `--accent`         | `#FFD400` | Primary button, focus ring        |
| `--border-strong`  | `#FFFFFF` | 2px solid outline on every edge   |

## What changed from the dark layer

- Surfaces collapse to pure black and text to pure white, the maximum 21:1 pair,
  versus roughly 13:1 in [[z=1|the dark layer]].
- Hairline dividers are replaced by `--border-strong`, a solid 2px white outline,
  so card edges, inputs, and toggles never rely on subtle fills.
- The accent moves to amber (`#FFD400`) because blue cannot reach 7:1 against
  black at a comfortable size. Amber on black is about 15.9:1.
- Toggle states no longer lean on color alone: "on" shows a filled box with a
  checkmark, "off" shows an empty box.

## Layout

```
+======================================+
|  Account                             |
|  jamie@corvidlabs.dev                |
|  ==============================      |
|  [ Email notifications     [x]on ]   |
|  [ Two-factor auth         [ ]off ]  |
|                                      |
|        [[ Save changes ]]            |
+======================================+
```

Back to the default treatment: [[z=0|light layer]]. Same card, three honest
renderings of the same screen.

title: Threat Model by Depth - Customer Records API
src:
---
3md: 1.0
axis: depth
title: Threat Model by Depth - Customer Records API
system: orders-api
methodology: STRIDE + attack-surface layering
reviewed: 2026-06-23
owner: platform-security
---
This threat model stacks the same system along a depth axis, from the
outermost attacker-facing surface down to the data at rest. Each plane
holds the assets, trust boundaries, and mitigations that live at that
depth. Read it front to back to follow an attacker's path inward, or jump
straight to the layer you own.

Depth convention: z=0 is the public edge (closest to the attacker), and
larger z values sit deeper inside the trust perimeter. The deepest plane
(the database) is the asset everything else exists to protect.

@plane z=0 label="Public edge" x=0 y=0
# Depth 0 - Public Edge

The internet-facing surface: DNS, the CDN, and the TLS-terminating load
balancer. Everything here is reachable by anonymous, unauthenticated
clients, so it is the broadest and least-trusted layer.

## Assets
- Public hostname `api.example.com` and its DNS records
- TLS certificate and private key (terminated at the load balancer)
- Edge WAF rules and rate-limit counters

## Trust boundary
Anonymous internet -> edge. Treat every byte as hostile.

## STRIDE highlights
- **Spoofing**: domain takeover via a dangling CNAME pointing at a
  decommissioned bucket.
- **Tampering**: request smuggling through inconsistent header parsing
  between the CDN and the origin.
- **Denial of service**: volumetric floods and slow-loris connections
  exhausting the listener pool.

## Mitigations
- HSTS with preload, TLS 1.2 minimum, and OCSP stapling.
- Monitored DNS with CAA records; alert on any record change.
- Anycast scrubbing plus per-IP and per-token rate limits at the WAF.

Once a request survives the edge it reaches the gateway, where it must
prove who it is: see [[z=1|the authentication layer]].

@plane z=1 label="Authentication and gateway" x=0 y=1
# Depth 1 - Authentication and Gateway

The API gateway authenticates callers and applies coarse authorization
before any business logic runs. This is the first layer that distinguishes
one principal from another.

## Assets
- OAuth2 / OIDC token validation keys (JWKS)
- Session and refresh tokens
- API keys issued to partner integrations

## Trust boundary
Unauthenticated request -> authenticated principal. Crossing it grants an
identity, so the integrity of token validation is critical.

## STRIDE highlights
- **Spoofing**: forged JWTs from an `alg: none` downgrade or a leaked
  signing key.
- **Elevation of privilege**: a token minted for a low-tier partner being
  accepted for admin scopes because audience and scope claims are not
  checked.
- **Repudiation**: missing correlation IDs make it impossible to tie an
  action back to a specific token.

## Mitigations
- Validate `alg`, `iss`, `aud`, and `exp` on every token; pin the JWKS and
  reject unknown key IDs.
- Short token lifetimes with rotating refresh tokens; revocation list
  checked at the gateway.
- Stamp every authenticated request with an immutable correlation ID that
  flows down to [[z=4|the audit log]].

The attacker who got past the edge ([[z=0|public edge]]) now needs a valid
identity. With one in hand, they reach the application logic at
[[z=2|the service layer]].

@plane z=2 label="Application service" x=0 y=2
# Depth 2 - Application Service

The order and customer microservices: where authenticated requests turn
into business actions. Authorization decisions and input handling live
here.

## Assets
- Business logic for reading and mutating customer and order records
- Per-resource authorization rules (who may see which customer)
- Internal service-to-service credentials

## Trust boundary
Authenticated principal -> authorized action on a specific resource. A
valid token does not imply access to a given record.

## STRIDE highlights
- **Information disclosure**: insecure direct object reference, where
  `GET /customers/{id}` returns any id the caller names.
- **Tampering**: mass-assignment letting a client set `role` or
  `account_balance` through an unfiltered request body.
- **Elevation of privilege**: server-side request forgery from an
  order-import feature reaching the internal metadata service.

## Mitigations
- Enforce object-level authorization on every record access, not just at
  the route. Default deny.
- Allowlist writable fields per endpoint; reject unknown keys.
- Egress allowlist for outbound calls; block link-local and metadata IP
  ranges.

Validated, authorized requests now read and write through the persistence
layer at [[z=3|the data access layer]].

@plane z=3 label="Data access" x=0 y=3
# Depth 3 - Data Access Layer

The ORM, query builders, and the cache that sit between the service and the
database. This layer shapes how data is fetched and is a classic injection
chokepoint.

## Assets
- Parameterized query templates and the ORM mapping
- Connection pool credentials to the database
- The read-through cache (may hold sensitive rows in memory)

## Trust boundary
Application intent -> concrete database operations. A flaw here can turn a
benign request into an arbitrary query.

## STRIDE highlights
- **Tampering / disclosure**: SQL injection through string-concatenated
  filters or unsafe `ORDER BY` clauses.
- **Information disclosure**: cache poisoning or cross-tenant cache key
  collisions returning another tenant's rows.
- **Denial of service**: unbounded queries (no `LIMIT`) that scan and load
  entire tables.

## Mitigations
- Parameterized queries everywhere; allowlist sortable columns rather than
  interpolating them.
- Tenant ID baked into every cache key and every query predicate.
- Mandatory pagination caps and statement timeouts on the connection.

Every operation that reaches this layer is mirrored into [[z=4|the audit
log]], and the data itself lives one layer deeper at [[z=5|the data store]].

@plane z=4 label="Audit and detection" x=1 y=3
# Depth 4 - Audit and Detection

A cross-cutting layer (offset on x to show it spans the stack) that
records what happened and surfaces anomalies. It does not block requests;
it makes attacks visible and recoverable.

## Assets
- Append-only audit log of authenticated, authorized mutations
- Security event stream feeding detection rules
- Alerting and on-call routing configuration

## Trust boundary
System behavior -> durable, tamper-evident record. The value of this layer
collapses if the log can be edited or silenced.

## STRIDE highlights
- **Repudiation**: an actor denies a deletion because the log lacks the
  correlation ID minted at [[z=1|the gateway]].
- **Tampering**: an attacker with database access edits or truncates the
  log to cover tracks.
- **Information disclosure**: logs capturing full request bodies leak
  tokens or personal data.

## Mitigations
- Write audit records to append-only, write-once storage shipped off-host
  in near real time.
- Redact secrets and personal fields before they ever reach the log.
- Detection rules on bulk reads, off-hours admin actions, and repeated
  authorization denials.

@plane z=5 label="Data store" x=0 y=4
# Depth 5 - Data Store

The deepest layer: the customer database and its backups. This is the
crown-jewel asset that every layer above exists to protect. Reaching it is
the attacker's goal and the last line of defense.

## Assets
- Customer PII and order history (the primary database)
- Encryption keys and the key management service
- Automated backups and their restore path

## Trust boundary
Internal service network -> persistent storage. By the time an action
arrives here it is assumed authorized, so defenses focus on containment and
limiting blast radius.

## STRIDE highlights
- **Information disclosure**: a stolen backup or snapshot exposing the full
  dataset at once.
- **Elevation of privilege**: a single overprivileged database account that
  can read every table and drop schemas.
- **Denial of service**: ransomware encrypting both the live store and its
  backups.

## Mitigations
- Encryption at rest with keys held in a separate KMS; rotate keys and
  scope grants per service.
- Least-privilege database roles: the order service cannot read the
  credentials table.
- Immutable, access-controlled backups with periodic restore drills.

An attacker who reaches this depth has traversed every layer above, from
[[z=0|the public edge]] inward. Defense in depth means no single layer is
trusted to be the only thing standing between them and these rows.

title: The Anatomy of a Tide Pool
src:
---
3md: 1.0
axis: depth
title: The Anatomy of a Tide Pool
author: Claude (blind-test author)
license: CC-BY-4.0
# the line below is a comment and is ignored by the parser
camera: "looking straight down, noon light"
---
A single tide pool, read as a stack of **depth planes** rather than a timeline.
Start at the glittering surface and descend, plane by plane, into the cold dark
where the rock meets the sea floor. Each plane is the same patch of water at a
different depth; the Z axis here is literal vertical distance, measured in
centimetres below the waterline.

Begin at [[z=0|the surface film]] and sink toward [[z=3]].

@plane z=0 label="Surface Film" depth_cm="0"
# 0 cm - The Surface Film

A skin of water one molecule could almost balance on. This is where air and
ocean negotiate.

- **Glare** scatters off every ripple; you see more sky than water.
- **Sea foam** collects in the lee of the rock, a lace of popped bubbles.
- A single **water strider** ghosts across, dimpling the meniscus.

> Everything below is refracted, bent, and a little bit lied about.

Look past the shine and descend to [[z=1|the sunlit shallows]].

@plane z=1 label="Sunlit Shallows"
# 1-10 cm - The Sunlit Shallows

Light still rules here. Photosynthesis hums.

1. **Green algae** fur the upper rock, soft as wet velvet.
2. **Snails** graze slow trails through the film of diatoms.
3. **Shrimp** flicker, near-transparent, betrayed only by their shadows.

This is the loud, bright, crowded floor of the pool - the part a child kneeling
on the rock actually sees. The real strangeness waits in the cold layers under
[[z=2]].

@plane z=2 label="The Cold Middle" x="0" y="-12"
# 10-25 cm - The Cold Middle

Colour starts to drain. Reds vanish first, so the **red anemones** here read as
muddy brown until you bring them up into the light.

| Inhabitant   | Strategy          |
|--------------|-------------------|
| Anemone      | Wait and sting    |
| Hermit crab  | Borrow and hide   |
| Brittle star | Curl and creep    |

The `x`/`y` attributes on this plane mark where, in the pool's footprint, the
cold pocket actually sits - twelve centimetres back from the front lip, dead
centre left-to-right.

Below this, the rock closes in. Continue to [[z=3|the dark floor]].

@plane z=3 label="The Dark Floor" mood="primordial"
# 25 cm and down - The Dark Floor

The bottom. Cold, still, and old.

- **Encrusting sponges** plate the rock in dull orange.
- A **chiton** clamps down, an armoured tongue of the sea.
- Detritus drifts and settles: the pool's slow memory of every tide.

Nothing here hurries. Nothing here needs the sun. When the tide returns it will
rinse this floor and reset the whole column - but for now, this is as deep as the
pool goes.

Rise back to [[z=0|the surface]] and start the descent again.

title: Traffic Light State Animation
src:
---
3md: 1.0
axis: frame
title: Traffic Light State Animation
fps: 1
loop: "true"
cycle_seconds: "12"
description: One full signal cycle for a four-way intersection, frame by frame.
---
A single playable cycle of a standard three-aspect traffic signal. Each plane is
one animation frame. Played in `z` order it loops forever: green holds, yellow
warns, red stops, and the red-plus-amber "get ready" beat (used in UK and German
signals) leads back into green.

The `x`/`y` offsets place each lamp where it sits on the physical housing: the
red lamp on top (`y=0`), amber in the middle (`y=1`), green at the bottom
(`y=2`). A spatial viewer can stack the lit lamp at the correct height.

Legend: a filled circle is lit, an empty circle is dark.

@plane z=0 x=0 y=2 label="Green - GO"
# Frame 0: GREEN

```
( )   red    off
( )   amber  off
(O)   green  ON
```

**State:** `GO`
**Hold:** 6 seconds (the long phase)
**Meaning:** Traffic may proceed if the way is clear.

Vehicles flow through the intersection. The crossing pedestrian signal shows a
steady "Don't Walk" because the conflicting approach is stopped.

Next beat: the green lamp drops out and amber lights up. Continue to
[[z=1|Frame 1: Yellow]].

@plane z=1 x=0 y=1 label="Yellow - CAUTION"
# Frame 1: YELLOW

```
( )   red    off
(O)   amber  ON
( )   green  off
```

**State:** `CAUTION`
**Hold:** 3 seconds
**Meaning:** The signal is about to turn red. Stop if it is safe to do so.

This is the shortest of the holding frames on purpose. The amber interval is
timed to the approach speed so a driver at the stop line can either clear the
intersection or come to a controlled stop.

Came from [[z=0|Frame 0: Green]]. Next up is the all-red moment in
[[z=2|Frame 2: Red]].

@plane z=2 x=0 y=0 label="Red - STOP"
# Frame 2: RED

```
(O)   red    ON
( )   amber  off
( )   green  off
```

**State:** `STOP`
**Hold:** 6 seconds (includes the all-red clearance interval)
**Meaning:** Do not proceed past the stop line.

While this approach is held at red, the cross street gets its own green. The
pedestrian "Walk" symbol lights for the parallel crosswalk. A short all-red
overlap at the start of this frame lets the intersection clear before any
conflicting movement begins.

Hold here, then advance to the priming beat in [[z=3|Frame 3: Red + Amber]].

@plane z=3 x=0 y=0 label="Red + Amber - GET READY"
# Frame 3: RED + AMBER

```
(O)   red    ON
(O)   amber  ON
( )   green  off
```

**State:** `PREPARE`
**Hold:** 2 seconds
**Meaning:** Stay stopped, but green is imminent. Release the brake, do not move.

This combined aspect is the British and German "get ready" phase. It gives
drivers a predictable cue to prepare without rolling forward early, which smooths
the launch when green returns.

This is the last frame of the cycle. Loop back to [[z=0|Frame 0: Green]] and
the animation repeats.

title: 16-Week Marathon Training Plan
src:
---
3md: 1.0
axis: time
title: 16-Week Marathon Training Plan
plan: Beginner to First Marathon
goal_time: sub-4:30
peak_weekly_mileage: 42
race: October 11 Sunday
---
A four-month build toward 26.2 miles, organized one plane per training block.
Each block stacks on the one before it: base first, then strength, then peak
volume, then a taper that sets up race day. Read the planes in Z order.

All paces assume an easy effort of roughly 10:30 per mile and a goal marathon
pace (GMP) near 10:18 per mile. Long runs are on Sundays. Take rest seriously,
it is where the adaptation actually happens.

@plane z=0 label="Base (Weeks 1-4)"
# Block 1: Base Building

The job here is simple: run consistently and get your body used to time on feet.
Keep every run conversational. If you cannot speak a full sentence, slow down.

| Week | Mon  | Tue 5K easy | Wed   | Thu 4mi | Fri  | Sat 3mi | Sun long | Total |
|------|------|-------------|-------|---------|------|---------|----------|-------|
| 1    | rest | 3.0         | cross | 4.0     | rest | 3.0     | 6.0      | 16    |
| 2    | rest | 3.0         | cross | 4.0     | rest | 3.0     | 7.0      | 17    |
| 3    | rest | 4.0         | cross | 4.0     | rest | 3.0     | 8.0      | 19    |
| 4    | rest | 4.0         | cross | 5.0     | rest | 3.0     | 6.0      | 18    |

Week 4 is a small step-back week (note the shorter long run) so the legs absorb
the first three weeks before you push on.

## Focus for this block

- Build the Sunday long run by no more than 1 mile per week.
- Cross-training (Wed) is a 40-minute easy bike or swim, zero pounding.
- Buy real shoes now and start breaking them in, not in week 15.

Next up: [[z=1|Block 2 adds tempo work]] once the aerobic base is in place.

@plane z=1 label="Strength (Weeks 5-8)"
# Block 2: Strength and Tempo

Now we introduce quality. One weekly tempo run teaches your legs to clear
lactate at a faster, sustained effort. Tempo pace is "comfortably hard", roughly
9:45 per mile, about 35 seconds quicker than easy pace.

| Week | Tue easy | Wed tempo        | Thu easy | Sun long | Total |
|------|----------|------------------|----------|----------|-------|
| 5    | 4.0      | 2mi @ 9:45       | 4.0      | 10.0     | 24    |
| 6    | 4.0      | 3mi @ 9:45       | 5.0      | 12.0     | 28    |
| 7    | 5.0      | 4mi @ 9:45       | 5.0      | 13.0     | 31    |
| 8    | 4.0      | 2mi @ 9:45       | 4.0      | 10.0     | 26    |

Week 8 cuts back again. Resist the urge to "make up" the volume, the cutback is
load-bearing.

## Workout detail: the tempo run

1. 1 mile easy warm-up.
2. The tempo block at 9:45 (hold it steady, do not start too hot).
3. 1 mile easy cool-down.

Add two short strength sessions a week: squats, lunges, single-leg calf raises,
and planks. Strong hips and core are what keep your form together at mile 22.

Back to [[z=0|the base block]] if a run still feels hard, or push on to
[[z=2|peak mileage]] when the tempos feel repeatable.

@plane z=2 label="Peak (Weeks 9-13)"
# Block 3: Peak Mileage

The most demanding block. This is where you bank the long runs that make 26.2
possible and rehearse goal marathon pace (GMP, near 10:18) on tired legs.

| Week | Wed quality       | Sun long             | Total |
|------|-------------------|----------------------|-------|
| 9    | 4mi @ 9:45 tempo  | 15.0                 | 33    |
| 10   | 5mi @ 9:45 tempo  | 17.0                 | 36    |
| 11   | 6x800m @ 5K pace  | 18.0 (last 4 @ GMP)  | 38    |
| 12   | 5mi @ 9:45 tempo  | 20.0 (last 6 @ GMP)  | 42    |
| 13   | 4mi @ 9:45 tempo  | 14.0                 | 34    |

The week-12 long run of 20 miles is the keystone of the whole plan. The final
6 miles at goal pace teach you what mile 20 of the race will actually feel like.

## Fueling the long run

- Practice race-day breakfast 3 hours before every long run.
- Take a gel every 40-45 minutes, starting at minute 40 (do not wait until you
  feel empty).
- Drink to thirst, roughly 4-6 oz every 20 minutes in the heat.
- Whatever you eat and drink here is exactly what you use on [[z=3|race day]].

Week 13 begins the wind-down. Do not panic, [[z=3|the taper]] is when fitness
becomes performance.

@plane z=3 label="Taper and Race (Weeks 14-16)"
# Block 4: Taper and Race Day

Mileage drops sharply while intensity stays sharp. You will feel restless and
slightly slow, this is normal and means the taper is working. The hay is in the
barn.

| Week | Wed              | Sun long | Total | Note                  |
|------|------------------|----------|-------|-----------------------|
| 14   | 4mi @ GMP        | 12.0     | 28    | first taper week      |
| 15   | 3mi @ GMP        | 8.0      | 20    | sleep is the workout  |
| 16   | 2mi shakeout     | 26.2     | race  | trust the training    |

## Race week checklist

- [ ] Lay out clothes, bib, and gels the night before (nothing new on race day).
- [ ] Eat a familiar dinner, hydrate, and stop drinking caffeine early.
- [ ] Set two alarms. Arrive at the start with time to spare.

## Race-day pacing

1. **Miles 1-6:** start 10-15 seconds SLOWER than GMP. Bank patience, not time.
2. **Miles 7-20:** settle into goal pace (about 10:18) and run the tangents.
3. **Miles 21-26.2:** this is the race. Hold form, shorten your stride, finish.

You arrived here from [[z=2|the 20-mile peak run]]. Everything you rehearsed
there, the fuel, the pace, the patience, is the plan for today. Go get it.

title: The Old Pond, Three Ways
src:
---
3md: 1.0
axis: layer
title: The Old Pond, Three Ways
poet: Matsuo Basho
written: c. 1686
form: haiku (5-7-5)
note: One source plane plus two English renderings, stacked as toggleable layers.
---
A single haiku by Matsuo Basho, kept in three layers: the Japanese source on the
base plane, a literal gloss above it, and a free verse rendering on top. Toggle the
layers to compare how each translator handled the same five-seven-five.

@plane z=0 label="Original (Japanese)" x=0 y=0
# Layer 0: Source

## furuike ya

```
古池や          furuike ya
蛙飛びこむ      kawazu tobikomu
水の音          mizu no oto
```

Romaji, line by line:

1. furu-ike ya (old pond, plus the cutting word *ya*)
2. kawazu tobikomu (a frog leaps in)
3. mizu no oto (the sound of water)

The pivot is *ya*, a *kireji* (cutting word) that holds the old pond in silence
before the frog disturbs it. There is no verb tense and no subject pronoun, so the
scene stays open. See the word-by-word gloss in [[z=1|the literal layer]], or skip
straight to [[z=2|the free rendering]].

@plane z=1 label="Literal gloss (English)" x=2 y=0
# Layer 1: Literal Gloss

A close, almost mechanical reading that preserves word order and the 5-7-5 count
where English allows it.

> Old pond, ah,
> a frog jumps in,
> the sound of water.

Notes on the choices:

- *furuike* becomes "old pond" (two words for one compound).
- *ya* is rendered as "ah," a small breath standing in for the cut.
- *tobikomu* is "jumps in," present tense, no named actor.
- *mizu no oto* is left as plain "the sound of water," resisting the urge to add
  "splash."

This layer sits directly over [[z=0|the source]]. For a version that breaks the
literal grid in favor of music, lift up to [[z=2|the free rendering]].

@plane z=2 label="Free rendering (English)" x=4 y=0
# Layer 2: Free Rendering

A looser translation that trades the strict syllable count for rhythm and a single
sharp image. (After Robert Hass, adapted.)

> The old pond,
> a frog jumps in:
> the splash of silence.

What this layer gives up, and what it keeps:

- It drops the 5-7-5 count entirely, choosing three short breaths instead.
- It adds the paradox "splash of silence," which is not in [[z=1|the gloss]] but
  honors the way the *ya* in [[z=0|the source]] frames quiet against sound.
- The colon does the work of the cutting word, pausing the eye before the splash.

Read top to bottom (z=2, z=1, z=0) and the poem grows more spare; read bottom to
top and it grows more interpreted. Same eleven syllables underneath all three.

title: Blinking Typing Cursor
src:
---
3md: 1.0
axis: frame
title: Blinking Typing Cursor
fps: 2
loop: true
glyph: U+2588 (full block)
duration_ms: 1000
---
A four-frame loop for a text caret that blinks on and off at the end of a
prompt. Played back at 2 frames per second, the cursor is solid for half a
second, then hidden for half a second, giving the classic 500 ms blink that
terminals and text editors use.

Read the frames in order: [[z=0|on]], [[z=1|dim]], [[z=2|off]], then
[[z=3|dim again]], and the player wraps back to [[z=0|on]] to repeat.

@plane z=0 x=0 y=0 label="On (solid)"
# Frame 0 - cursor fully on

The caret is drawn at full opacity right after the prompt text.

```
> hello world█
```

- Opacity: 100%
- Glyph: full block (`█`)
- Hold: 250 ms

This is the resting state when you stop typing. Next comes the
[[z=1|fade out]].

@plane z=1 x=0 y=0 label="Dim (fading out)"
# Frame 1 - cursor fading out

Halfway through the off-stroke the caret is rendered at reduced opacity so the
transition does not snap. On hardware that cannot fade, this frame is simply
skipped and the cursor jumps straight from [[z=0|on]] to [[z=2|off]].

```
> hello world▓
```

- Opacity: 40%
- Glyph: medium shade (`▓`)
- Hold: 250 ms

@plane z=2 x=0 y=0 label="Off (hidden)"
# Frame 2 - cursor fully off

The caret is not drawn at all. Only the prompt and the typed text remain, so
the underlying character (or trailing space) shows through cleanly.

```
> hello world
```

- Opacity: 0%
- Glyph: none
- Hold: 250 ms

This is the darkest point of the blink. The loop now climbs back toward
visibility via [[z=3|the fade in]].

@plane z=3 x=0 y=0 label="Dim (fading in)"
# Frame 3 - cursor fading in

The mirror of [[z=1|frame 1]]. The caret returns at partial opacity before
snapping back to solid, which softens the on-stroke the same way frame 1
softened the off-stroke.

```
> hello world▓
```

- Opacity: 40%
- Glyph: medium shade (`▓`)
- Hold: 250 ms

After this frame the player wraps to [[z=0|frame 0]] and the one-second cycle
starts over.

title: ASCII Walk Cycle
src:
---
3md: 1.0
axis: frame
title: ASCII Walk Cycle
fps: 8
loop: true
direction: rightward
artist: CorvidLabs
---
A four-frame walking loop drawn in monospace ASCII. Play the frames in order
(0, 1, 2, 3) and then jump back to [[z=0|frame 0]] to repeat. The figure keeps
its head and torso fixed while the legs and arms swing through a contact, a
recoil, a passing pose, and a high point, the four poses every classic walk
cycle is built from.

Read each plane as a single drawn cel. Render at 8 fps for a natural stroll;
drop to 4 fps for a slow, heavy trudge.

@plane z=0 x=0 y=0 label="Contact"
# Frame 0 - Contact

The lead foot has just landed and the trailing foot is still pushing off. This
is the widest stride of the cycle and the pose viewers read as "a step."

```
  O
 /|\
 / \
/   \
```

- Lead leg: forward and straight, heel striking down
- Trail leg: extended back, toe still grounded
- Arms: opposed to legs (the wide `/|\` swing)

Next pose absorbs the impact: see [[z=1|frame 1, Recoil]].

@plane z=1 x=2 y=1 label="Recoil"
# Frame 1 - Recoil

The body sinks to its lowest point as the front leg takes the full weight. Knees
bend, the torso dips by one row, and momentum is briefly soaked up before the
push forward.

```
  O
 /|\
  |
 / \
```

- Lead leg: bent, bearing weight directly under the hips
- Trail leg: lifting, beginning to swing through
- Body height: lowest in the cycle (the dip sells the weight)

Coming up out of the dip leads to [[z=2|frame 2, Passing]].

@plane z=2 x=4 y=0 label="Passing"
# Frame 2 - Passing

The trailing leg passes underneath the body and the two legs cross. The torso is
balanced over a single support leg, rising back toward neutral height. Arms are
nearly straight down at the midpoint of their swing.

```
  O
 /|\
  |
  |
```

- Support leg: vertical, directly under the head
- Free leg: passing forward, knee high
- Arms: hanging near center, about to reverse direction

This is the balance point between [[z=1|the recoil]] and the next high step,
[[z=3|frame 3, High Point]].

@plane z=3 x=6 y=-1 label="High Point"
# Frame 3 - High Point

The body lifts to its highest point as the passing leg reaches forward and the
support leg straightens onto its toe. One more frame and the reaching foot will
land, returning the figure to the contact pose.

```
  O
 /|\
 / \
 \
```

- Reaching leg: swinging forward, heel about to drop
- Support leg: pushing up onto the toe (tallest pose)
- Arms: opposed swing widening again, mirroring frame 0

The reaching foot lands and the loop restarts at [[z=0|frame 0, Contact]].

title: A Toast to Mara and Devin
src:
---
3md: 1.0
axis: slide
title: A Toast to Mara and Devin
speaker: Best Man (Theo)
occasion: Wedding Reception, The Old Mill
duration: about 3 minutes
---
A five-beat toast. Each plane is one slide, advanced as the words land. Hold a
beat of silence between slides; let the room catch up before you move on.

@plane z=0 x=0 y=0 label="The hook"
# Beat 1: Stand up, get the room

*(Raise glass. Wait for the clink to settle. Smile.)*

Good evening, everyone. For those who do not know me, I am Theo, and I have had
the questionable honor of being Devin's best friend since we were eleven, when he
traded me his entire lunch for a single, slightly melted chocolate bar.

I tell you that because I want you to understand the kind of negotiator Mara
married.

*(Pause for laugh.)*

Next: [[z=1|how these two actually met]].

@plane z=1 x=1 y=0 label="The story"
# Beat 2: The how-they-met

Mara and Devin met in line for terrible coffee at a conference neither of them
wanted to be at. Devin says he opened with a brilliant line. Mara says he spilled
half his cup on her notes and apologized for ninety straight seconds.

Both versions are true, which is the first thing you should know about this
marriage: there will always be two accounts, and somehow both of them are real.

What I noticed, the first time I met Mara, was simple. Devin laughs at his own
jokes. Around her, he started waiting to see if she laughed first.

Back to [[z=0|the beginning]], or on to [[z=2|the turn]].

@plane z=2 x=2 y=0 label="The turn"
# Beat 3: The turn (get a little real)

*(Slow down here. Quieter.)*

There was a hard winter a couple of years ago. Devin's dad was sick, and Devin
was not himself, and I watched Mara do the unglamorous work of love: showing up,
making soup nobody asked for, sitting in waiting rooms with a deck of cards.

She did not fix anything. You cannot fix some things. She just refused to let him
go through it alone.

That is when I stopped thinking of them as two people who were dating and started
thinking of them as a team.

@plane z=3 x=3 y=0 label="The line about them"
# Beat 4: The thing that is just *them*

Here is what I want you both to remember, on the nights that are easy and the
nights that are not.

- Mara, you make Devin braver than he believes he is.
- Devin, you make Mara laugh in the exact places she forgets to.
- Together, you are kinder than either of you is alone.

Marriage is not one big decision. It is ten thousand small ones, made at the
kitchen counter, in the dark, half-asleep. Keep choosing each other in the small
ones, and the big ones take care of themselves.

The last slide is [[z=4|the toast itself]].

@plane z=4 x=4 y=0 label="The toast"
# Beat 5: Glasses up

*(Raise glass. Look at the couple, then the room.)*

So please, everyone, on your feet.

To Mara and Devin. May your coffee always be better than the cup that started
this, may your two versions of every story always agree on the ending, and may
you keep choosing each other for all the small, ordinary, extraordinary days
ahead.

To the happy couple.

*(Drink. Sit down before the applause finishes.)*

Return to [[z=0|the top]] if the room asks for an encore.

title: 20-Minute HIIT Circuit
src:
---
3md: 1.0
axis: step
title: 20-Minute HIIT Circuit
duration: 20 min
rounds: 3
equipment: none (bodyweight only)
---
A no-equipment HIIT circuit you move through one step at a time. Each work
interval is 40 seconds on, 20 seconds rest, then you cycle the whole circuit
three times. Start at the warm-up and follow the steps in order.

@plane z=0 x=0 y=0 label="Warm-up"
# Step 0: Warm-up (3 minutes)

Wake the joints and raise the heart rate before the hard work begins. Do not
skip this, cold muscles are how ankles roll.

| Drill | Time |
| --- | --- |
| March in place, arms swinging | 45 sec |
| Bodyweight squats, slow tempo | 45 sec |
| Arm circles forward then back | 30 sec |
| Leg swings, 10 each side | 30 sec |
| Jumping jacks, easy pace | 30 sec |

Aim for a 6 out of 10 effort here. You should be breathing harder but still
able to talk. When you feel loose, advance to [[z=1|the first work block]].

@plane z=1 x=1 y=0 label="Block A: Lower Body"
# Step 1: Block A, Lower Body

Two movements, 40 seconds work and 20 seconds rest each. Push the pace but keep
the form clean.

1. **Jump squats** - drop to a parallel squat, explode up, land soft through
   the midfoot. Scale down to regular squats if the knees complain.
2. **Reverse lunges** - alternate legs, keep the front knee stacked over the
   ankle, torso tall.

> Coaching cue: drive through the heels on every squat and lunge. If your knees
> cave inward, slow down and reset.

Recover for 30 seconds, then move to [[z=2|Block B]].

@plane z=2 x=2 y=0 label="Block B: Upper Body"
# Step 2: Block B, Upper Body

Push and pull patterns to balance the legs you just torched.

1. **Push-ups** - hands under the shoulders, body in one line, chest to a fist
   off the floor. Drop to the knees when reps get sloppy.
2. **Plank shoulder taps** - hold a high plank, tap each hand to the opposite
   shoulder, keep the hips still and square.

Same 40 on, 20 off rhythm. If your hips are sagging on the plank, that is the
sign to take the optional knee variation.

When both moves are done, push into [[z=3|the core finisher]].

@plane z=3 x=3 y=0 label="Block C: Core"
# Step 3: Block C, Core

The grindy part. Brace your abs as if bracing for a punch on every rep.

1. **Mountain climbers** - drive the knees fast, hips low and level, do not let
   the shoulders creep forward past the wrists.
2. **Bicycle crunches** - slow and controlled, opposite elbow toward opposite
   knee, fully extend the straight leg.

After this block you have finished one full round. Catch your breath for 45
seconds.

- Rounds remaining 2 or more: loop back to [[z=1|Block A]] and run it again.
- Final round complete: continue to [[z=4|the cooldown]].

@plane z=4 x=4 y=0 label="Cooldown"
# Step 4: Cooldown (3 minutes)

Bring the heart rate down gradually and stretch the muscles you worked.

| Stretch | Hold |
| --- | --- |
| Standing quad stretch, each side | 30 sec |
| Forward fold for hamstrings | 30 sec |
| Chest and shoulder opener in a doorway | 30 sec |
| Child's pose for the lower back | 45 sec |
| Slow nasal breathing, eyes closed | 45 sec |

Hydrate, log how many rounds you actually finished, and note which block was the
limiter. Next session, try to add one round or shave the rest down to 15
seconds. Jump back to [[z=0|the warm-up]] whenever you are ready to go again.
